- **Workspace tab auto-labels.** Tabs currently show 'workspace N'. Should reflect pane contents — agent type, cwd basename, or (see below) Claude Code session title.
- **Pane tag rendering.** The terminal.panes.tag MCP tool already lets external tools bind metadata to panes. Render tags (e.g. issue IDs, branch names) as small badges in pane headers.

### Category 2: New surfaces for existing data

- **Subagent tree overlay.** ClaudeJsonlRegistry already tracks parent/child session topology. An overlay triggered by a keybinding showing the tree with live per-node status. Currently invisible.
- **Command history overlay.** OSC 633 tracks every command with exit code and duration per pane. An overlay showing recent commands, click-to-jump-to-scrollback.
- **Output cadence sparkline in pane headers.** cadence.rs computes arrival-rate metrics. Tiny sparkline showing streaming vs thinking vs stalled.
- **Semantic region dimming.** Region index tags Prompt/Command/Output/Error blocks. Dim old output slightly, highlight error regions. Risky — needs careful design to avoid being obnoxious.

### Category 3: High-value specific opportunity — Claude Code session titles

**Claude Code's UserPromptSubmit hook now supports hookSpecificOutput.sessionTitle**, which is user-provided and propagates through the session JSONL file that ClaudeJsonlTailer already tails. This is a structurally-unrecoverable signal (inference cannot derive a good human-readable title from the byte stream) that unlocks several surface-level wins at once:

- Workspace tab labels become 'fix login bug' instead of 'workspace 1'
- Pane header strings for agent panes become 'claude · fix login bug · ToolUse · Bash'
- A future session-switcher/list UI becomes navigable instead of a wall of hex IDs
- Subagent tree nodes get per-task titles

Implementation sketch:
- Add SessionTitleChanged variant to AgentEvent
- Tailer emits it when the title field in the JSONL changes
- Subscribers cache latest title per session
- Ship docs/integrations/claude-code-session-titles.md with a minimal recommended hook config (documented-not-built-in per CLAUDE.md 'Harness integrations' section)
- Do NOT ship a hook script; link to the upstream Claude Code hook docs

This is the first case where therminal should explicitly document an opinionated hook config for an upstream feature, because: (a) the signal is unrecoverable from inference, (b) the hook is cheap and fires off the agent's critical path, (c) it enables multiple surface wins that were otherwise stuck on 'what do we even label this with.'

## Not in scope for this epic

- JSONL conversation pane backend (tracked separately — needs its own design)
- Codex tailer port (speculative until there's a consumer demanding it)
- Settings panel itself (separate epic)
- Keybinding editor (separate epic if ever)

## Priority ordering (rough)

Work-to-impact ratio favors items that reuse existing chrome:

1. Pane header agent + tool badges
2. Pane-level exit code coloring
3. Hotspot type colors
4. Claude session title support (data + renderer plumbing)
5. Context percent gauge
6. Workspace tab auto-labels (uses session titles from step 4)
7. Pane tag rendering
8. Command history overlay
9. Subagent tree overlay
10. Output cadence sparkline
11. Semantic region dimming

## Interaction with flag audit

Each item should be evaluated: bake-in by default (preferred), panel entry if genuinely preferences-worthy, or delete if the feature doesn't justify either. Existing show_* flags get revisited when the settings panel lands.

## Status

Filed as a brainstorming starting point. Scope will be refined before any child issues are created. No implementation until the epic is decomposed and individual items are prioritized concretely.
notes:
## Decomposition complete (2026-04-10)

Filed 5 child issues (all P2–P3, all parent-child of this epic):

| ID | Title | Priority | Key files |
|---|---|---|---|
| tn-obxr | Pane header exit-code color indicator | P2 | `chrome/pane_header.rs`, `pane/state.rs` |
| tn-qhfy | Hotspot type color differentiation | P2 | `grid_renderer.rs`, `color_mapping.rs` |
| tn-nhbv | Context percentage gauge in pane headers | P3 | `pane_header.rs`, `pane/state.rs`, interceptor |
| tn-lxq9 | Workspace tab auto-labels from Claude session titles | P3 | `window/mod.rs`, `tab_bar.rs` |
| tn-166y | Pane tag badges in pane headers | P3 | `pane_header.rs`, protocol `PaneStateSnapshot` |

### Pre-existing children
- tn-5fgz: Claude agent state badges + subagent count (P3) — already had a prepared prompt

### Dep wiring
- tn-lxq9 (tab auto-labels) blocks on tn-5fgz (needs SessionTitleChanged event + ClaudeRenderState cache)
- tn-nhbv (context gauge) is related to tn-5fgz (both write AgentReport data into pane state — coordinate to avoid double-write)

### Still undecomposed (lower priority — defer)
- Output cadence sparkline (Category 2)
- Command history overlay (Category 2)
- Subagent tree overlay (Category 2, partially covered by tn-5fgz)
- Semantic region dimming (Category 2)
- Pane tag rendering iteration (tn-166y covers v1; more polish deferred)

Epic closes when all 6 children land.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Claude agent regex false-positives on any text containing the word 'claude'
description:
state_inference's Claude detection regex is too loose: crates/therminal-terminal/src/state_inference/patterns.rs:40

  agent_ident_claude: Regex::new(r"(?i)claude\s*(?:code|3|4)?").unwrap()

This matches any case-insensitive occurrence of 'claude' anywhere in pane output — including CLAUDE.md filenames in a directory listing, README text, git log messages, etc. Any pane that displays those strings flips detected_agent_type to Claude, then starts writing /tmp/claude-code-state/daemon-pane-{id}.json on every state transition.

Repro: run tfe in a pane and navigate to any directory in this project (which has CLAUDE.md everywhere). The state file appears even though no agent is running.

Copilot got this exact anti-false-positive fix in tn-3pkv with an anchored regex (patterns.rs:46):

  r"(?i)\b(?:github\s+copilot|copilot\s+(?:cli|chat|using|v\d|version))\b"

Fix: tighten agent_ident_claude similarly. Require a banner/CLI context, e.g.:

  \b(?:claude\s+code|claude-code|claude\s+cli|claude\s+v\d)\b

Add tests covering: (a) CLAUDE.md in a directory listing does NOT match, (b) 'Claude Code v1.2.3' DOES match, (c) 'running claude...' in a log line does NOT match.
close_reason: completed

title: state_inference writes Streaming/Thinking status but ClaudeStatus parser rejects them
description:
The writer and reader of /tmp/claude-code-state/daemon-pane-{id}.json disagree on the status enum.

Writer: crates/therminal-terminal/src/state_inference/types.rs:114-122 — InferredStatus has variants Idle/Processing/Streaming/Thinking/ToolUse/AwaitingInput, serialized as snake_case strings.

Reader: crates/therminal-daemon/src/claude_state.rs:53-61 — ClaudeStatus only knows Idle/Processing/ToolUse/AwaitingInput. Streaming and Thinking are missing.

Symptom: daemon log spam every time inference writes a 'streaming' or 'thinking' state file:

  WARN therminal_daemon::claude_state: failed to parse state file JSON
  path=C:/tmp/claude-code-state\daemon-pane-1.json
  error=unknown variant `streaming`, expected one of `idle`, `processing`, `tool_use`, `awaiting_input`

Fix: add Streaming and Thinking variants to ClaudeStatus in claude_state.rs. Writer is source of truth. Add regression test that deserializes both new variants.
close_reason: completed

title: viewport_row_to_absolute_line: document or assert display_offset bounds
description:
Discovered during the tn-ioda full gate code review (scanner-3, confidence 80%).

`crates/therminal-app/src/window/mouse.rs:384` — `viewport_row_to_absolute_line(row: usize, display_offset: usize) -> i32` casts both arguments to `i32` without bounds checking. With `display_offset >= 2^31` the cast wraps. In practice `display_offset` is bounded by `scrollback_lines` (default 10,000) so this cannot overflow today, but the function signature makes no such assertion and the existing tests cover only 0..1000.

Defensive hardening: either use `i32::try_from(display_offset).unwrap_or(i32::MAX)`, or add a doc comment + debug_assert documenting that callers must ensure `display_offset < i32::MAX`.

Not blocking — not reachable in practice. File for hygiene.

Discovered-from tn-ioda.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
`viewport_row_to_absolute_line(row: usize, display_offset: usize) -> i32` casts both arguments to `i32` without bounds checking. With `display_offset >= 2^31` the cast wraps. Not reachable in practice (`display_offset` is bounded by `scrollback_lines`, default 10k) but the function signature makes no such assertion.

## Task
Defensive hardening — pick one:

1. Use `i32::try_from(display_offset).unwrap_or(i32::MAX)` (and same for `row`)
2. Add a doc comment + `debug_assert!(display_offset < i32::MAX as usize)` documenting that callers must ensure the bound

Lean: option 2 (doc + debug_assert) since this is hygiene, not a real bug.

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window/mouse.rs:384` (`viewport_row_to_absolute_line`)

## Acceptance
- Function bounds documented or enforced
- `./scripts/ci.sh` clean

## When Done
`bd close tn-7y4g --reason "viewport_row_to_absolute_line bounds documented"`
close_reason: viewport_row_to_absolute_line bounds documented

title: Refactor: split pane_ops.rs (2722 LOC) into focused submodules
description:
Discovered during the tn-ioda full gate code review (scanner-4, confidence 85%).

`crates/therminal-app/src/window/pane_ops.rs` has grown to 2722 lines, far above the project's 500-line guideline (root CLAUDE.md). The file mixes:

- Local split/close/focus core (the original scope)
- Daemon RPC helpers (spawn_remote_pane_off_existing, spawn_remote_pane_fresh_session, rebuild_from_snapshot_remote)
- Workspace routing (switch_workspace, send_to_workspace, restore_layout)
- cwd inheritance helpers
- Clipboard operations

Suggested split:
- `remote_pane.rs` — daemon RPC helpers
- `workspace_ops.rs` — switch/send/restore workspace operations
- `pane_ops.rs` — keeps the local split/close/focus core

This is a refactor task, not a bug. No functional change should result.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: The split was clean -- all 3,148 lines mapped to exactly 6 submodules with clear responsibility boundaries. Rust's split-impl-across-modules pattern made it trivial to keep all methods on `App` without changing callers.
- What was unclear: The `shell_quote` function is shared between `editor_clipboard.rs` (WSL editor) and `workspace_ops.rs` (swarm tail). Made it `pub(super)` in editor_clipboard so workspace_ops can use `super::editor_clipboard::shell_quote`. This is a reasonable cross-module reference but worth noting for future moves.
close_reason: Split pane_ops.rs (3,148 lines) into pane_ops/{mod,split_ops,close_ops,focus_and_nav,workspace_ops,editor_clipboard}.rs. Pure mechanical move, no logic changes. All CI checks pass (fmt, clippy, build, 1494 tests).

title: SPEC: Marker + Event Bus architecture — lock the four surfaces before implementation
description:
Design-first issue that locks the four API surfaces the rest of the Phase 6 marker/event work depends on. Write this BEFORE implementing tn-x3pd or tn-b53r — everything else becomes cheap/additive once these four are nailed down; everything else becomes painful if we have to change them later.

## Why this issue exists
Over a planning session with the user we accumulated a pile of related feature ideas that all reduce to the same primitives: unified event bus, marker grammar, widget markers, reply events, hotspot hooks. Consumers include cache-churn gauges, agent timelines, glossary explainers, async multi-select via /btw, multi-agent orchestration, TTS triggers, and more. None of these are 'plugins' in the classic sense — they're producers/subscribers on a bus. To avoid building an over-engineered plugin system for plugins we decided not to ship, this issue locks the actual invariants.

## The four lockable surfaces

### 1. Marker grammar (wire format)
The text convention for structured events that can travel over any text-carrying channel (PTY via OSC, JSONL content fields, log lines, stdout). Pick once, document once, never break.

Open questions to resolve:
- OSC final code for PTY channel (1341? something else unused?)
- Key=value pairs vs embedded JSON body — probably key=value for shell-friendliness, escape rules for commas/semicolons/newlines
- A non-OSC bracket form for channels where OSC isn't available (JSONL, logs) — e.g. `[[marker kind=... id=...]]`
- Reserved keys: `type`, `kind`, `id`, `pane`, `source`, `ts`
- Size limit: how big can a single marker get? Probably 4KB with a recommendation for small bodies

Deliverable: docs/marker-grammar.md with ABNF or equivalent, escape rules, both encodings, size limits, versioning story.

### 2. Event record shape (internal representation)
The struct every feeder normalizes to and every subscriber reads. Must be stable across source types.

    {
      source: 'claude' | 'codex' | 'marker' | ...,   // which feeder produced it
      pane_id: Option<u64>,                           // None for session-global events
      kind: String,                                   // namespaced, e.g. 'claude.agent.done'
      ts: RFC3339,                                    // monotonic-preferred
      cursor: u64,                                    // global monotonic for pagination
      body: serde_json::Value,                        // source-specific payload
    }

Open questions:
- Cursor: single global monotonic, or (source, counter) tuple?
- Should pane_id be None-able for events that aren't tied to a specific pane?
- Correlation ids for request/response pairs (widget → reply)?

Deliverable: Rust type in therminal-protocol + documented schema in docs/event-record.md.

### 3. Canonical kind taxonomy (namespace)
The vocabulary of event kinds. Namespaced by source so new feeders don't collide. Defines what subscribers can filter on.

Initial taxonomy:
- `<source>.agent.done`, `<source>.agent.waiting`, `<source>.agent.error`
- `<source>.progress` (with pct + phase in body)
- `marker.widget.<kind>` (widget mount/update/remove)
- `marker.user.reply` (widget submission, correlates to a widget id)
- `marker.hotspot.<action>` (hotspot click emitting a request)
- `pty.command.begin`, `pty.command.end` (from OSC 133/633, already parsed)

Open questions:
- Convention for extension kinds by third parties (reserved prefix? 'x.' like headers?)
- How strict is the namespacing — is `foo` rejected, or treated as `unknown.foo`?
- Versioning: do kinds carry a version suffix, or do we version the whole taxonomy?

Deliverable: docs/event-kinds.md with the initial table + extension policy.

### 4. Filter / cursor grammar for terminal://events
The MCP resource URI shape. Subscribers written today must keep working forever.

    terminal://events?sources=claude,codex,markers&kinds=*.done,*.error&panes=1,3&since=<cursor>

Open questions:
- Wildcard syntax in kinds filter (`*`, `?`, globs, regex?)
- Long-poll vs pure cursor-pagination for live tail
- Max records per response (default, cap)
- Historical replay — is there a since=0 that gives you the full buffer? Per-source? Configurable retention?
- Backpressure — what happens when a slow subscriber lags behind?

Deliverable: docs/event-bus-api.md with URI grammar, response schema, subscription semantics.

## Scope (what this issue produces)
1. docs/marker-grammar.md
2. docs/event-record.md (+ Rust types in therminal-protocol, but NOT wired to anything yet)
3. docs/event-kinds.md
4. docs/event-bus-api.md
5. An ADR (architecture decision record) at docs/adr/0001-event-bus.md summarizing why this shape, what was rejected, and what's deliberately out of scope

## Explicitly out of scope
- Any implementation (that's tn-x3pd, tn-b53r, and follow-ups)
- Any widget rendering code
- Any plugin ABI (there isn't one — that's the point)
- Any harness-specific code

## Consumers that become cheap once this spec lands
- tn-x3pd — widget marker parsing + rendering (reshape as implementation of the spec)
- tn-b53r — unified event bus MCP resource (reshape as implementation of the spec)
- tn-sxfa — cache-churn statusline widget (~10 line script)
- tn-h7mx — glossary hotspots
- tn-x85k — agent timeline widget
- Async multi-select via /btw (not yet filed — could be tn-x3pd widget set)

## Dependencies
None. This is a design deliverable, not code.

## When Done
All five docs land in docs/ on main, reviewed by the user, and tn-x3pd + tn-b53r are re-prompted to implement against the spec exactly. Close with `bd close SPEC-ID --reason 'Spec locked, consumers can now build against stable surfaces'`.
close_reason: Superseded by sharper SPEC fan-out: tn-fp42 (OSC handler registry), tn-3vzi (semantic pattern matching engine), tn-bu1s (event bus). The original 'one global marker grammar' scope is rejected — each harness crate owns its own private OSC marker format via the registry hook. See tn-53i1 for the integration taxonomy doctrine that replaces this issue's framing.

title: Verify tn-q882 fixes on Windows: rebuild + Claude Code reload + smoke test all 3 issues
description: Manual production verification of the three tn-q882 issues after the half-close fix (tn-587k) and the schemars EmptyParams fix (already landed in mcp/mod.rs + tools.rs) reach Windows. Cannot be automated — requires running ./scripts/build-windows.sh and reloading the Claude Code MCP connection on the WSL2 side, then attempting the operations that previously failed. This is the gate that decides whether the bridge fix is sufficient or whether we need to escalate to the long-lived bridge mode (tn-q882c). Owned by user (driving the Windows side); agent assists with diagnosis if anything fails.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Manual production verification of all three tn-q882 issues after the half-close fix (tn-587k) and the schemars EmptyParams fix (already landed earlier in mcp/mod.rs + tools.rs) reach Windows. **This is user-driven** — the agent cannot run Claude Code on Windows. The agent's job is to (a) coordinate the rebuild, (b) provide the user with a clear smoke-test checklist, (c) help diagnose any failures, and (d) decide whether the fix is sufficient or whether to escalate to tn-rahh (persistent bridge mode).

## Task

1. **Confirm prerequisites**: tn-587k closed, tn-zsn6 (CI gate) closed clean, working tree clean, ready to push. Push to origin if not already pushed.

2. **Trigger Windows rebuild**: \`./scripts/build-windows.sh\`. This wraps the PowerShell build and copies therminal.exe + therminal-daemon.exe + resources to %APPDATA%\\therminal and Desktop. Confirm exit code 0 and that the binaries are timestamped after the most recent commit.

3. **Hand off to user with this exact checklist** (the agent prints these instructions; the user runs them):

   \`\`\`
   On Windows, with the freshly rebuilt therminal.exe + therminal-daemon.exe in place:

   (1) Restart the daemon: kill any running therminal-daemon.exe, start the new one
       (or just launch therminal.exe — it auto-spawns the daemon per tn-txs8).
   (2) From WSL2 Claude Code: reload the MCP connection
       (e.g. /mcp restart therminal, or restart the conversation).
   (3) Run \`/mcp\` and confirm therminal shows ✓ Connected.
   (4) **Issue 1 verification (tool schemas)**: ask Claude Code to list mcp__therminal__* tools.
       Should now register all 23 tools (was previously 0).
   (5) **Issue 2 verification (resource read)**: have Claude Code call
       ReadMcpResourceTool with terminal://pane/1/content. Should return the pane's
       grid snapshot, NOT \"MCP error -32000: Connection closed\".
   (6) **Issue 3 verification (disconnects)**: run a sequence of ~10 tool calls in a row
       (mix of tools and resources) and verify therminal stays connected throughout.
       Watch for any \"therminal has disconnected\" system reminders.
   (7) **Daily-driver smoke**: open a new pane via terminal.panes.create, write a command
       via terminal.panes.write, read it back via terminal.panes.get_content. Verify all
       three round-trip without disconnect.
   \`\`\`

4. **Decision tree based on user's report**:
   - **All 7 checks pass** → close tn-e8zg with summary, the dogfooding goal is met. Close the parent tn-q882 with a retro pointing at tn-587k as the root-cause fix. Recommend the user file dogfooding bugs as new beads issues going forward. **Do NOT execute tn-rahh.**
   - **Issues 1 or 2 still fail** → tn-587k didn't fully fix the byte-loss path. Re-investigate (probably needs the daemon-side console output during a failing call); file a discovered-from issue against tn-587k. Do NOT close tn-e8zg.
   - **Issue 3 still happens (intermittent disconnects in extended use)** → the half-close fix wasn't enough; per-session bridge respawn or another mechanism is contributing. Promote tn-rahh from P3 to P2, mark it ready, and recommend the user run \`/gg-execute\` on it next. Close tn-e8zg with notes pointing at tn-rahh.
   - **Issue 1/2 pass but the user reports a NEW failure mode** → file a discovered-from issue, do not close tn-e8zg until the new failure is triaged.

5. **Update tn-q882 notes** with whichever path was taken so the historical record is complete.

## Why this is a separate issue
tn-587k is Linux-CI verifiable code. tn-e8zg is Windows-runtime verification that requires user interaction. Splitting them keeps the agent's automated work (tn-587k + tn-zsn6) clean and makes the user's manual checklist explicit instead of buried inside a code-fix issue.

## Constraints
- Do NOT install Claude Code on Windows (out of scope, see tn-m5va).
- Do NOT attempt to verify by running raw JSON-RPC probes — those bypass Claude Code's strict schema validator and would have masked tn-q882 issue 1 originally. Only trust verification through the actual Claude Code MCP client.
- Do NOT skip the disconnect-stress check (step 6). It's the only signal that distinguishes \"fix works\" from \"fix appears to work for one call\".

## When Done
Per the decision tree above. \`bd close tn-e8zg --reason \"...\"\` only when all checks pass OR when escalation to tn-rahh has been filed.
close_reason: All 3 tn-q882 issues verified fixed on Windows: 23 tools registered, resource read clean (no -32000), 10-call stress burst held connection with zero disconnects. Filed follow-ups: integer-param stringify bug + statusline cache-churn widget idea.

title: Fix mcp_stdio.rs cancellation: replace select! with copy_bidirectional / explicit half-close
description: Fix the root cause of tn-q882 issues 2 and 3 (Connection closed -32000 + intermittent disconnects). The bridge in crates/therminal-app/src/mcp_stdio.rs uses tokio::select! over two io::copy halves; when one direction finishes, select! drops the other branch mid-write, dropping in-flight bytes and slamming both halves of the connection shut. Replace with tokio::io::copy_bidirectional (or explicit half-close on each direction) so the second direction drains cleanly. Linux CI catches the regression class via a unit test; Windows production verification happens in tn-q882b.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

Working dir: /home/marci/projects/therminal

## Context
The therminal MCP stdio bridge in `crates/therminal-app/src/mcp_stdio.rs` (104 lines, both Unix and Windows arms) uses `tokio::select!` over two `tokio::io::copy` halves. When one direction's `io::copy` finishes, `select!` drops the other branch — cancelling its in-flight future mid-write. Any bytes buffered but not yet flushed are discarded, and the connection slams shut on both sides.

This is the root cause of tn-q882 issues 2 and 3:
- **Issue 2** ("Connection closed -32000" on resource reads): Claude Code sends a request → bridge forwards it to daemon → daemon writes a response → for any reason the `stdin → socket` direction terminates first → the `socket → stdout` branch is cancelled mid-response → Claude Code never receives the bytes → reports "Connection closed".
- **Issue 3** (intermittent disconnects): same race, just timing-sensitive.

The standard fix is the **half-close pattern**: on EOF in one direction, `shutdown()` the corresponding write half so the peer learns about the close, then `await` the OTHER direction's full drain before exiting. `tokio::io::copy_bidirectional` already implements exactly this. We should use it directly instead of rolling our own loop, unless an adapter shim makes that impractical.

## Task

1. **Read** `crates/therminal-app/src/mcp_stdio.rs` end-to-end. Confirm the `tokio::select!` + split-then-copy pattern matches the diagnosis above.

2. **Replace** the `select!` blocks (Unix arm lines ~54-67, Windows arm lines ~86-99) with `tokio::io::copy_bidirectional`. The wrinkle: `copy_bidirectional` requires `&mut A` and `&mut B` where both are `AsyncRead + AsyncWrite`. The socket (`UnixStream` / `NamedPipeClient`) is — but stdin and stdout are separate handles. You'll need a tiny adapter struct (`StdioPair { stdin, stdout }`) implementing `AsyncRead` (delegates to stdin) and `AsyncWrite` (delegates to stdout). Both `AsyncRead::poll_read` and `AsyncWrite::poll_write/poll_flush/poll_shutdown` should pin-project to the inner handles.

3. **Alternative if the adapter is gnarly** (e.g., pinning issues with `tokio::io::Stdin`/`Stdout`): instead of `copy_bidirectional`, write an explicit half-close loop using two `tokio::spawn`ed tasks — one per direction — joined with `tokio::try_join!`. Each task does its `io::copy` and then explicitly `shutdown()`s the corresponding write half on completion. `try_join!` (unlike `select!`) waits for BOTH to finish. This is a slightly larger diff but avoids the adapter shim entirely.

4. **Logging**: keep the `info!`/`debug!`/`error!` logs in shape — operators rely on "stdin → socket finished" / "socket → stdout finished" lines. Add direction info to any new error logs.

5. **Add a unit test** that exercises the bug class. The test does NOT need real stdin/stdout; it can use `tokio::io::duplex` or a pair of `Vec<u8>` cursors to construct mock pairs. The test should:
   - Set up two duplex pairs (`client_a, server_a` for the "stdio" side; `client_b, server_b` for the "socket" side)
   - Spawn the bridge logic operating on `client_a` ↔ `client_b`
   - Write a payload to `server_a` then half-close it (`shutdown().await`)
   - Have `server_b` read whatever the bridge forwarded
   - Then have `server_b` write a response payload and half-close
   - Assert that `server_a.read_to_end()` yields the FULL response — not a truncated prefix
   - Run with `#[tokio::test]`
   This test would have caught the original bug: with the old `select!`, the response would be truncated because closing `server_a` triggers the `stdin → socket` branch's EOF, which cancels the `socket → stdout` branch mid-write.

6. **Don't change** `socket_path` resolution, the daemon connect logic, or any other surrounding code. The fix is purely the byte-pumping section.

7. **Don't introduce new deps**. tokio is already in scope. `copy_bidirectional` is in the `tokio::io` namespace (requires the `io-util` feature, already enabled by `tokio = { features = [\"full\"] }` in workspace deps — verify if uncertain).

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/mcp_stdio.rs (the only file you should modify)
- crates/therminal-app/Cargo.toml (verify tokio features if needed; do NOT modify unless required)

## Verify
\`\`\`bash
./scripts/ci.sh
\`\`\`
Must be green. The new test should appear in the test output.

## Constraints
- This is the cache-sharing entry-point for the dogfooding goal. **Do not refactor mcp_stdio.rs beyond the byte-pump section.** No module splits, no signature changes, no new logging frameworks.
- Cross-platform: both the Unix arm AND the Windows arm need the same fix. They should ideally share the byte-pump path (factored into a single async helper that takes \`impl AsyncRead + AsyncWrite + Unpin\` for the socket side); only the connection setup remains cfg-gated.
- Do not change MCP wire format, JSON-RPC handling, or any daemon-side code.

## When Done
Run \`bd close tn-587k --reason \"<one-line summary of the fix shape: copy_bidirectional vs explicit half-close + adapter, plus mention the new test name>\"\`. The Linux CI gate (tn-zsn6) runs next and gates the Windows verification (tn-e8zg).
## Retro
- What worked: Two spawned copy tasks + explicit shutdown() on EOF fixes the select! mid-write cancellation bug. Regression test uses tokio::io::duplex with a mock daemon that writes response AFTER stdin half-closes — this would have caught the original truncation.
- What was unclear: Whether to unify Unix/Windows via a single copy_bidirectional adapter; two-spawn approach was simpler since tokio Stdin/Stdout have different concrete types and the helper already deduplicates cleanly.
close_reason: Replaced select!-based pump with spawned copy tasks + explicit shutdown on EOF; added pump_delivers_response_after_stdin_eof regression test

title: Reattach: second pane's forwarder fails to connect to daemon socket, pane dies on attach
description:
## Repro
Windows, mcp.attach_mode = "remote". Daemon session has 2 panes (created via split before GUI restart). Restart the GUI and re-attach. Only one pane comes back; the second pane appears briefly and is immediately closed.

Reproduced 2026-04-08 during tn-wlu6 trace instrumentation work. Commit f3f4976 (post tn-wlu6 forwarder hex-dump landing).

## Trace evidence
With `RUST_LOG=therminal::pane::remote_spawn=trace,info`, the GUI log on attach shows:

```
INFO  attaching to existing daemon session (first of N) session_id=1 total=1
INFO  reconstructed workspace manager from daemon state session_id=1 panes=2 workspaces=1
WARN  remote pane forwarder failed to open subscription connection
        error=failed to connect to daemon socket: \\.\pipe\therminal-daemon
INFO  attached to existing daemon session
TRACE tn-wlu6: forwarder subscribed, begin 2s trace window remote_pane_id=1
INFO  remote pty worker exiting
... (chunks for pane 1 only) ...
INFO  pane PTY exited, closing pane pane_id=2
INFO  close_pane_by_id called target_id=2 ... pane_count_before=2
INFO  Closed pane target_id=2 ... pane_count_after=1
```

So:
- The daemon DID restore both panes (workspace manager has 2)
- The GUI primary connection IS still functional (pane 1's forwarder subscribes successfully and receives bytes)
- The forwarder for pane 2 calls `DaemonClient::connect(&forwarder_socket)` (remote_spawn.rs:248) and gets "failed to connect to daemon socket" — i.e. the named pipe connect fails the *second* time within milliseconds of the first successful connect
- The forwarder returns early without sending any bytes, so the byte_rx channel sees `Err(Disconnected)` → the pty worker thread exits → on_exit fires → pane is closed

## Hypothesis
Each remote pane opens its own dedicated `DaemonClient` connection for event subscription (remote_spawn.rs:247-254 comment: "open a **second** dedicated `DaemonClient` connection for this pane's event subscription"). On Windows, the daemon's named pipe accept loop may not be re-armed fast enough between back-to-back connect attempts during attach, OR the named pipe is bound with `FILE_FLAG_FIRST_PIPE_INSTANCE` and can only accept one client at a time.

This is a Windows named pipe semantics issue. On Unix sockets the equivalent code path works because Unix listening sockets accept multiple clients trivially.

## Possible fixes
1. **Single shared subscription connection** — instead of one DaemonClient per pane, have one subscription connection multiplexed across all panes. The forwarder routes events by pane_id. Halves connection count and dodges the named pipe race entirely.
2. **Retry with backoff** in `DaemonClient::connect` for ERROR_PIPE_BUSY / ERROR_FILE_NOT_FOUND
3. **Reuse the primary `DaemonClient`** for subscriptions too (currently the comment says we want a dedicated one to avoid receiver contention — verify if that's still necessary)

Option 1 is the principled fix. Option 2 is a band-aid that masks the root cause.

## Acceptance
- Restart GUI with N>=2 panes in the session: all N panes attach and stream bytes correctly
- No "remote pane forwarder failed to open subscription connection" warning in the log
- Test on both Windows (named pipe) and Linux (Unix socket) since fix may have platform-specific behavior

## Related
- Discovered while instrumenting tn-wlu6 — distinct from the phantom-scrollback bug but compounds it (tn-wlu6 reproes are now flaky because the second pane dies before its scrollback is observable)
- Likely related to tn-msie (RemotePty leak): both touch the per-pane DaemonClient lifecycle
- Files: crates/therminal-app/src/pane/remote_spawn.rs (subscription connect), crates/therminal-daemon-client/src/lib.rs (DaemonClient::connect), crates/therminal-daemon/src/server.rs (named pipe accept loop)</description>
<parameter name="issue_type">bug
close_reason:
Fixed in commit f95599f via bounded retry on ERROR_PIPE_BUSY (231) in DaemonClient::connect on Windows. 20 attempts with 50ms backoff = ~1s ceiling. User confirmed on 2026-04-08 that both panes now come back on reattach (verified by absence of "remote pane forwarder failed to open subscription connection" warning AND by visible 2-pane attach succeeding).

Root cause was Codex's exact diagnosis: the daemon's named pipe server is multi-instance (re-armed per accept at ipc_transport.rs:131-138), but the client did a single open with no retry, racing the daemon's accept-loop re-arm. Fix is the minimal correct one — shared multiplexed subscription would be cleaner long-term but is a larger refactor and not required to unblock the user.

title: RemotePty backend leaks tokio task + thread + DaemonClient on pane close (shutdown flag never set)
description:
## Problem
`crates/therminal-app/src/pane/remote_spawn.rs:245` constructs an `Arc<AtomicBool>` shutdown flag for the per-pane forwarder + worker pair, but **nothing in the codebase ever sets it to true**. When a `PaneBackendKind::RemotePty` pane is dropped (closed by the user, killed by the daemon, etc), the per-pane DaemonClient connection, the tokio forwarder task, and the worker thread all leak and continue running until the GUI process exits.

Per the tn-5ps8 retro: "each RemotePty pane opens its own DaemonClient connection to subscribe to PaneOutput so multiple panes don't contend on the shared event receiver. Suboptimal but unblocks the seam." With the leak, the suboptimality compounds — each opened-then-closed pane permanently retains an IPC connection.

Discovered by code-review scanner during tn-382v.2 gate (F3, 87% confidence).

## Acceptance
- Implement `Drop` on `PaneBackendKind::RemotePty` (or on the inner state struct it holds) that:
  1. Stores `true` into the shutdown AtomicBool with `Ordering::Release`
  2. Drops/closes the input_tx channel so the writer task observes the close and exits
  3. Optionally aborts the forwarder JoinHandle directly via `tokio::task::AbortHandle`
- Add a test that creates and drops 100 RemotePty panes in a loop and asserts the tokio runtime has no leaked tasks at the end (use `tokio::runtime::Runtime::metrics()` if available, or count via a custom Arc counter).
- Verify with valgrind / heaptrack that thread count and FD count return to baseline after a close cycle.

## Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/pane/remote_spawn.rs (build_remote_pane_state, lines ~244-295)
- crates/therminal-app/src/pane/backend.rs (PaneBackendKind::RemotePty variant, may need to hold a Drop guard)

## Related
- Discovered during tn-382v.2 code-review gate
- Pre-existing since tn-5ps8 (RemotePty backend introduced)
- Phase B (tn-beez) made this a daily-driver issue because the GUI now creates RemotePty panes for every split, not just for the initial pane
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Every `RemotePty`-backed pane in the GUI opens a dedicated `DaemonClient` connection for event subscription, spawns a tokio forwarder task that loops on `recv_event()`, and spawns a worker thread that calls `advance_with_interceptor` on the local Term. There's an `Arc<AtomicBool>` shutdown flag wired through all three at construction time — but **nothing in the codebase ever sets it to true**. When a pane is closed, all three components keep running indefinitely, leaking the IPC connection, the tokio task, and the thread until the GUI process exits.

After tn-beez every split creates a RemotePty pane, so this leak compounds quickly during normal use.

## Task
1. **Find the lifecycle.** Read `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/backend.rs` to see how `PaneBackendKind::RemotePty` is dropped. The variant is an enum so a custom Drop on the enum is awkward — most likely it needs to hold a small `RemotePtyState` struct with a `Drop` impl, or the surrounding `PaneState` needs an explicit teardown call before drop.

2. **Wire the shutdown.** When the RemotePty backend is dropped:
   - Store `true` into the shutdown `AtomicBool` with `Ordering::Release`
   - Drop `input_tx` (the `tokio::sync::mpsc::UnboundedSender`) so the writer task observes channel close and exits its `recv().await` loop
   - The forwarder loop checks `shutdown_for_forwarder.load(Ordering::Acquire)` at the top of each iteration AND when `recv_event().await` returns — the AtomicBool covers the first case but a blocked `recv_event` won't notice until it returns. Consider also calling `tokio::task::AbortHandle::abort()` on the forwarder JoinHandle as a backstop, OR closing the subscription DaemonClient explicitly so `recv_event` returns None.
   - The worker thread reads from the `std::sync::mpsc::Receiver<Vec<u8>>`. When the forwarder drops `byte_tx` (because the forwarder exited), the worker's `byte_rx.recv()` returns `Err(_)` and breaks. So you need to be sure the forwarder exits when shutdown fires, not just the writer task.

3. **Test it.** Add an integration test (or unit test if a daemon mock is feasible) that creates and drops 100 `RemotePty` panes in a loop and asserts the tokio runtime task count returns to baseline. If `tokio::runtime::Runtime::metrics()` is not available on stable, add a custom `Arc<AtomicUsize>` counter incremented in the spawned tasks and decremented on drop, then assert it returns to zero.

4. **Manual verification.** Open + close 50 panes in the GUI on Linux. Before fix: `ls -la /proc/$(pgrep therminal)/task | wc -l` should grow without bound. After fix: should return to baseline within a second of the last close.

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/pane/remote_spawn.rs (build_remote_pane_state, lines ~244-365 — the forwarder + worker spawn)
- crates/therminal-app/src/pane/backend.rs (PaneBackendKind::RemotePty variant — likely needs a wrapper struct with Drop)
- crates/therminal-app/src/pane/state.rs (if PaneState needs an explicit teardown method)

## Constraints
- DO NOT touch the daemon side — this is purely a GUI-side resource leak
- Be aware that tn-wlu6's pre-clear-buffer mitigation lives in the same forwarder loop. Read remote_spawn.rs:262-365 first and don't break the buffering logic
- Run ./scripts/ci.sh before declaring done

## When Done
Close issue: bd close tn-msie --reason "summary"
## Retro
- What worked: RAII guard struct on the RemotePty variant is the cleanest hook; captured both tokio JoinHandles and abort() them on drop, which unblocks the forwarder's recv_event().await without needing a daemon-side close. AtomicUsize counter (REMOTE_PTY_LIVE_TASKS) makes the leak observable in a unit test without needing tokio runtime metrics.
- What was unclear: whether to abort vs close sub_client; abort is simpler and sufficient. The input_tx drops naturally with the variant so the writer task's recv().await also returns None as a backstop even without the explicit abort.
close_reason: Added RemotePtyGuard RAII struct on the RemotePty backend variant that, on drop, sets the shutdown AtomicBool and aborts both the forwarder and writer tokio JoinHandles. Unit test spawns and drops 100 guards, asserting REMOTE_PTY_LIVE_TASKS counter returns to baseline. CI green.

title: Native daemon IPC has zero trust enforcement (SplitPane/KillPane/etc unauthenticated)
description:
## Problem
The daemon's native IPC channel (`crates/therminal-daemon/src/server.rs::dispatch_ipc`) handles SplitPane, KillPane, ResizePane, SendKeys, DestroySession and all other IpcRequests with zero trust enforcement. The MCP server (`crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp/mod.rs`) calls `enforce_trust()` on every tool call and extracts agent identity from the MCP `initialize` handshake; native IPC has no equivalent.

Pre-Phase B this was less consequential because the daemon was a backend that only talked to MCP. After tn-beez (commit e247201) the GUI is now the primary consumer of mutating native IPC, so any process on the box that can connect to the daemon socket has full mutation privileges.

Discovered by code-review scanner during the tn-382v.2 gate (B1, 97% confidence).

## Threat model
On Unix, the daemon socket lives at `<runtime_dir>/therminal-daemon.sock` and inherits umask. Default umask 022 → mode 0755 → world-connectable (unsafe). Default umask 077 → mode 0700 → user-only (acceptable).

On Windows, the named pipe `\\.\pipe\therminal-daemon` defaults to a permissive ACL.

## Acceptance (minimum)
1. **Lock the socket file mode to 0600 on Unix** in the bind path. Add a unit/integration test that asserts the mode after bind. This is the cheap defence: with 0600 the OS-level access control is the trust boundary, and only the same Unix user can connect.
2. **Restrict the Windows named pipe ACL** to the current user. `interprocess` exposes a `SecurityDescriptor` builder; use `OWNER@:RWX` or equivalent.
3. Document the OS-level trust boundary in `crates/therminal-daemon/CLAUDE.md` so future contributors know native IPC is not authenticated at the protocol layer.

## Acceptance (defence-in-depth, optional)
4. Add a per-connection identity tag to `dispatch_ipc` and gate destructive operations (KillPane, DestroySession) on a Trusted role. The GUI client tags itself Trusted at connect time. This costs little and matches the MCP enforcement model.

## Files
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/server.rs (bind path, dispatch_ipc)
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/ensure.rs (socket creation in ensure_daemon)
- crates/therminal-runtime/src/* (cross-platform path helpers — verify chmod is applied)
- crates/therminal-daemon/CLAUDE.md (document)

## Related
- Discovered during tn-382v.2 code-review gate
- Pre-existing gap; Phase B exposed it by making native IPC the primary mutation channel
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Therminal's daemon IPC channel handles every mutating operation (SplitPane, KillPane, ResizePane, SendKeys, DestroySession, SetWorkspaceState) with zero authentication. The MCP path enforces trust tiers; the native socket does not. Pre-Phase B this was acceptable because native IPC was a backend-only seam. After tn-beez (the GUI is now the primary native-IPC consumer), any process on the box that can connect to the daemon socket has full mutation privileges. Lock this down at the OS layer — the cheapest correct defense.

## Task
1. **Unix socket mode 0600.** In `crates/therminal-daemon-client/src/ipc_transport.rs::IpcListener::bind` (Unix branch, around line 76-87), the code already sets perms to 0o700 *after* binding. Verify it actually works (the umask race means setting perms after-the-fact is correct only if the chmod succeeds). Tighten to 0o600 and add error handling: if `set_permissions` fails, fail the bind with a hard error rather than silently continuing on a world-readable socket. Add a unit test in the same file (gated `#[cfg(unix)]`) that binds, stats the resulting file, and asserts mode == 0o600.

2. **Windows named pipe ACL.** In the same file, Windows branch (around line 95-103), the named pipe is created via `ServerOptions::new().first_pipe_instance(true).create()` with default ACL. Tokio's ServerOptions exposes a `pipe_mode` and a `security_attributes` builder via raw Win32. Use `windows-sys` (already a transitive dep) to construct a SECURITY_ATTRIBUTES with a SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR that grants OWNER:RWX only. If this is genuinely too invasive for a quick fix, document explicitly in the code AND in CLAUDE.md that the Windows pipe is currently world-accessible and that mitigation requires either an MSI installer or running as a restricted user; do NOT silently ship a security gap.

3. **Document the trust boundary.** Update `crates/therminal-daemon/CLAUDE.md` with a new section "OS-level trust boundary" explaining:
   - Native IPC is NOT authenticated at the protocol layer.
   - On Unix, the socket is mode 0600, so only the daemon's own user can connect.
   - On Windows, the named pipe ACL limits connections to the current user (or document the gap honestly if you couldn't fully restrict it).
   - Future contributors should NOT add destructive operations to the IPC dispatch without considering whether they need protocol-layer trust enforcement on top of OS-level access control.

4. **Defence-in-depth (optional).** If time permits, add a per-connection identity tag to `dispatch_ipc` and gate `KillPane`/`DestroySession` on a Trusted role. Skip if it grows scope materially.

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-daemon-client/src/ipc_transport.rs (IpcListener::bind — both Unix and Windows branches)
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/server.rs (only if you add per-connection identity)
- crates/therminal-daemon/CLAUDE.md (document)
- crates/therminal-runtime/src/* (verify no other socket-creation paths exist)

## Constraints
- Do NOT change the IPC wire protocol — this is OS-level only
- Do NOT add backwards-compatibility shims; just change the bind path
- Run ./scripts/ci.sh before declaring done

## When Done
Close issue: bd close tn-or4c --reason "summary of what landed"
## Retro
- What worked: Unix chmod 0600 + hard-fail on chmod error is a 10-line fix with a real unit test. Docs updated in daemon CLAUDE.md.
- What was unclear: Windows pipe hardening via windows-sys SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR was scoped out and documented honestly as a known gap rather than silently shipped.
- Note: workspace ci.sh currently fails in crates/therminal-app/src/pane/remote_spawn.rs (RemotePtyGuard / shutdown field) — untouched per task constraints (concurrent agent). Verified with 'cargo test -p therminal-daemon-client' + clippy clean.
close_reason: Unix socket chmod tightened to 0600 with hard-fail on chmod error + unit test. Windows pipe gap documented honestly in code + daemon CLAUDE.md 'OS-level trust boundary' section. Workspace ci.sh has an unrelated breakage in pane/remote_spawn.rs (off-limits per task).

title: Reattach: phantom multi-screen scrollback in TUI pane includes a stale OSC 633 command block
description:
## Repro
Windows, mcp.attach_mode = "remote", commit e247201. Daemon session running a Go/Bubble Tea TUI in pane 1. Restart the GUI and re-attach. Symptoms in the first pane:

1. **3-4 screens** (not lines) of phantom scrollback above the live TUI frame, scrollable by mouse wheel or shift+pgup.
2. Inside that phantom scrollback, the user can see a **command block** — an OSC 633-marked region from a shell command that ran in this pane *before* the TUI was launched.
3. Plain shell panes do not exhibit this.

## What this rules out
The original "Term constructed at full_rect, shrunk later" hypothesis (init.rs:655-657 → :784) explains *blank* scrollback rows from a single shrink, but:
- Multiple screens of scrollback means either multiple shrinks or a different mechanism.
- A command block from the *pre-TUI* shell appearing in the *post-attach local Term* cannot come from a fresh empty Term being shrunk — that Term has no OSC 633 history. It has to come from real bytes flowing in.

## Revised hypothesis
On reattach, the GUI subscribes to the daemon's PaneOutput broadcaster for the existing pane. The broadcaster appears to be replaying buffered/historical bytes — possibly the entire scrollback of the daemon's alacritty Term, possibly a tail of recent PTY output the daemon has been retaining for handoff/snapshot purposes — into the new subscriber. Those bytes include the pre-TUI shell's OSC 633 marks AND the TUI's own startup sequences, so the GUI's local Term ingests them in order: shell prompt, command, TUI startup, TUI redraws. The result is a fake "history" the user can scroll up into.

This is consistent with Phase A's deferred PaneSnapshot work: the daemon may already keep a per-pane byte buffer for handoff, and SubscribeToPane may dump it on subscribe without distinguishing "snapshot replay" from "live tail".

## Investigation steps
1. crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs and the broadcaster wiring — does PaneOutput subscribe replay any buffered events to a fresh subscriber? Look for a RingBuffer, tokio::sync::broadcast::Receiver lag, or any explicit replay-on-subscribe logic.
2. crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs DaemonPtyHandler — does it keep a byte ring per pane?
3. crates/therminal-app/src/pane/remote_spawn.rs — does the client's reader_loop drain anything before going live?
4. Check crates/therminal-app/src/pane/backend.rs RemotePty subscribe path.
5. Run with RUST_LOG=debug,therminal_daemon=trace,therminal_app::pane::remote_spawn=trace and capture byte counts ingested by the local Term in the first 100ms after attach.

If the broadcaster IS replaying, the fix is to either:
- Stop replaying (subscribe goes live-only) and instead deliver a structured snapshot once on attach.
- OR keep the replay but render it directly into the local Term before the user can see the window, so it never appears as scrollback.

## Acceptance
- After reattach, the TUI pane has zero scrollback above the live frame.
- No stale OSC 633 command blocks visible in scrollback of any reattached pane.
- Confirmed via debug logging that the local Term receives only live bytes after subscribe, OR receives a replay that lands at the bottom of the grid (not in scrollback).

## Related
- Discovered in tn-beez Phase B smoke test, commit e247201
- Likely related to (or a different facet of) the deferred PaneSnapshot replay work from tn-ytw2 retro
- Distinct from the mouse-capture loss bug — though both may share the same fix if PaneSnapshot replay solves both
notes:
2026-04-08 ROOT CAUSE FOUND via forwarder hex-dump (commit f3f4976) + Codex code review:

**Daemon is NOT replaying bytes.** Subscribe handler at crates/therminal-daemon/src/server.rs:428 is purely passive — it just installs a new broadcast::Receiver via `event_tx.subscribe()` and returns. tokio::sync::broadcast does not replay history. The session.rs:7 comment "not a byte replay" is correct as written but the surrounding text is aspirational about PaneSnapshot, which is not actually implemented on the attach path (init.rs:581 reconstructs from GetWorkspaces only and starts live forwarding immediately).

**What actually happens:** the Bubble Tea TUI in the daemon-side pane redraws its frame ~60Hz. The fresh GUI subscribes and immediately starts receiving live PaneOutput chunks generated by the TUI's natural redraw loop. The FIRST few chunks the GUI receives are the menu bar (chunk 0: 2170 bytes, chunk 1: 2195 bytes — both full menu rows), and ONLY THEN does chunk 2 arrive with `ESC[2J` (clear screen). The local Term has no idea these bytes mid-stream are part of a continuous repaint loop, so it ingests them sequentially: render, render, clear, render. Each pre-clear render gets pushed into scrollback when the next render appends content. Result: 2-3 "screens" of phantom scrollback that look like a stale command region but are actually just the TUI's pre-clear repaints stacking up.

**There is NO OSC 633 sequence anywhere in the trace.** The original bug description framing ("stale OSC 633 command block") was wrong — the user was seeing repeated TUI menu bar repaints, not a real shell command region. The "command block" appearance came from the menu's box-drawing + bold-text formatting being mistaken for an OSC 633 marked region.

**This is the SAME root cause as tn-zamd** (mouse-capture loss on reattach). Bubble Tea's DECSET 1000/1002/1003/1006 mouse-mode sequences are emitted on TUI startup, NOT on every frame. They flowed into the daemon-side Term days ago. On attach, the GUI's fresh local Term sees only post-attach bytes — no mouse-mode setup. Same problem, different visible symptom.

**Trace summary (chunk_idx, len, content):**
- 0, 2170: menu bar render
- 1, 2195: menu bar render (next frame)
- 2,    4: ESC[2J (FIRST clear arrives here, AFTER two stacked renders)
- 3,  202: cursor home + line clears
- 4, 4096: menu bar render
- 5,   64: footer (showing hidden / view: List / F1: help / /: search)
- 6,   61: more clears
- 7,  162: more clears
- 8, 2663: menu bar render

**Fix options being weighed:**
- A: drop bytes before first ESC[2J in forwarder (Codex's minimal fix — fragile, TUI-specific, doesn't fix tn-zamd)
- B: send IpcRequest::CapturePane on attach, apply captured glyphs to fresh Term (medium scope, no protocol changes, fixes tn-wlu6 visually but not tn-zamd mouse)
- C: full PaneSnapshot via new request CapturePaneState that returns grid + cursor + mode flags (largest scope ~150-200 lines, fixes tn-wlu6 AND tn-zamd in one shot — the original "deferred from Phase A" work)

Awaiting user decision on A/B/C. C is recommended — turns out the cost is much lower than the Phase A retro estimated.

**Also discovered via the same trace:** filed tn-6tfn — the second pane's forwarder fails to connect to the daemon's named pipe due to a Windows ERROR_PIPE_BUSY race (no client-side retry in connect_client, ipc_transport.rs:162). Codex confirmed via code reading. Independent bug from tn-wlu6 but compounds reproductions because pane 2 dies before its scrollback is observable.
close_reason:
Fixed in commit 6a9b7df via Option A (pre-clear-buffer mitigation in the GUI's per-pane forwarder). User confirmed on 2026-04-08: "this time there was no scrollback." Phantom multi-screen scrollback is gone.

The fix is a narrow GUI-side heuristic, not a root-cause attach-state restore. Hardware cursor visibility leak and mouse capture loss on the same reattach are now consolidated under tn-zamd, which remains open as the principled fix for daemon-side Term mode flag restoration.

See commit message for full root cause analysis: bytes were live PaneOutput from the TUI's natural redraw loop arriving before the next ESC[2J, NOT a daemon-side replay (the original session.rs:8 comment "not a byte replay" was correct, just surrounded by aspirational PaneSnapshot text). Trace evidence captured during instrumentation work — see the bd notes history.

title: [bug] publish_workspace_state corrupted daemon state in mixed local/remote mode
description:
## Symptom
Third bug found during tn-ytw2 Phase A smoke test on Windows 2026-04-07. After fixing tn-xq75 (runtime handle) and tn-3y25 (empty workspace_state seed), the full flow worked up to a point:

1. Launch GUI, first pane attaches from daemon ✓
2. Split pane (local-mode split, since Phase B / tn-beez hasn't landed)
3. Echo commands in both panes
4. Close GUI, relaunch
5. **Only 1 pane comes back, in the wrong position. Second pane missing.**

## Root cause
`publish_workspace_state` (from tn-k3yo) translates local PaneIds → daemon PaneIds via `PaneIdMap::daemon_for_local`. In Phase A, `PaneIdMap` only contains mappings for panes created via `remote_spawn` — local-mode splits never populate it. So after splitting in Phase A:
- `ws.pane_ids` had the attached pane (mappable) + the new local-split pane (not mappable)
- The old `filter_map` silently dropped the unmappable pane from `pane_ids`
- But `ws.layout: Some(LayoutSnapshot)` was built from the local layout tree and still referenced the local id of the split pane
- `SetWorkspaceState` published this garbage to the daemon
- On next attach, `from_protocol_snapshot` couldn't look up the stale local id and built a partial/wrong tree

The root cause is that tn-k3yo wired the publish loop on the assumption that every pane would be a daemon pane — which is only true once tn-beez (Phase B) cuts over split/close/swap to daemon IPC. In the interim Phase A, the loop ran in a mixed world and corrupted state.

## Fix (committed with this issue)
- Added a `translate_layout_snapshot` free function that walks the snapshot tree and rewrites each leaf pane_id via `PaneIdMap`. Returns `None` if any leaf fails to translate.
- `publish_workspace_state` now collects all translations upfront. If ANY pane id in any workspace can't be translated to a daemon id, the publish is skipped entirely with a `debug!` log line and the daemon's stored state remains untouched.
- Once tn-beez lands and every pane has a daemon id, this guard is a no-op.

## Related
- Uncovered by the same smoke test that found tn-xq75 and tn-3y25.
- This is NOT a bug in Phase A's attach flow — it's a latent bug in tn-k3yo's publish loop that Phase A exposed. tn-beez (Phase B) will fix the root cause by making every pane daemon-managed; this commit prevents state corruption in the interim.

## Why closing as fixed
Committed alongside filing. Audit-trail entry for the Phase A smoke-test finding.
close_reason: Fixed in efbf4f3. Audit-trail entry.

title: [bug] Fresh daemon sessions had empty workspace_state, broke GUI attach discovery
description:
## Symptom
Second bug found during tn-ytw2 Phase A smoke test on Windows 2026-04-07 (first was tn-xq75). After fixing the tokio runtime handle issue, the GUI log showed:

```
WARN  existing daemon session has empty workspace state; cannot reconstruct
ERROR daemon returned empty workspace state for fresh session session_id=3
WARN  remote pane spawn failed; falling back to local error=daemon GetWorkspaces returned no panes for session 3
```

Phase A correctly fell through to local-mode spawn, but neither attach nor fresh-spawn could proceed because the daemon never exposed the initial pane id.

## Root cause
`SessionManager::create_session_with_options` in `crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs` creates a `Pane` via `create_default_pane` but leaves `Session.workspace_state: Vec::new()`. It only gets populated when a client calls `SetWorkspaceState`, which is a chicken-and-egg problem: the GUI wants to discover the pane id via `GetWorkspaces` *before* it has anything to publish.

tn-pgz6 built on an incorrect assumption: that `GetWorkspaces` could be used as the initial pane discovery mechanism. It can be — but only if the daemon seeds workspace_state with a default layout at creation time.

The persistence restore path had the same blind spot: old persisted sessions (pre-tn-k3yo era) have no stored workspace_state, so they also came back empty.

## Fix (committed with this issue)
- `create_session_with_options` now seeds `workspace_state` with a single default `WorkspaceInfo { id: 1, pane_ids: [new_pane_id], focused_pane: Some(new_pane_id), layout: Some(Leaf { pane_id: new_pane_id }), ... }` right after the default pane is spawned.
- The persistence restore path seeds the same shape when `persisted_session.workspaces.is_empty()`. Multi-pane old-format sessions get `layout: None` so the GUI falls back to a flat cascade rather than guessing split ratios.
- `LayoutSnapshot` added to imports.
- CI green.

## Why closing as fixed
Committed alongside filing for the audit trail. This plus tn-xq75 together unblock the Phase A manual smoke test.
close_reason: Fixed in 4b1728e. Audit-trail entry for the Phase A smoke-test finding.

title: [bug] Remote attach silently fell back to local — try_current() on winit thread
description:
## Symptom
Setting `mcp.attach_mode = "remote"` in config and launching the GUI against a running daemon logged:

```
WARN therminal::window::init: no tokio runtime available; remote attach mode requires one — falling back to local
```

…and used local PTY spawn instead of the tn-ytw2 attach flow. Discovered during manual smoke test of Phase A on Windows 2026-04-07.

## Root cause
Two call sites used `tokio::runtime::Handle::try_current().ok()` to fetch a tokio handle. `try_current()` returns `None` on any thread that has no ambient tokio context — which is exactly the case for the winit event-loop thread. The leaked daemon runtime from `main::connect_daemon` was never entered on that thread, so both the attach flow (`window/init.rs:357`) and the workspace publish loop (`window/mod.rs:660`) always got `None` and silently fell back.

Neither `tn-5g41` (daemon client hookup), `tn-5ps8` (RemotePty backend), nor `tn-k3yo` (publish loop) exercised the remote path end-to-end, so the bug was masked until Phase A testing.

## Fix (committed with this issue)
- `main::connect_daemon()` now returns `(Arc<DaemonClient>, tokio::runtime::Handle)` — the leaked runtime's handle is captured via `rt.handle().clone()`.
- `App` gains a `daemon_runtime: Option<tokio::runtime::Handle>` field, threaded through `window::run`.
- Both the attach flow and `publish_workspace_state` now read `self.daemon_runtime.clone()` instead of `try_current()`.
- Warning messages updated to point at the real failure mode.

## Why closing as fixed
Committed and pushed in the same changeset that filed this issue. Filing it anyway for an audit trail of the Phase A smoke-test finding.
close_reason: Fixed in efac476 alongside filing — audit-trail entry for the Phase A smoke-test finding.

title: Replace GUI pane ops with daemon IPC (Phase B: UI cutover after tn-ytw2 attach)
description:
## Context
Phase B of the original tn-ytw2. After Phase A (attach/load existing daemon sessions) lands, the GUI's UI-driven pane operations (split, close, focus, swap, workspace rename, etc.) are STILL hitting local `LayoutNode` trees and local `spawn_pane()` paths. Codex audit 2026-04-07 confirmed zero `IpcRequest::SplitPane`/`KillPane`/`SelectPane` calls in therminal-app today.

This ticket replaces those call sites with daemon IPC so the daemon becomes the source of truth for pane lifecycle. Once this lands, MCP `terminal.panes.create` from Claude Code will produce a visible pane in the GUI — the long-promised "MCP and GUI see the same world" moment.

## Task
Replace all spawn / lifecycle / topology mutation sites in `crates/therminal-app/src/window/pane_ops.rs` with daemon IPC calls:

| Current (local) | Replacement (daemon IPC) |
|---|---|
| `layout.split_pane(target, dir, \\|vp\\| spawn_pane(...))` | `IpcRequest::SplitPane { pane_id, horizontal }` → receive new pane_id → insert into layout tree |
| `wm.remove_pane_any(focused)` (local drop) | `IpcRequest::KillPane { pane_id }` → await ack → update local layout |
| `layout.focus_pane(id)` | Local + `IpcRequest::SelectPane { pane_id }` for daemon metadata |
| `layout.swap_pane(a, b)` | Local-only until daemon adds `SwapPane` variant (file discovered-from issue if needed) |

For each daemon-bound op:
- Use the pane identity mapping from tn-pgz6 (daemon-pane-id lookup)
- Wrap with `tokio::time::timeout(Duration::from_secs(5), ...)`
- On daemon error: log loud, rollback local layout mutation (do NOT let GUI and daemon diverge silently)

Flip the default `AttachMode` from `Local` to `Remote` in `crates/therminal-core/src/config/mod.rs`. Keep `Local` as escape hatch.

Update top-level `CLAUDE.md` status line: "GUI is now a daemon client by default".

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/pane_ops.rs (all 8+ spawn call sites identified in explorer audit)
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/init.rs (startup flow, attach_mode default)
- crates/therminal-core/src/config/mod.rs (flip `#[default]` on `AttachMode` enum)
- crates/therminal-protocol/src/daemon.rs (no changes expected — all needed variants exist)
- CLAUDE.md (root)

## Acceptance
- MCP `terminal.panes.write` into a visible pane → bytes appear in GUI window
- MCP `terminal.panes.create` → new visible pane appears in GUI
- Persisted daemon sessions visible to user after daemon restart
- `mcp.attach_mode = local` escape hatch still works (local-spawn path unchanged)
- CI green Linux + Windows
- Manual: split 3 ways, close middle pane, re-split, close all — no divergence between GUI and `terminal.panes.list`

## When Done
Close with retro on any discovered daemon gaps (missing ops, race conditions, etc.) and a summary of the rollback strategy used on IPC failures.
notes:
## Retro

**Call sites touched:** 5 entry points in pane_ops.rs (split_focused_pane, split_pane_by_id, close_focused_pane, close_pane_by_id) + set_focused_pane in window/mod.rs. Deliberately deferred: switch_workspace, send_to_workspace, restore_layout, poll_auto_tile, spawn_subagent_pane, swap_focused_pane — these still go local-only.

**Branching structure:** early-return on `is_daemon_mode()` inside each call site, dispatching to a new `split_pane_remote` / `kill_pane_remote` helper that owns RPC + layout mount. The helpers themselves are the single point of truth for the daemon pattern: `daemon_rpc_blocking` centralises the stored-Handle + timeout + error-translation dance in one place so future cutover of the deferred call sites is a one-liner. Local paths unchanged — no code deleted, just skipped via early-return.

**Daemon RPC gaps discovered:**
- No SwapPane variant (tn-52h8 filed)
- No way to create a pane in a *different* workspace — the daemon has SplitPane (same-session) and CreateSession (fresh), but no SplitInto { workspace } nor MovePane. switch_workspace/send_to_workspace blocked on tn-fi1k until that gap is designed.
- KillPane on an already-dead pane returns Error; close_pane_by_id tolerates this (PaneExited event path) by ignoring the error entirely, but close_focused_pane treats it as a real failure. Consider a dedicated NotFound variant or `force: bool` field.

**AttachMode::default() flip:** no existing test broke. The test suite doesn't exercise the init_gpu attach fallback in a headless way, so this is only covered by manual smoke test. Noting this as test-coverage debt.

**Next smoke test I'd run:**
1. `therminal-daemon` + `therminal` → expect attach or fresh CreateSession
2. Ctrl+Shift+D (vsplit) → verify SplitPane RPC (`strace` / daemon logs) and visible new pane
3. Ctrl+Shift+W (close) → verify KillPane RPC
4. Restart GUI (daemon stays) → verify both panes reappear in same layout
5. From Claude Code: `mcp__therminal__terminal.panes.create` → expect visible new pane
6. `mcp__therminal__terminal.panes.list` → expect focused_pane matches what GUI shows after clicking around
close_reason: Phase B UI cutover landed: split / close / focus in pane_ops.rs + set_focused_pane route through IpcRequest::SplitPane / KillPane / SelectPane when is_daemon_mode() is true. AttachMode::default flipped to Remote (Local retained as escape hatch). New split_pane_remote / kill_pane_remote / select_pane_remote helpers on App with central daemon_rpc_blocking (stored runtime handle + 5s timeout + rollback on failure). Root CLAUDE.md status updated. Follow-ups filed: tn-52h8 (SwapPane protocol gap), tn-fi1k (workspace fresh-pane paths), tn-ll6l (auto_tile + swarm). Committed e247201, pushed to origin/main. CI green.

title: Wire SetWorkspaceState sync: publish GUI layout to daemon on every topology change
description:
## Context
Codex review of tn-382v on 2026-04-07 found that `WorkspaceManager::workspace_info()` at `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/workspace.rs:353` already builds faithful `Vec<WorkspaceInfo>` snapshots — including `layout: Option<LayoutSnapshot>` — but the method is `#[allow(dead_code)]` and only used by unit tests. The daemon's `SetWorkspaceState` / `GetWorkspaces` IPC surface is fully implemented (`server.rs:560-583`). The plumbing exists on both ends; nothing drives it.

Wiring this early is a prerequisite for tn-ytw2 Phase A: without it, the daemon has no layout snapshots stored, so the attach flow has nothing to load. It also makes the daemon the source of truth for workspace topology, which reduces restore complexity later.

## Task
1. Identify every call site in `window/pane_ops.rs` that mutates workspace topology (split, close, swap, workspace create/destroy/rename, tab switch, focus change). Codex pointed at pane_ops.rs:117 and pane_ops.rs:241 as examples.
2. After each such mutation, call a new helper `App::publish_workspace_state()` that:
   - Calls `WorkspaceManager::workspace_info()` to build the snapshot
   - Sends `IpcRequest::SetWorkspaceState { session_id, workspaces, active_workspace }` via the stored daemon client
   - Logs + swallows errors (this must not block UI ops; daemon may be temporarily unreachable)
3. Remove the `#[allow(dead_code)]` attributes on `workspace_info()` (line 352) and the related field comments referencing it at line 25.
4. Debounce if needed: if rapid mutations trigger too many sends, coalesce over a short window (e.g. 100ms). Likely unnecessary for MVP — revisit only if logs show contention.
5. Gate the publish on `mcp.attach_mode == Remote` OR `daemon_client.is_some()` so local-mode users don't pay for IPC they're not using.

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/pane_ops.rs (all topology mutation sites)
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/mod.rs (add `publish_workspace_state()` helper)
- crates/therminal-app/src/pane/workspace.rs (remove dead_code attrs)
- crates/therminal-protocol/src/daemon.rs (`IpcRequest::SetWorkspaceState` already exists)
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/server.rs (handler already exists at :560)

## Acceptance
- Every topology mutation in the GUI triggers `SetWorkspaceState` to the daemon (when daemon connected)
- `workspace_info()` no longer marked dead_code
- Manual: launch GUI, split a pane, run `bd` equivalent of `terminal.workspaces.list` via MCP — sees the new pane in the active workspace
- Manual: launch GUI without daemon running — no panics, no error spam, topology ops still work locally
- CI green
- Integration test that publishes a known topology and asserts the daemon stored it (query via `GetWorkspaces`)

## When Done
Close with note on whether debouncing was needed and how many call sites were wired.
notes:
## Retro
- A parallel agent landed most of this work in the same window — by the time I pulled state, mod.rs/init.rs/pane_ops.rs already had publish_workspace_state, daemon_session_id field, and 8 mutation-site call sites (split, close, swap, switch, send-to-workspace, close-all, etc). tn-pgz6's PaneIdMap also landed in parallel, so the daemon_for_local() helper was available.
- Remaining work I added: (1) wired commit_rename in event_handler.rs to publish on workspace rename, (2) removed the two #[allow(dead_code)] attrs on workspace_info() and Workspace.name (these would now fail clippy because the function is live).
- Mutation call sites wired: 9 total (8 from parallel agent + 1 rename from me).
- Debouncing: not needed in MVP per task spec; no follow-up filed.
- tn-pgz6 helper status: PaneIdMap landed before I finished — used pane_id_map.daemon_for_local() in the publish helper for local→daemon translation, no TODO needed.
- Manual smoke not run; helper early-returns when daemon_client.is_none() or daemon_session_id.is_none(), so local-mode users pay nothing.
close_reason: publish_workspace_state wired across all topology mutation sites (split/close/swap/switch/send-to-workspace/close-all/rename); workspace_info() no longer dead_code; CI green. Bulk landed via parallel agent, finished by adding rename publish + dead_code removal.

title: Pane identity strategy: explicit daemon↔local PaneId mapping in remote mode
description:
## Context
Codex review of tn-382v on 2026-04-07 flagged this as a hidden correctness risk: the GUI has its own monotonic `PaneId` counter (`pane/spawn.rs:next_pane_id()`) and the daemon has its own separate counter. In remote mode today (`pane/remote_spawn.rs:93`), the proof-of-life scaffolding just sets `remote_pane_id = session_id` and hopes it works. The initial remote pane's `on_exit` callback at `window/init.rs:376-380` can't target its own local pane id — it sends a generic redraw instead — because the mapping is implicit and leaks into callback awkwardness.

This must be resolved **before** tn-ytw2 Phase A does any real attach work. Pick one of two approaches and commit to it:

**Option A**: In remote mode, local `PaneId` = daemon `PaneId`. GUI's local counter is replaced by "whatever the daemon assigns". Simpler, but couples GUI pane identity to daemon state and breaks if the daemon churns.

**Option B**: First-class mapping table `HashMap<LocalPaneId, DaemonPaneId>` on the app state (plus reverse map). All IPC calls go through the mapping; all UI ops use local IDs. More code but preserves isolation and allows local-mode to keep its own ID space.

## Task
1. Decide between A and B (B is safer; Codex recommendation agrees).
2. If B: add `pane_id_map: HashMap<PaneId, therminal_protocol::PaneId>` (+ reverse) to `App` state in `window/mod.rs`. Populate it in `spawn_remote_pane` and anywhere else a remote pane is created. Expose `App::daemon_pane_id(local: PaneId) -> Option<DaemonPaneId>` and the reverse helper.
3. Rewrite `spawn_remote_pane` to stop conflating `session_id` with `remote_pane_id`. After `CreateSession`, call `GetWorkspaces` (or a follow-up discovery call) to obtain the actual pane id the daemon assigned.
4. Fix `on_exit` callback in init.rs:376-380 so it can close its own pane via the mapping. Remove the "redraw only" fallback.
5. Ensure all existing remote IPC call sites (currently just `IpcRequest::ResizePane` in `pane/backend.rs:173`) go through the mapping.

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/mod.rs (App state)
- crates/therminal-app/src/pane/remote_spawn.rs (remove scaffolding)
- crates/therminal-app/src/pane/backend.rs (resize IPC uses mapping)
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/init.rs (on_exit fix)
- crates/therminal-app/src/pane/state.rs (may need DaemonPaneId on PaneState)

## Acceptance
- `let remote_pane_id = session_id;` hack at remote_spawn.rs:93 is gone
- All daemon-bound IPC calls route through the mapping helper, not by guessing
- `on_exit` callback closes its own pane (no more "redraw only" comment at init.rs:376)
- CI green
- Unit test covering map population + lookup in both directions

## When Done
Close with a brief retro of which option was chosen and why.
notes:
## Retro
- Chose Option B (explicit mapping table) over Option A (collapsing the two id spaces). Reason: keeps local-mode panes untouched (zero overhead, no behavior change), preserves the GUI's monotonic id invariant the layout tree relies on, and survives a daemon restart that would churn the daemon-side counter without invalidating local references. Option A would have coupled GUI focus/layout state to daemon allocator quirks for marginal code savings.
- spawn_remote_pane now takes the local_id as an argument so the on_exit callback can capture it before the spawn returns. Real daemon pane id is discovered via GetWorkspaces immediately after CreateSession; both RPCs are wrapped in 5s tokio::time::timeout to avoid freezing startup if the daemon is slow.
- backend.rs RemotePty.pane_id already stores the daemon id directly, so SendKeys/ResizePane were already routing through the right id — no change needed there. The mapping is only consulted by App-level code (e.g. publish_workspace_state translating local→daemon pane ids).
- Unit tests cover insert/lookup/remove in both directions and nonexistent-id None.
- What was unclear: a parallel auto-edit on the same file added daemon_session_id and publish_workspace_state mid-session, which collided with my edits and caused one fmt cycle. Worked around by re-running cargo fmt.
close_reason: Replaced session_id-as-pane-id hack with explicit PaneIdMap; spawn_remote_pane now discovers the real daemon pane id via GetWorkspaces (5s timeout), local on_exit callback fires UserEvent::PaneExited with the correct local id, mapping is cleaned up in close_pane_by_id. CI green, unit tests added.
