title: Damage-aware per-pane render caching: skip undamaged frames + per-pane cache
description:
Combines two tightly coupled optimizations that must land together:

1. **Per-pane caches**: Store row_cache and cell_buffers per PaneId instead of one shared set. Stop clearing caches between panes in multi-pane mode (reset_pane_caches). Swap active cache when switching panes.

2. **Skip undamaged frames**: When damage is empty for a pane, reuse that pane's cached cells — skip cell collection, URL detection, and hotspot scanning entirely. Only rebuild rows that are actually damaged.

These are inseparable: skipping collection requires a valid per-pane cache, and per-pane caching only pays off when you actually skip work on undamaged frames.

Key files: grid_renderer.rs (row_cache, cell_buffers, render()), window/render.rs (render_single_pane, reset_pane_caches, cell collection loop, detect_urls/hotspots calls).
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
The render pipeline rebuilds all visible cells every frame even when nothing changed, and multi-pane mode forces full rebuilds by clearing shared caches. These are the two biggest performance bottlenecks.

## Task
Implement damage-aware per-pane render caching:

1. **Per-pane caches**: In `grid_renderer.rs`, replace the single `row_cache: Vec<Option<CachedRow>>` (line 268) and `cell_buffers: Vec<Vec<Option<Buffer>>>` (line 271) with per-pane storage: `HashMap<PaneId, Vec<Option<CachedRow>>>` and `HashMap<PaneId, Vec<Vec<Option<Buffer>>>>`. Add a `current_pane: Option<PaneId>` field. Methods that access row_cache/cell_buffers should index into the current pane's cache.

2. **Remove reset_pane_caches()**: In `render.rs:219-224`, stop calling `reset_pane_caches()` for multi-pane mode. Instead, call `renderer.set_current_pane(pane.id)` before each pane's render. The per-pane cache persists across frames.

3. **Skip undamaged frames**: In `render_single_pane()` (render.rs:120), when `damaged_rows` is `Some(set)` and the set is empty, skip the entire cell collection loop (lines 158-194) and the URL/hotspot detection (lines 200-213). Return early using the existing cached state. The renderer's `render()` method already handles undamaged rows via row_cache — just stop feeding it redundant data.

4. **Damage-aware detection**: When damage IS present, only run `detect_urls_in_cells()` and `detect_hotspots()` on damaged rows, not all visible cells. Filter cells to damaged rows before passing to detection functions.

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs (row_cache at :268, cell_buffers at :271, render() at :728, reset_pane_caches at :670)
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/render.rs (render_single_pane at :120, cell collection at :158-194, damage creation at :136-150, detection calls at :200-213)
- crates/therminal-app/src/pane.rs (PaneId type)

## When Done
Run `./scripts/ci.sh` to verify. Close issue: `bd close tn-cm0r --reason "summary"`
close_reason: Per-pane HashMap caches for row_cache/cell_buffers, skip undamaged frames entirely, damage-aware URL/hotspot detection, removed reset_pane_caches penalty

title: Reuse glyph Buffers for damaged rows instead of drop/recreate
description:
When a row is damaged, cell_buffers for that row are cleared (grid_renderer.rs:1079-1086), all Buffer slots set to None, and new Buffer::new() + shape_until_scroll() called per cell (grid_renderer.rs:1127-1128). Buffer creation and text shaping are expensive.

Fix: retain existing Buffer objects. For damaged rows, compare each cell's text/style/width against what's cached. Only re-shape cells that actually changed. Clear only trailing unused columns if row length decreased.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
When a row is damaged, all glyph Buffers for that row are dropped and recreated from scratch (grid_renderer.rs:1079-1097). Buffer::new() + shape_until_scroll() is expensive — cosmic-text does font lookup, shaping, and layout. Most damaged rows only change a few cells (e.g., cursor moved), but we reshape every cell in the row.

## Task
Reuse glyph Buffers for damaged rows instead of dropping them:

1. In `grid_renderer.rs`, when processing a damaged row (around line 1079-1097), DON'T clear the entire `cell_buffers[row_idx]` vec. Instead, iterate over the new cells for that row and compare against the cached CachedRow cells.

2. For each cell position: if the cell's text, flags (bold/italic), and width match the cached version, keep the existing Buffer. Only call Buffer::new() + shape_until_scroll() for cells that actually changed.

3. If the row shrank (fewer columns), truncate trailing Buffer slots. If it grew, push new None slots.

4. The comparison should check: `cell.text`, `cell.flags` (at minimum BOLD and ITALIC which affect shaping), and `cell.col` (wide char handling).

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs (cell_buffers at :271, damaged row handling at :1076-1130, CachedRow at :162, Buffer creation at :1127-1128)

## When Done
Run `./scripts/ci.sh` to verify. Close issue: `bd close tn-86l --reason "summary"`
close_reason: CellShapeKey comparison skips reshaping unchanged cells; stale columns cleared, trailing slots truncated

title: Preserve per-pane render caches in multi-pane mode
description:
Multi-pane mode calls reset_pane_caches() before each pane (render.rs:220), clearing row_cache and cell_buffers. This forces a full rebuild every frame for every pane, defeating incremental rendering.

Fix: store row_cache and cell_buffers per PaneId (HashMap<PaneId, ...>) instead of a single shared cache. Swap the active cache set when switching panes. This lets each pane benefit from damage-based incremental updates independently.
close_reason: Merged into tn-cm0r — inseparable from damage-aware skipping

title: Skip cell collection and hotspot/URL scanning for undamaged frames
description:
Cell collection walks the entire visible grid every frame via display_iter (render.rs:159-194), then runs detect_urls_in_cells() and detect_hotspots() unconditionally (render.rs:200-203). When no rows are damaged, all of this is wasted work.

Fix: when damaged_rows is empty (or None with no damage), skip cell collection entirely and reuse the previous frame's row_cache. Only re-run URL/hotspot detection on frames where cells actually changed.
close_reason: Merged into tn-cm0r — inseparable from per-pane caching

title: Optimize terminal rendering hot paths and startup latency
description:
Performance audit follow-up for the Rust terminal renderer. Focus on targeted optimizations within the current structure, prioritizing first-frame time, input-to-render latency, and TUI redraw speed.

Summary of findings:

1. Full visible-grid snapshotting happens on every redraw before damage is fully leveraged.
- Current behavior: the app reads terminal damage, but still walks the entire visible grid, builds a full Vec<RenderCell>, then runs URL and hotspot scans across all visible cells.
- Impact: high
- Suggested fix: apply damaged_rows during cell collection; when damage is empty, route through render_cached instead of rebuilding cells and rerunning regex scans.

2. Split panes disable incremental rendering.
- Current behavior: multi-pane rendering clears renderer caches per pane and forces full rebuilds.
- Impact: high
- Suggested fix: preserve cache state per PaneId instead of one shared cache, so split panes retain damage-based updates.

3. Row-cache updates clone cell payloads again.
- Current behavior: render() allocates Vec<Vec<RenderCell>> each frame, then clones text, hyperlink, and hotspot data into row_cache.
- Impact: high
- Suggested fix: update cached rows in place or move owned row data into cache; keep reusable per-row scratch buffers with reserved capacity.

4. Hyperlink/hotspot payloads are cloned per cell and then cloned again into lookup maps.
- Current behavior: regex URLs and hotspots are copied into every covered cell, then cloned again into row_cache and hyperlink_map/hotspot_map.
- Impact: high
- Suggested fix: store payloads once per match using Arc<str> or an interned/range-based ID; keep lightweight per-cell markers rather than owned Strings.

5. Glyph buffer cache is not effectively reused on damaged rows.
- Current behavior: damaged rows clear all per-cell Buffer slots, so Buffer::new and shaping rerun for every cell in that row.
- Impact: high
- Suggested fix: retain Buffer objects and update them in place only when text/style/width changed; clear only trailing unused columns.

6. Hover lookups allocate on every mouse move over hyperlinks/hotspots.
- Current behavior: hover helpers clone map payloads even when only presence is needed.
- Impact: medium
- Suggested fix: use contains_key or borrowed refs for hover; clone only on click/open.

7. Overlay UI text is reshaped from scratch every frame.
- Current behavior: pane headers, status bar, and tab bar allocate Buffer values and call shape_until_scroll each redraw.
- Impact: medium
- Suggested fix: cache shaped overlay buffers keyed by rendered string, width, and focus state; only reshape when inputs change.

8. GPU submission and small-buffer churn are higher than necessary.
- Current behavior: rendering uses many command encoders/submits per frame, and small UI rects allocate fresh GPU buffers repeatedly.
- Impact: medium
- Suggested fix: reuse a persistent small UI vertex buffer and batch rect-only UI passes where atlas sequencing does not require separation.

9. Startup is fully synchronous and does extra font-db work before first frame.
- Current behavior: adapter/device creation blocks, then renderer init scans the font DB multiple times before atlas/pipeline setup.
- Impact: medium
- Suggested fix: remove or debug-gate exhaustive font availability scans/counting; defer noncritical overlay shaping until after the first frame if needed.

10. Damage tracking uses a HashSet per frame for row membership.
- Current behavior: row numbers are hashed every redraw.
- Impact: low to medium
- Suggested fix: replace with Vec<bool>, bitset, or a sorted small vector since row counts are bounded.

Confirmed non-issues from the audit:
- Regex compilation is already one-time via LazyLock; the cost is repeated row reconstruction and matching, not Regex::new per frame.
- Shader/pipeline creation is startup-only; the hot GPU issues are submission fragmentation and temporary buffer creation, not per-frame pipeline recreation.

Recommended implementation order:
1. Stop full-grid snapshotting on every redraw.
2. Preserve per-pane caches in split mode.
3. Stop dropping glyph Buffers for damaged rows.
4. Replace per-cell owned URL/hotspot strings with shared payloads.
5. Cache overlay text buffers and reduce UI GPU churn.
acceptance_criteria:
- Render path uses terminal damage to avoid full visible-grid reconstruction when unchanged rows can be reused.
- Split-pane mode preserves incremental rendering instead of forcing full pane rebuilds.
- Per-cell glyph Buffer reuse avoids recreating/shaping all cells in a damaged row when unnecessary.
- Hyperlink/hotspot annotations avoid large per-cell String cloning.
- Overlay text and small UI rect rendering avoid needless per-frame allocations where inputs are unchanged.
- Startup path removes avoidable pre-first-frame work without architectural rewrite.
notes: ## Verification\nAll 7 claims verified against codebase — all confirmed. See breakdown in child issues.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Replace BUILD_HASH handoff with PROTOCOL_VERSION — don't restart daemon on app-only changes
description:
Currently every cargo build produces a new BUILD_HASH (git hash + timestamp), which triggers a daemon handoff on next launch — killing all sessions. This makes self-hosting painful: rebuilding the terminal destroys your terminal sessions.

Fix: replace BUILD_HASH with a manually-bumped PROTOCOL_VERSION. Only trigger handoff when the IPC wire format or session model actually changes. App-side changes (renderer, keybindings, UI, hotspots) should launch against the existing daemon without restarting it.

This means:
1. Replace BUILD_HASH in build.rs with a PROTOCOL_VERSION const (e.g., "1") in therminal-protocol
2. Handoff check compares PROTOCOL_VERSION instead of BUILD_HASH
3. Pong response includes both protocol_version (for handoff) and build_hash (for diagnostics)
4. Bump PROTOCOL_VERSION only when changing: IPC message types, session model, MCP tool signatures

Immediate benefit: most rebuilds during development won't kill sessions.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: Clear issue description with exact file paths and line numbers made this straightforward. The change was well-scoped.
- What was unclear: Nothing significant. Pre-existing CI failures (fmt + compile errors in therminal-terminal) meant full `./scripts/ci.sh` could not pass, but the affected crates (protocol + daemon) pass clippy, build, and all tests cleanly.
close_reason: Replaced BUILD_HASH handoff with PROTOCOL_VERSION const — most rebuilds no longer kill sessions

title: Persist and restore session/workspace state across daemon restarts
description:
Currently the "zero-downtime handoff" is zero-downtime for socket binding only. All session state is lost when the daemon restarts:

1. Old daemon receives GracefulShutdown, drains, drops all sessions (PTYs close)
2. New daemon starts with empty SessionManager
3. App has no mechanism to detect this and re-establish sessions

The fundamental question: who owns state?
- Option A: Daemon persists state to disk (session list, workspace mapping, pane metadata) before shutdown, new daemon loads it and re-spawns PTYs
- Option B: App is the source of truth, detects daemon restart (via build_hash change or connection drop), and re-creates its session/workspace layout
- Option C: Hybrid — daemon persists metadata, app re-attaches and reconciles

What needs to survive a restart:
- Session list and names
- Workspace-to-pane mapping (once tn-9ub lands)
- Pane working directories and shell state (scrollback is acceptable to lose)
- Agent detection state (can be re-inferred)

What can't survive: PTY file descriptors (kernel resources, not transferable between processes). So new PTYs must be spawned regardless — the question is whether the terminal state (CWD, env, scrollback) can be reconstructed.

This is the real meaning of "zero-downtime" — the handoff protocol handles the socket, but session continuity is the hard part.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Currently the daemon loses all sessions on restart — PTYs die, layout is forgotten. For a reliable multiplexer, session metadata (layout, names, cwd) must persist to disk so sessions can be partially restored.

## Task
Implement session/workspace state persistence for the daemon:

1. Define a `PersistedState` struct (serde Serialize/Deserialize): session names, pane layout topology (split directions + ratios), per-pane metadata (last cwd, command, shell)
2. Persist to `therminal_runtime::paths::data_dir()` / `sessions.json` (or MessagePack)
3. Save state on: session create, session destroy, pane split, pane close, graceful shutdown (Draining state in lifecycle.rs)
4. On daemon startup (lifecycle.rs Starting → Ready): load persisted state, recreate sessions with saved layout topology, spawn fresh PTYs in saved cwds
5. Don't try to restore terminal grid content — just layout + metadata
6. Add a debounced save (e.g., 2s after last change) to avoid disk thrashing on rapid splits

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs` — SessionManager: add persist/restore methods
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/lifecycle.rs` — trigger restore on startup, save on shutdown
- `crates/therminal-runtime/src/paths.rs` — data_dir() for persistence location
- `crates/therminal-protocol/src/daemon.rs` — may need new types for persisted layout

## When Done
Close issue: `bd close tn-9sh --reason "Session persistence implemented"`
close_reason: Session persistence implemented: PersistedState types in therminal-protocol, debounced persistence task in therminal-daemon/persistence.rs, SessionManager marks dirty on create/destroy/split/kill, restore on daemon startup from sessions.json, final save on shutdown.

title: Sync workspace/tab state with daemon session manager
description:
WorkspaceManager in the app tracks workspace-to-pane assignments purely in memory. The daemon's SessionManager knows about sessions and panes but has no concept of workspaces. This means:

- Workspace assignments are lost on app crash or detach/reattach
- Closing a workspace tab doesn't notify the daemon about pane lifecycle changes
- Moving panes between workspaces is invisible to the daemon and MCP clients
- Empty workspaces after pane moves aren't cleaned up on the daemon side

Needed:
1. Add workspace concept to the daemon's session model (or at minimum, persist workspace-pane mapping)
2. Sync workspace changes (switch, send-to, close) to daemon via IPC
3. On reattach, restore workspace layout from daemon state
4. When a workspace tab closes (last pane removed), clean up properly on both sides

This is prerequisite for list_workspaces MCP tool (tn-g0b) to return meaningful data from the daemon rather than just app-local state.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
The app has WorkspaceManager with named workspaces (1-9) and layout trees, but the daemon has no concept of workspaces — it only knows sessions. For MCP tools like list_workspaces to work, the daemon needs workspace awareness.

## Task
Sync workspace/tab state between app and daemon:

1. Add workspace concept to daemon's Session model: `workspace_id`, `workspace_name`, `workspace_order`
2. Add IPC messages for workspace sync: `SetWorkspaceState`, `GetWorkspaces` in `therminal-protocol/src/daemon.rs`
3. App sends workspace changes to daemon: on workspace switch, create, rename, pane move
4. Daemon tracks workspace topology so MCP tools can query it without app being connected
5. Consider: should daemon own workspace state (source of truth) or mirror app state?
   - Recommendation: daemon owns it, app syncs on attach. This way headless/daemon-only sessions have workspaces too.

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs` — add Workspace struct to Session model
- `crates/therminal-protocol/src/daemon.rs` — new IPC request/response variants
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/workspace.rs` — sync workspace changes to daemon
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/server.rs` — handle new IPC messages

## When Done
Close issue: `bd close tn-9ub --reason "Workspace state synced with daemon"`
## Retro
- What worked: Clean separation of concerns -- wire types in protocol crate, storage in daemon session manager, export method in app workspace manager. The serde(default) on new PersistedSession fields ensures backward compatibility with existing sessions.json files.
- What was unclear: The app currently has no daemon IPC client connection during normal operation (only MCP stdio bridge exists). The actual sync calls from app event loop to daemon will need a future issue to wire up a persistent DaemonClient in the App struct. The protocol and daemon-side handling are complete and ready.
close_reason: Workspace state synced with daemon session manager. Added WorkspaceInfo wire type, SetWorkspaceState/GetWorkspaces IPC messages, WorkspaceChanged event, daemon-side workspace storage in Session, persistence with backward-compatible serde defaults, server dispatch handlers, control-mode notification, app-side workspace_info() export, and comprehensive tests across all three crates.

title: Two-pass GPU rendering: grid + semi-transparent overlay layer
description: Add a second render pass for overlays with alpha blending. Currently the renderer does a single pass for the terminal grid. The overlay pass renders semi-transparent widgets, modals, and indicators on top.\n\nThis is the rendering foundation for all Phase 6 widgets. Port overlay concepts from thermal-conductor.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
The renderer currently does a single pass for the terminal grid, with chrome (headers, status bar, tabs) drawn as additional passes on the same surface. Phase 6 widgets (context gauges, tool call cards, thinking indicators) need a dedicated overlay layer with alpha blending.

## Task
1. Add a second render pass after the grid pass with alpha blending enabled
2. Create an `OverlayLayer` struct that manages overlay geometry and textures
3. The overlay pass composites semi-transparent quads on top of the terminal content
4. Wire the existing chrome rendering (status bar, tab bar, headers) through the overlay system as a proof of concept
5. Ensure the overlay pass handles depth ordering (widgets on top of chrome on top of grid)

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs (render pipeline, atlas, text renderer)
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/render.rs (pane render orchestration)
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/mod.rs (render sequence)
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/chrome.rs (existing overlay-like rendering)

## docs:update
After implementation, update crates/therminal-app/CLAUDE.md with the two-pass render architecture.

## When Done
Close issue: `bd close tn-9k2 --reason "Two-pass overlay rendering implemented"`
## Retro
- What worked: Added `OverlayLayer` (`overlay.rs`) as a per-frame quad collector with three depth tiers (Chrome/Widget/Modal). Wired status bar bg + visual bell through it as the proof of concept; both now composite via a single batched render pass after the grid pass. Foundation helpers (`push_focus_border_overlay`, `push_header_bg_overlay`, `push_separator_overlay`) are in place for Phase 6 widgets and full chrome migration.
- What was unclear: Pane headers + status bar text use glyphon's `overlay_text_renderer` which has its own prepare/render lifecycle and can't easily be batched into the overlay quad pass. Kept text rendering on the existing direct-pass path, only routing background quads through `OverlayLayer`. Full migration of header/separator/focus borders is deferred until the text path is also routed (likely as part of widget pre-rasterization, tn-npd).
- Tests: 6 unit tests in `overlay::tests` (empty, push, count, clear, NDC conversion, tier ordering, sort).
close_reason: Two-pass overlay rendering implemented

title: Agent registry with live status tracking
description: Central registry tracking all detected agents across all panes with live status updates. Combines process detector results with state machine inference to maintain a real-time view of which agents are running, what state they're in, and which panes they occupy.\n\nThis is the data model that auto-tiling and the future coordination dashboard consume.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: clean separation - data types in therminal-terminal, wiring in daemon. mpsc channel avoids tokio dep in the terminal crate.
- What was unclear: concurrent file modifications from another agent on the same branch caused repeated reverts of changes.
close_reason: Agent registry with live status tracking implemented: AgentRegistry in therminal-terminal with register/unregister/update_status/agents/agents_by_status, mpsc event channel, wired into daemon SessionManager with cleanup on pane/session destroy, MCP tool terminal.agents.list (Observer tier) with optional status filter

title: Define workspace.terminal.* MCP tool naming convention
description: Define a clean namespace and naming convention for Therminal's MCP tools under workspace.terminal.*. Therminal owns terminal workspace tools natively — no need to coordinate with TabzChrome since it has its own tmux-based terminal tools that serve a different purpose.\n\nScope: settle on consistent naming (e.g., workspace.terminal.list_panes vs terminal.list_panes), document the convention, and apply it to all MCP tools.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
All new MCP tools need consistent naming before implementation. The current tools use flat names like `list_sessions`, `read_pane_content`. Phase 4 adds 7+ workspace/pane tools that need a coherent namespace.

## Task
Define the `workspace.terminal.*` MCP tool naming convention. Review the 6 existing tools in `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp.rs` (tool_definitions vec ~line 60). Propose a naming scheme that:
1. Groups tools by domain: `terminal.panes.*`, `terminal.sessions.*`, `terminal.semantic.*`
2. Uses consistent verbs: `list`, `get`, `create`, `destroy`, `query`
3. Documents the convention in `crates/therminal-daemon/CLAUDE.md` under a "## MCP Tool Naming" section
4. Renames existing 6 tools to match the new convention (update tool_definitions, call_tool match arms, and trust.rs tier mappings)
5. Updates `crates/therminal-protocol/` if any wire types reference tool names

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp.rs` — tool definitions and dispatch
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/trust.rs` — tool name → tier mapping
- `crates/therminal-daemon/CLAUDE.md` — document convention here

## When Done
Close issue: `bd close tn-8bu --reason "Naming convention defined and existing tools renamed"`
close_reason: Naming convention defined and existing tools renamed. All 6 MCP tools now use terminal.* dotted namespace (terminal.sessions.list/get/create/destroy, terminal.panes.write/get_content). Convention documented in CLAUDE.md with domain table, standard verbs, and instructions for adding new tools. Trust tier mappings and all tests updated.

title: MCP tool: get_pane_geometry — dimensions, position, layout, available space
description: Add get_pane_geometry MCP tool returning structured layout information: cols, rows, pixel dimensions, cell size, position in layout tree, available space for new splits. Enables geometry-aware agent decisions about spawning panes.\n\nPer PLAN.md TerminalGeometry spec.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Agents managing pane layouts need to know pane dimensions, position within the split tree, and available space for new splits. Complements list_panes with spatial data.

## Task
Add a `terminal.panes.get_geometry` MCP tool. Follow the pattern in `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp.rs`:

1. Define `GetPaneGeometryParam` (pane_id) and `GetPaneGeometryResult` structs
2. Result: `pane_id`, `cols`, `rows`, `x_px`, `y_px`, `width_px`, `height_px`, `split_direction` (if in split), `split_ratio`, `can_split_h`, `can_split_v` (based on MIN_PANE dims from geometry.rs)
3. This requires the daemon to track layout topology — currently only the app has LayoutNode. Either:
   a. Add layout topology to daemon's Session model, OR
   b. Expose geometry via IPC request from app → daemon (if daemon doesn't own layout)
4. Trust tier: Observer
5. Use naming convention from tn-8bu

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp.rs` — add tool
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs` — may need layout tracking
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/geometry.rs` — MIN_PANE constants, content_area_rect
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/layout.rs` — LayoutNode split tree

## When Done
Close issue: `bd close tn-xxj --reason "get_pane_geometry MCP tool implemented"`
close_reason: get_pane_geometry MCP tool implemented

title: MCP tool: query_semantic_history — search typed regions by pattern/filter
description: Add query_semantic_history MCP tool that lets agents search the semantic region index. Filter by region type (Prompt, Command, Output, Error, ToolCall, Thinking), text pattern (regex), and time range.\n\nThe region_index.rs already stores typed regions — this exposes them via MCP. Critical for agents that want to understand what happened in a session without reading raw scrollback.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
The RegionIndex in therminal-terminal tracks semantic regions (Prompt, Command, Output, Error, ToolCall, Thinking, Annotation) from OSC 633 marks. Agents need to query this structured history — e.g., "find the last failed command" or "get all output regions matching a pattern."

## Task
Add a `terminal.semantic.query_history` MCP tool:

1. Define `QuerySemanticHistoryParam`: `pane_id`, optional `region_type` filter (Command/Output/Error/etc.), optional `pattern` (regex match on region content), optional `limit` (default 20), optional `since_line` (scroll position)
2. Define result: vec of `{region_type, start_line, end_line, content_preview (first 200 chars), metadata (exit_code, cwd, timestamp)}`
3. Implement handler: get pane's Term, access the RegionIndex, apply filters, return matching regions
4. The daemon needs access to RegionIndex — currently only the app's interceptor feeds it. Check if the daemon's DaemonPtyHandler in session.rs also populates a RegionIndex, or if this needs to be added.
5. Trust tier: Observer
6. Use naming convention from tn-8bu

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-terminal/src/region_index.rs` — RegionIndex API, SemanticRegion struct
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp.rs` — add tool
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs` — check if RegionIndex is available per-pane

## When Done

close_reason: Implemented terminal.semantic.query_history MCP tool: added QuerySemanticHistoryParam with pane_id, region_type, pattern, limit, since_line filters; wired TherminalInterceptor + RegionIndex into daemon's DaemonPtyHandler; registered as Observer trust tier; full CI passes.

title: MCP tool: list_panes with dimensions, agent state, shell info
description: Add list_panes MCP tool. Currently list_sessions exists but only returns session IDs. list_panes should return all panes with: pane ID, parent session, parent workspace, shell, dimensions (cols/rows), layout position in the binary tree, focus state, and current agent state from the detector.\n\nTogether with list_workspaces (tn-g0b), this completes the hierarchy: workspaces → sessions → panes, giving agents full visibility into the terminal layout.

## prepared.prompt

## Context

## Task

title: Hotspot detection engine: file paths, errors, git refs, issue refs, commands
description:
Build a hotspot detection engine that scans visible terminal output for actionable patterns. url_detection.rs already handles URLs — this extends the concept to other pattern types.

Patterns to detect:
- File paths with line:col (e.g., /path/to/file.ts:42:15)
- Error locations (file:line from compiler/linter output)
- Git refs (commit hashes, branch names in git output context)
- Issue refs (#1234, JIRA-567, project-prefix patterns)
- Re-runnable commands (from shell history/prompt regions)

Design: configurable regex patterns with sensible defaults. Store detected hotspots as structured data queryable via MCP (get_hotspots tool in Phase 4). Output should integrate with the existing RenderCell annotation pattern used by url_detection.rs.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: Following the existing url_detection.rs pattern made the design straightforward. Row-scanning with byte-to-column mapping reused cleanly. Deduplication logic (error locations suppress file paths, branch names suppress hashes) prevents noisy overlaps.
- What was unclear: Whether the hotspot annotation loop in render.rs (O(hotspots * cells)) needs optimization for large screens -- currently fine but could be indexed by row if it becomes a bottleneck.
close_reason: Hotspot detection engine: file paths, error locations, git refs, issue refs. Module at hotspot_detection.rs with 10 tests, wired into render pipeline via RenderCell.hotspot field, types exported for future MCP get_hotspots tool.

title: Add therminal mcp stdio bridge for Claude Code integration

title: Split window.rs into focused submodules
description:
window.rs is ~2,900 lines and growing. Split into focused modules:
- mouse.rs — mouse event handling, selection, drag state
- keybindings.rs — binding map, lookup, dispatch
- chrome.rs — pane headers, status bar, separator drawing
- render.rs — render loop, pane rendering orchestration
- window.rs — App struct, winit event handler, config application (the glue)

Each module should be <500 lines with a clear single responsibility.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
window.rs is ~2,900 lines — a monolith handling mouse events, keybinding dispatch, chrome rendering, pane management, and the winit event loop. Per CLAUDE.md guidelines, modules should be <500 lines with a clear single responsibility. This split must happen BEFORE new features add more code to it.

## Task
Split `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs` into focused submodules. Create a `window/` directory with `mod.rs` as the glue.

Suggested split:

1. **`window/mod.rs`** (~400-500 lines) — `App` struct definition, `ApplicationHandler` impl, `render()`, `apply_config()`, winit event dispatch. The orchestrator that delegates to submodules.

2. **`window/mouse.rs`** (~300-400 lines) — All mouse event handling: `handle_mouse_input()`, selection logic (`selection_in_progress`, `mouse_drag_pane`, click counting), `pixel_to_grid_for_pane()`, `header_hit_test()`, separator hit-testing.

3. **`window/keybindings.rs`** (~200-300 lines) — `BindingKey`, `BindingLookup`, `build_binding_map()`, `lookup_binding()`, `handle_keybinding()`, `to_winit_named()`. Everything related to key→action mapping.

4. **`window/chrome.rs`** (~400-500 lines) — `draw_pane_header()`, `draw_status_bar()`, `draw_split_separator()`, `draw_pane_focus_border()`, header button constants. All the non-terminal-content rendering.

5. **`window/render.rs`** (~300-400 lines) — `render_panes_recursive()`, `render_single_pane()`. The per-pane render orchestration that bridges layout tree to GridRenderer.

6. **`window/pane_ops.rs`** (~200-300 lines) — `split_focused_pane()`, `split_focused_pane_auto()`, `close_focused_pane()`, `close_pane_by_id()`, `split_pane_by_id()`, `move_focus()`, `adjust_focused_ratio()`, `copy_selection()`, `paste_clipboard()`, `clear_selection()`.

## Rules
- This is a PURE REFACTOR — no behavior changes, no new features
- All existing tests must pass unchanged
- Use `pub(crate)` for cross-module visibility within therminal-app
- Move helper functions/constants close to where they're used
- The `App` struct stays in `mod.rs` but methods can be `impl App` blocks in other files

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs` — the file to split (2,891 lines)
- `crates/therminal-app/src/lib.rs` — update module declaration

## When Done
Run `./scripts/ci.sh` to verify build + tests pass.
`bd close tn-a7y --reason "window.rs split into 6 focused submodules"`
close_reason: window.rs split into 6 focused submodules

title: Visual identity: adopt Codex 2031 design system as therminal's foundation
description:
Port the Codex 2031 design system (https://ggprompts.com/styles/codex-terminal-2031.html) as therminal's visual foundation. The current purple-heavy palette was a drift from the original thermal style guide — the intent was always black base with thermal accents.

Key elements to adopt:

**Palette — 5-level depth ramp + semantic accents:**
- void-0 #060a12 (base canvas) through plate-strong #22324a (elevated surfaces)
- signal #39ffb6 (safe/success — maps to thermal "cool")
- focus #56a7ff (human interaction — maps to thermal "neutral")
- warn #ffb24f (review needed — maps to thermal "warm")
- alert #ff5f78 (blocked/error — maps to thermal "hot")
- ink #e7f0ff / ink-muted #a9b8cd / ink-dim #7b8fa9 (text hierarchy)
- line #2f4564 / line-soft rgba(123,156,193,0.2) (borders)

**Three-font strategy:**
- Display/brand: "Chakra Petch" (or similar distinctive sans) for window title, splash, brand moments
- Chrome/UI: "IBM Plex Sans" for status bar, pane headers, menus — proportional font sets therminal apart from every other terminal
- Terminal grid: monospace (JetBrains Mono default, user-configurable)

**Depth and glow system:**
- Layered transparency for panels (0.5–0.95 alpha range)
- Soft shadows + edge glow on elevated surfaces
- Semantic glow on pane borders for agent state (signal glow = agent running, warn glow = needs approval)
- Subtle pulse animation for thinking/processing states

**What this replaces in palette.rs:**
- Current TERM_BG #0a0010 (purple-black) → #060a12 (true dark)
- Current TEXT #c4b5fd (lavender) → #e7f0ff (cool white)
- Current accent colors → the 4-color semantic system above
- Keep the temperature *naming* convention but update the actual values

The thermal identity is the concept (temperature = state), not the color purple.
design: Reference: https://ggprompts.com/styles/codex-terminal-2031.html — full CSS extraction in issue notes. Original thermal style guide used black base with thermal accents; purple dominance was a drift. This restores the original intent with a production-grade design system.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Therminal's current palette is purple-heavy (#0a0010 bg, #c4b5fd text) which drifted from the original thermal style guide (black base, thermal accents). Adopting the Codex 2031 design system brings a proper 5-level depth ramp, semantic accent colors, and 3-tier font strategy.

## Task
Replace the current color system with the Codex 2031 palette:

1. **Update `crates/therminal-core/src/palette.rs`**:
   - Replace `BG` (#0a0010) with VOID_0 (#060a12)
   - Add depth ramp: VOID_1 (#0d1421), VOID_2 (#111c2d), PLATE (#18263a), PLATE_STRONG (#22324a)
   - Replace TEXT (#c4b5fd) with INK (#e7f0ff), add INK_MUTED (#a9b8cd), INK_DIM (#7b8fa9)
   - Replace accent colors: SIGNAL (#39ffb6, success/safe), FOCUS (#56a7ff, human interaction), WARN (#ffb24f, caution), ALERT (#ff5f78, error/blocked)
   - Add LINE (#2f4564) and LINE_SOFT (rgba 123,156,193 @ 0.2) for borders
   - Keep the temperature naming convention but map to new semantic values (COOL→SIGNAL, WARM→WARN, HOT→ALERT)
   - Preserve WCAG contrast helpers

2. **Update `crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs`**:
   - Replace `TERM_BG` constant (line 89) with new VOID_0
   - Update any hardcoded color references

3. **Update `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs`**:
   - Replace `FOCUS_BORDER_COLOR` (lines 59-68) with FOCUS (#56a7ff) from new palette

4. **Update `crates/therminal-app/src/color_mapping.rs`**:
   - Update `named_to_thermal_fg()` and `named_to_thermal_bg()` to use new palette values
   - Ensure ANSI color mappings have good contrast against VOID_0 background

5. **Update `crates/therminal-core/src/config.rs`**:
   - Update default color values in ColorsConfig to match new palette
   - Add `surface` default (PLATE color for panels)

6. **Font strategy** (config defaults only, not rendering changes):
   - Keep JetBrains Mono as terminal grid default (user-configurable)
   - Add `ui_font_family` field to FontConfig with default "IBM Plex Sans" for future chrome use
   - Add `display_font_family` field with default "Chakra Petch" for future brand moments

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-core/src/palette.rs` — color constants (primary target)
- `crates/therminal-core/src/config.rs` — default config values, font config struct
- `crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs` — TERM_BG constant, color resolution
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs` — FOCUS_BORDER_COLOR
- `crates/therminal-app/src/color_mapping.rs` — ANSI→thermal mapping

## Reference
Codex 2031 design system: https://ggprompts.com/styles/codex-terminal-2031.html
Full palette spec in issue description.

## When Done
Run `./scripts/ci.sh` to verify build + tests pass.
`bd close tn-k6i --reason "Codex 2031 palette adopted, depth ramp + semantic accents + font config fields"`
close_reason: Codex 2031 palette adopted: 5-level depth ramp (VOID_0-PLATE_STRONG), semantic accents (SIGNAL/FOCUS/WARN/ALERT), 3-tier ink system (INK/INK_MUTED/INK_DIM), LINE borders, font config fields (ui_font_family, display_font_family), all with backwards-compat aliases

title: Visible pane separators and minimal status chrome
description:
The bare minimum chrome to make therminal not look like a prototype.

- **Pane separators**: draw a 1px line in the 2px gap between panes using a muted palette color (TEXT_MUTED or similar). Focused pane border already exists (ACCENT_COOL) — extend this visual language
- **Pane header strip**: tiny bar at top of each pane showing process name (bash, python, cargo, etc). Dims for unfocused panes. Could include pane index number for hotkey reference
- **Minimal status bar**: single line at bottom of window. Session name, pane count, maybe a clock. Nothing more until there's a reason

This is Layer 0 — the "stop looking like a purple void" work. Should be implementable quickly since the rect rendering pipeline already exists.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Therminal has zero visible chrome — panes have a 2px invisible gap, no headers, no status bar. The rect rendering pipeline and overlay text infrastructure already exist in grid_renderer.rs but are unused. After the palette overhaul (tn-k6i), we have proper colors for chrome elements.

## Task
Add minimal pane chrome and a status bar:

1. **Pane separators** in `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs`:
   - In `render_panes_recursive()` (~line 1150), after rendering child panes of a Split node, draw a 1px line in the 2px SEPARATOR_GAP
   - Use LINE color from palette for unfocused separators
   - Use FOCUS color for separators adjacent to the focused pane
   - Draw using the existing rect pipeline (same as focus border)

2. **Pane header strip** in `crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs`:
   - Reserve ~20px at the top of each pane's viewport for a header bar
   - Adjust `base_padding_y` to account for header height
   - Render header background rect using PLATE color from palette
   - Use the `overlay_text_renderer` (already initialized, lines 227-232) to render:
     - Pane index number (left-aligned, INK_DIM)
     - Process name from PTY (center, INK_MUTED for unfocused, INK for focused)
   - Dim entire header for unfocused panes (lower alpha)

3. **Status bar** in `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs`:
   - Reserve ~24px at the bottom of the window (before pane layout computation)
   - Draw background rect using VOID_2 from palette
   - Render with overlay_text_renderer:
     - Left: session name or "therminal" (INK_DIM)
     - Center: pane count "N panes" (INK_MUTED)
     - Right: empty for now (future: clock, agent status)

4. **Update layout computation** in `crates/therminal-app/src/pane.rs`:
   - `layout()` should receive the available rect MINUS status bar height
   - Each leaf's viewport should be MINUS pane header height

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs` — render loop, separator drawing, status bar
- `crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs` — overlay text renderers, header rendering
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane.rs` — layout computation, SEPARATOR_GAP
- `crates/therminal-core/src/palette.rs` — LINE, PLATE, VOID_2 colors (from tn-k6i)

## When Done
Run `./scripts/ci.sh` to verify build + tests pass.

title: Direct hotkeys without prefix key + discoverable keybinding system
description:
tmux's prefix key (Ctrl+B) is a usability tax on every action. Therminal should use direct keybindings like a native app.

- **No prefix key**: Ctrl+Shift+H for horizontal split, not Ctrl+B then %. Direct, immediate
- **Modifier convention**: Ctrl+Shift+<key> for terminal management actions (avoids conflicts with shell keybinds)
- **Arrow navigation**: Ctrl+Shift+Arrow to move between panes (like Hyprland's Super+Arrow)
- **Discoverable**: all keybinds shown in context menus (right-aligned hints), and eventually in a command palette
- **Configurable**: keybindings section in therminal.toml already exists, wire it through
- **Chords for power users**: optional vim-style sequences for those who want them, but never required

The config already has a keybindings section — this is about wiring it through input handling and making the defaults feel natural.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Keybindings are currently hardcoded in window.rs `handle_pane_shortcut()` (lines 464-507). The config already defines `KeyAction` enum and `Binding` struct (config.rs lines 487-520) but they're not wired to the input handler. tmux's prefix key is a usability tax — therminal should use direct Ctrl+Shift+<key> bindings.

## Task
Wire config-driven keybindings through the input system:

1. **Parse keybinding strings** in `crates/therminal-core/src/config.rs`:
   - Add `parse_binding()` function that converts strings like "ctrl+shift+h" into (KeyCode, Modifiers) pairs
   - Support modifiers: ctrl, shift, alt, super
   - Support keys: a-z, 0-9, f1-f12, arrow keys, enter, tab, escape, backspace, etc.
   - Return errors for invalid bindings (logged at startup, binding skipped)

2. **Build keybinding lookup table** in `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs`:
   - On startup and config reload, parse all bindings into a `HashMap<(KeyCode, Modifiers), KeyAction>`
   - Store on App struct

3. **Replace hardcoded shortcuts** in `handle_pane_shortcut()`:
   - Instead of matching specific key combos, look up (key, modifiers) in the binding map
   - Match on the returned `KeyAction` to dispatch behavior
   - Preserve all existing actions: SplitHorizontal, SplitVertical, ClosePane, ResizeGrow, ResizeShrink, FocusUp/Down/Left/Right

4. **Add new KeyAction variants** to config.rs `KeyAction` enum:
   - `FocusNext`, `FocusPrev` — cycle through panes
   - `ZoomPane` — toggle focused pane fullscreen
   - `Copy`, `Paste` — for clipboard integration (tn-oxa will wire these)

5. **Sensible defaults** in `KeybindingsConfig::default()`:
   - `ctrl+shift+h` → SplitHorizontal
   - `ctrl+shift+v` → SplitVertical
   - `ctrl+shift+w` → ClosePane
   - `ctrl+shift+arrows` → FocusUp/Down/Left/Right
   - `ctrl+shift+=` → ResizeGrow, `ctrl+shift+-` → ResizeShrink
   - `ctrl+shift+c` → Copy, `ctrl+shift+v` → Paste (note: Ctrl+Shift+V won't conflict with terminal Ctrl+V)
   - `ctrl+shift+z` → ZoomPane

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-core/src/config.rs` — KeyAction enum, Binding struct, parse logic
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs` — handle_pane_shortcut(), keybinding lookup, App struct

## When Done
Run `./scripts/ci.sh` to verify build + tests pass.
`bd close tn-utv --reason "Config-driven keybindings wired, hardcoded shortcuts replaced"`
close_reason: Config-driven keybindings wired, hardcoded shortcuts replaced

title: Hyprland-style smart pane sizing and auto-rebalance
description:
Pane management should feel like Hyprland's automatic tiling, not tmux's rigid 50/50 splits.

Key behaviors:
- **Auto-balance on split**: new pane gets fair share of space, not always 50% of parent. If you have 3 panes, each should be ~33%, not 50/25/25
- **Auto-rebalance on close**: when a pane dies, redistribute its space proportionally to neighbors (like Hyprland's remove-node behavior), not just "parent absorbs"
- **Smart split direction**: auto-detect whether to split H or V based on the pane's aspect ratio (wide pane → vertical split, tall pane → horizontal split)
- **Resize with keyboard**: hotkeys to grow/shrink focused pane by increments
- **Minimum pane size**: enforce minimums so panes don't become uselessly small

This is critical for AI workflows where agents spawn and kill terminals constantly — the layout should feel alive and self-organizing, not requiring manual management.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Pane layout uses a binary tree with fixed ratios (pane.rs). Splits always use 0.5 ratio, and closing a pane just promotes the sibling. This feels rigid compared to Hyprland's auto-tiling. The layout tree, split logic, and geometry helpers are in place — this is about making them smarter.

## Task
Implement Hyprland-style smart pane sizing:

1. **Auto-balance on split** in `crates/therminal-app/src/pane.rs`:
   - Modify `split_pane()` (~line 227) to compute a fair ratio based on sibling count
   - When splitting a node that already has N leaves, the new ratio should give each leaf ~equal space
   - Example: 3 panes → split should create ~33/67 not 50/50, so final result is ~33/33/33
   - Add `rebalance()` method on LayoutNode that walks the tree and adjusts ratios for even distribution

2. **Auto-rebalance on close** in `crates/therminal-app/src/pane.rs`:
   - After `remove_pane()` promotes a sibling, call `rebalance()` on the parent
   - Space should redistribute proportionally to neighbors, not just collapse into parent

3. **Smart split direction** in `crates/therminal-app/src/pane.rs`:
   - Add `auto_split_direction()` method that examines the focused pane's viewport rect
   - If width > height * 1.5 → Vertical split (side-by-side)
   - If height > width * 1.5 → Horizontal split (stacked)
   - Otherwise → alternate based on last split direction
   - Wire this into `split_focused_pane()` in window.rs as the default when no explicit direction given

4. **Keyboard resize** in `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs`:
   - `adjust_focused_ratio()` already exists — ensure it works with the rebalanced tree
   - Resize increment: 0.05 (5% of parent) per keystroke

5. **Minimum pane size** in `crates/therminal-app/src/pane.rs`:
   - Add `MIN_PANE_WIDTH = 80.0` and `MIN_PANE_HEIGHT = 60.0` pixel constants
   - In `layout()`, clamp leaf rects to minimums
   - In `split_pane()`, refuse to split if result would be below minimum

6. **Tests**: Add tests for rebalance (3-way, 4-way, asymmetric), auto-direction, minimum size enforcement

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane.rs` — LayoutNode, split_pane, remove_pane, layout()
- `crates/therminal-core/src/geometry.rs` — Rect split methods
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs` — split_focused_pane(), adjust_focused_ratio()

## When Done
Run `./scripts/ci.sh` to verify build + tests pass.
`bd close tn-lpj --reason "Smart pane sizing: auto-balance, auto-direction, min sizes, rebalance on close"`
close_reason: Smart pane sizing: auto-balance, auto-direction, min sizes, rebalance on close

title: Modern copy/paste — select-to-copy, no prefix mode
description:
Copy/paste should work like a normal application, not like tmux's prefix+[ vi-mode dance.

- Click and drag to select text (already partially implemented via mouse routing)
- Selected text auto-copies to clipboard (or Ctrl+C when selection active)
- Ctrl+V pastes (with bracketed paste mode for safety)
- Double-click selects word, triple-click selects line
- Shift+click extends selection
- Right-click on selection shows copy options (integrates with context menu feature)

The goal: anyone who's used a web browser can copy/paste in therminal without learning anything. Zero ceremony.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Selection rendering exists (grid_renderer.rs draws selection rects from alacritty_terminal's SelectionRange), and clipboard.rs has copy/paste helpers + OSC 52 handling. But mouse-driven selection isn't wired — handle_cursor_moved() tracks position but has no drag logic. Copy/paste requires no keybinding yet.

## Task
Implement browser-like copy/paste:

1. **Mouse selection** in `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs`:
   - On left mouse button press: record start position, begin selection on the focused pane's Term
   - Use `alacritty_terminal::selection::Selection` — create a `Simple` selection with the start point
   - On cursor move while button held (drag): update selection end point via `Selection::update()`
   - On left mouse button release: finalize selection
   - Convert pixel positions to grid (col, row) using cell metrics from grid_renderer
   - Store selection state on the App struct (or per-pane)

2. **Double-click word select** in `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs`:
   - Track click timing — if two clicks within 300ms at same position, use `Selection::Semantic` (word select)
   - Triple-click within 300ms: use `Selection::Lines` (line select)

3. **Shift+click extend** in `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs`:
   - If shift held during left click, extend existing selection to new position instead of starting new one

4. **Copy to clipboard** in `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs`:
   - On selection finalize (mouse release): extract selected text from Term grid, call `clipboard::copy_to_clipboard()`
   - Also wire Ctrl+Shift+C (via keybinding system from tn-utv) to copy current selection
   - If no selection active, Ctrl+Shift+C is a no-op (don't send SIGINT)

5. **Paste from clipboard** in `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs`:
   - Wire Ctrl+Shift+V (via keybinding system) to `clipboard::paste_from_clipboard()`
   - Write pasted text to focused pane's PTY
   - Use bracketed paste mode: send `\x1b[200~` before and `\x1b[201~` after pasted text

6. **Clear selection on keypress**: any keypress or click elsewhere should clear the active selection

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs` — mouse event handlers, App struct, selection state
- `crates/therminal-app/src/clipboard.rs` — copy_to_clipboard(), paste_from_clipboard()
- `crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs` — cell metrics for pixel→grid conversion, selection rendering (already works)

## When Done
Run `./scripts/ci.sh` to verify build + tests pass.
`bd close tn-oxa --reason "Mouse-driven selection, double/triple click, shift-extend, copy/paste wired"`
close_reason: Mouse-driven selection, double/triple click, shift-extend, copy/paste wired

title: GPU-rendered right-click context menus (tmux popup style)
description:
Implement right-click popup menus rendered directly on the GPU surface, inspired by tmux's popup menu but modernized.

Two context levels:
1. **Pane context menu** (right-click pane chrome/border): Split H/V, Swap Up/Down, Kill, Respawn, Zoom, Mark — with hotkey hints shown inline
2. **Content context menu** (right-click detected content like file paths, URLs, errors): Copy Path, Copy @Path, Open, Send to Terminal, Paste — context-aware based on what's under the cursor

Design: grouped sections with separators (like the tmux screenshot reference), monospace font, hotkey hints right-aligned. Should feel snappy — GPU-rendered, not a system menu.

This replaces tmux's prefix+key discovery problem: users can right-click to discover what's possible, then learn the hotkeys from the menu itself.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Therminal has no context menu system. The overlay text renderer and rect pipeline exist in grid_renderer.rs for GPU-rendered UI. After tn-e1y (chrome) establishes the overlay rendering pattern and tn-oxa (copy/paste) provides selection context, this adds right-click popup menus.

## Task
Implement GPU-rendered right-click context menus:

1. **Menu data model** — create `crates/therminal-app/src/menu.rs`:
   - `MenuItem { label, hotkey_hint, action: KeyAction, enabled: bool }`
   - `MenuSection(Vec<MenuItem>)` — grouped items with separators
   - `ContextMenu { sections: Vec<MenuSection>, position: (f32, f32), selected_index: Option<usize> }`
   - `MenuContext` enum — `Pane { pane_id }`, `Selection { text }`, `Hotspot { kind, value }` (future: file paths, URLs)

2. **Pane context menu** — default right-click on pane area:
   - Sections: [Split H (h), Split V (v)] | [Swap Up (u), Swap Down (d)] | [Kill (x), Zoom (z)]
   - Hotkey hints right-aligned in each row (from keybinding config)
   - Reference: tmux popup menu screenshot style

3. **Selection context menu** — right-click when text is selected:
   - Sections: [Copy (ctrl+shift+c)] | [Paste (ctrl+shift+v)]
   - Future: Send to Chat, Copy as Markdown

4. **Menu rendering** in `crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs` or new overlay module:
   - Background: PLATE color with SHADOW_SOFT drop shadow
   - Border: 1px LINE color, border-radius if rect pipeline supports it (else square)
   - Text: INK for items, INK_DIM for disabled items, INK for hotkey hints
   - Highlight: PLATE_STRONG background on hovered/selected item
   - Use overlay_text_renderer for menu text
   - Menu width: auto-fit to widest label + hotkey hint + padding

5. **Menu input handling** in `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs`:
   - Right mouse button → determine MenuContext from click position, show menu
   - Arrow keys navigate menu items (wrapping)
   - Enter/click executes selected action, closes menu
   - Escape or click outside closes menu
   - While menu is open, intercept all input before normal dispatch

6. **Menu lifecycle** on App struct:
   - `active_menu: Option<ContextMenu>` field
   - Menu open → request_redraw() each frame until closed
   - Menu renders as final overlay pass (on top of everything)

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-app/src/menu.rs` — new file, menu data model
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs` — right-click handling, menu lifecycle, input interception
- `crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs` — overlay rendering for menu
- `crates/therminal-core/src/palette.rs` — PLATE, LINE, SHADOW colors

## When Done
Run `./scripts/ci.sh` to verify build + tests pass.
`bd close tn-nvq --reason "GPU-rendered right-click context menus with pane and selection contexts"`
close_reason: GPU-rendered right-click context menus with pane and selection contexts

title: resources_dir() broken under cargo run — shell integration missing in dev
description:
## What
resources_dir() checks paths relative to current_exe(), which under `cargo run` points to target/debug/therminal. The relative path ../resources resolves to target/resources (doesn't exist), not the repo's resources/ directory. THERMINAL_RESOURCES_DIR is set to a non-existent path.

## Impact
Default development workflow silently loses all shell integration (prompt markers, CWD tracking). Developers may not notice because tests pass.

## Fix needed
Add a cargo-workspace-aware fallback: check for CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR at compile time, or check for resources/ relative to CWD, or check env var override. The Ghostty approach (GHOSTTY_RESOURCES_DIR env var override) is the cleanest.

## Found by
Codex audit 2026-04-05, finding #5
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
resources_dir() resolves relative to current_exe(), which under `cargo run` points to target/debug/. The relative ../resources becomes target/resources (doesn't exist), not the repo's resources/ directory.

## Task
Fix resources_dir() for development builds:

1. Add a compile-time fallback: use `env!("CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR")` in a build.rs or conditional compilation to embed the workspace root path in debug builds
2. Or add a runtime THERMINAL_RESOURCES_DIR env var override check at the top of resources_dir() (the Ghostty approach — simplest and most flexible)
3. Or check CWD/resources as an additional fallback before the data_dir fallback
4. Verify the fix works with `cargo run --bin therminal` from the workspace root
5. Add a test that resources_dir() returns a path containing the actual shell scripts

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-runtime/src/paths.rs` — resources_dir()
- `crates/therminal-terminal/src/pty.rs` — uses resources_dir() for THERMINAL_RESOURCES_DIR

## When Done
`bd close tn-jwh --reason "resources_dir() works correctly under cargo run"`
close_reason: resources_dir() works correctly under cargo run

title: Mouse wheel/drag/motion targets focused pane, not hovered pane
description:
## What
pane_at_position() exists but wheel, drag, motion, and grid coordinate translation still resolve through focused_pane. In split layouts, pointer events go to the wrong PTY until focus is explicitly clicked.

## Impact
Scrolling in one pane while the cursor is over another scrolls the wrong pane. Mouse drag selection can target wrong pane. Mouse reporting (for TUI apps like vim) sends coordinates to wrong PTY.

## Fix needed
Route all pointer events (wheel, drag, motion, mouse reporting) through pane_at_position(cursor_pos) instead of focused_pane. Only keyboard input should follow focus.

## Found by
Codex audit 2026-04-05, finding #4
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Mouse wheel, drag, motion, and reporting resolve through focused_pane instead of the pane under the pointer. In split layouts, scrolling while the cursor is over a different pane scrolls the wrong one.

## Task
Route pointer events by hovered pane:

1. In the MouseWheel handler: use `pane_at_position(cursor_position)` to find the target pane, scroll that pane's term
2. In mouse drag/motion handlers: resolve grid coordinates against the pane under the pointer, not focused_pane
3. In mouse button reporting (SGR 1006): send coordinates relative to the hovered pane's viewport
4. Keep keyboard input routed to focused_pane (only pointer events change)
5. Left-click should still switch focus AND route the click to the clicked pane

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs` — MouseWheel, MouseInput, CursorMoved handlers
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane.rs` — pane_at_position()

## When Done
`bd close tn-i88 --reason "Mouse events routed to hovered pane"`
close_reason: Mouse events routed to hovered pane

title: GridRenderer cache bleeds between panes in split layout
description:
## What
All panes render through one shared GridRenderer instance. The renderer keeps pane-local state in shared fields: row_cache, cell_buffers, last_cursor_pos, hyperlink_map. After rendering pane A, stale cached rows/cursor/hyperlinks can leak into pane B's render pass.

## Impact
Visual artifacts: stale text, cursor ghosts, or selection highlights appearing in the wrong split pane. Gets worse with more panes.

## Fix needed
Either:
1. Make caches per-pane (HashMap<PaneId, PaneRenderCache>)
2. Or reset/invalidate all caches between pane render passes
3. Or create separate GridRenderer instances per pane

## Found by
Codex audit 2026-04-05, finding #2
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
All panes render through one shared GridRenderer with shared caches (row_cache, cell_buffers, last_cursor_pos, hyperlink_map). After rendering pane A, stale state can bleed into pane B.

## Task
Make GridRenderer cache pane-aware:

1. Read grid_renderer.rs to identify all stateful caches that persist between render calls
2. Option A (preferred): Add a `reset_pane_caches()` method and call it before each pane render in `render_single_pane`
3. Option B: Make caches keyed by PaneId (HashMap<PaneId, PaneCacheState>)
4. Option C: Create per-pane GridRenderer instances (most memory, cleanest isolation)
5. Verify the fix by testing: render pane A with content, render pane B empty, verify B doesn't show A's content (may need a visual/snapshot test or just careful code review)

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs` — caches, render methods
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs` — render_single_pane, render_panes_recursive

## When Done
`bd close tn-orp --reason "GridRenderer caches reset between pane renders"`
close_reason: GridRenderer caches reset between pane renders

title: Route mouse events by pane under cursor
description: Mouse wheel, motion, drag, and reporting in therminal-app are still derived from the focused pane rather than the pane under the pointer. In split layouts this sends scroll and mouse-reporting input to the wrong PTY. Update hit-testing and mode lookup to use pane-under-cursor and add split-pane interaction tests.
close_reason: Duplicate of tn-i88 — same mouse targeting bug with better prepared prompt

title: Isolate renderer caches per pane in split layouts
description: The shared GridRenderer stores row_cache, cell_buffers, hyperlink_map, and cursor state globally while rendering multiple panes sequentially. With partial damage this can leak stale rows and cursor artifacts across panes. Introduce per-pane render cache ownership or equivalent pane-scoped state, then add regression coverage for split-pane redraws.
close_reason: Duplicate of tn-orp — same renderer cache bleed bug with better prepared prompt

title: In-app keybinding help overlay (Ctrl+Shift+?)
description:
7 keyboard shortcuts exist with zero discoverability. No menu, no tooltip, no help text anywhere.

Add a help overlay triggered by Ctrl+Shift+? that shows all available shortcuts in a semi-transparent panel:
- Ctrl+Shift+H/V — Split horizontal/vertical
- Ctrl+Shift+W — Close pane
- Ctrl+Shift+Arrow — Move focus
- Ctrl+Shift++/- — Resize split
- Ctrl+Shift+C/V — Copy/paste
- Ctrl++/-/0 — Font size

Should read from the [keybindings] config so it reflects user customizations. Dismiss on Escape or any other key.

This is low-cost, high-impact — a single GPU overlay render pass.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
7 keyboard shortcuts exist with zero discoverability. Users have no way to learn them without reading source code.

## Task
Add a help overlay triggered by Ctrl+Shift+?:

1. **Trigger**: Ctrl+Shift+/ (question mark) toggles the overlay
2. **Rendering**: Semi-transparent dark panel centered in the window, rendered via wgpu. Should use the same text rendering as the grid (glyphon/cosmic-text).
3. **Content**: Two-column table of all keybindings — shortcut on left, description on right. Read from the `[keybindings]` config so it reflects user customizations.
4. **Dismiss**: Escape, any other keypress, or clicking outside the panel
5. **Styling**: Use palette colors — surface background with text foreground, accent for section headers
6. **Sections**: "Pane Management", "Font", "General"

Build on the overlay rendering pattern established by the pane tab bar (tn-j5c). The help panel is a full-window overlay, not per-pane.

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs` — keyboard handling, overlay rendering
- `crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs` — text rendering
- `crates/therminal-core/src/config.rs` — keybinding definitions
- `crates/therminal-core/src/palette.rs` — colors

After implementation, update CLAUDE.md with the help overlay shortcut.

## When Done
`bd close tn-1ef --reason "Keybinding help overlay implemented"`
close_reason: Keybinding help overlay implemented — Ctrl+Shift+?, reads from config, 3 sections

title: Pane tab bar with split/close/focus affordances
description:
When multiple panes exist, there's no visual indication of which pane is focused (beyond a subtle border), no way to discover split/close/resize without knowing shortcuts, and no pane identification.

Add a thin tab bar or header strip per pane showing:
- Pane title (shell name or running command)
- Focus indicator (highlight active pane header)
- Split H/V buttons (or right-click context menu on the header)
- Close button (X)
- Drag to resize split ratio (cursor change on separator)

This is the most impactful discoverability improvement — splits are the flagship Phase 2 feature and completely invisible without it.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Split panes exist but are completely invisible — no visual indication of which pane is focused, no way to discover split/close/resize without knowing keyboard shortcuts. This is the most impactful discoverability improvement for Phase 2.

## Task
Add a thin pane header bar rendered via wgpu:

1. **Header strip per pane**: ~20px tall bar at the top of each pane showing the pane's running command or shell name
2. **Focus indicator**: Active pane header uses accent color (from palette), inactive panes use muted color
3. **Close button**: Small X on the right side of the header that closes the pane on click
4. **Split buttons**: H|V icons/text on the header that trigger horizontal/vertical split on click
5. **Mouse interaction**: Click header to focus pane, click X to close, click split icons to split
6. **Rendering**: Use the existing `GridRenderer` text rendering (glyphon/cosmic-text) to draw header text. The header should be part of the pane's viewport rect calculation — terminal content starts below the header.
7. **Single pane**: When only one pane exists, don't show the header (no need for chrome)
8. **Separator styling**: Draw a 1px line between panes using the palette surface color

Read `window.rs` and `grid_renderer.rs` first to understand the rendering pipeline. The header is essentially a colored rect with text drawn on top, similar to how the grid renders cells.

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs` — render loop, pane viewport calculation
- `crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs` — text rendering, add header render method
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane.rs` — PaneState, viewport rect calculation
- `crates/therminal-core/src/palette.rs` — accent/muted colors

After implementation, update CLAUDE.md with the pane header bar architecture.

## When Done
`bd close tn-j5c --reason "Pane tab bar with split/close/focus affordances implemented"`
close_reason: Pane tab bar with split/close/focus affordances implemented

title: Tests: IPC server/client round-trip integration tests
description:
server.rs and client.rs have no tests beyond a doc-test that checks compilation. The multiplexed request/response protocol, event subscriptions, legacy detection, and connection lifecycle are all untested.

Needs integration tests covering:
- Client sends Ping, gets Pong back (basic round-trip)
- Multiple concurrent requests with correct response correlation (request_id multiplexing)
- Event subscription: subscribe, trigger event, verify delivery, unsubscribe
- Legacy vs IPC protocol auto-detection (first frame detection)
- Connection drop handling (server closes, client gets error)
- Large frame handling (near MAX_FRAME_SIZE payloads)
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
IPC server/client have no round-trip tests. The multiplexed protocol, event subscriptions, and legacy detection are all untested.

## Task
Write integration tests for IPC round-trips:

1. Create `crates/therminal-daemon/tests/ipc_roundtrip.rs`
2. Test: client sends Ping → server responds Pong (basic round-trip)
3. Test: multiple concurrent requests → correct response correlation by request_id
4. Test: event subscription → trigger event → verify client receives it
5. Test: large frame near MAX_FRAME_SIZE → verify it works
6. Test: client disconnect → server handles gracefully (no panic)
7. Use temp socket paths to avoid interference

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/server.rs` — IpcServer
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/client.rs` — DaemonClient
- `crates/therminal-protocol/src/daemon.rs` — IPC message types

## When Done
`bd close tn-kg5 --reason "IPC round-trip integration tests added"`
close_reason: IPC round-trip integration tests added in crates/therminal-daemon/tests/ipc_roundtrip.rs — 10 tests covering Ping/Pong, concurrent request correlation, event subscription/delivery, filtered subscriptions, large frames near MAX_FRAME_SIZE, client disconnect handling, GetState, ListSessions, and Unsubscribe. All workspace tests pass.

title: Tests: daemon handoff & ensure_daemon integration tests
description:
ensure.rs and handoff.rs have zero tests. This is the highest-risk untested code — it decides whether to reuse, replace, or start a daemon. Bugs here cause stale daemons, lost sessions, or socket corruption on every upgrade.

Needs integration tests covering:
- Start a daemon, call ensure_daemon(), verify it reuses (same BUILD_HASH)
- Start a daemon with different BUILD_HASH, verify handoff triggers
- Call ensure_daemon() with no daemon running, verify it starts fresh
- Stale socket cleanup (socket file exists but nobody listening)
- Handoff timeout path (old daemon hangs, force-remove after ping)
- Concurrent ensure_daemon() calls (two processes racing to start)
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
ensure.rs and handoff.rs have zero tests. This is the highest-risk untested code — it decides whether to reuse, replace, or start a daemon.

## Task
Write integration tests for daemon handoff and ensure_daemon:

1. Create `crates/therminal-daemon/tests/daemon_lifecycle.rs`
2. Test: start a daemon (bind socket), call ensure_daemon() with same BUILD_HASH → verify Reused
3. Test: ensure_daemon() with no daemon running → verify Started
4. Test: stale socket (file exists, nobody listening) → verify cleanup and Started
5. Test: handoff timeout (mock a daemon that accepts but never shuts down) → verify force path
6. Use temp directories for socket paths to avoid test interference
7. Each test should clean up its sockets/processes

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/ensure.rs` — ensure_daemon() to test
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/handoff.rs` — perform_handoff() to test
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/server.rs` — IpcServer::bind() for setup
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/client.rs` — ping for verification

## When Done
`bd close tn-dii --reason "Daemon handoff integration tests added"`
close_reason: Daemon handoff integration tests added in crates/therminal-daemon/tests/daemon_lifecycle.rs — 9 tests covering all specified scenarios, all passing.

title: Mouse input: clicks, scroll, and selection
description:
## What
Mouse events are not handled at all. The winit window_event handler only processes KeyboardInput — no MouseInput, CursorMoved, or MouseWheel events.

## Why
TUI apps (yazi, htop, etc.) expect mouse support. Scrollback navigation requires scroll events. Text selection requires click+drag.

## Scope
1. Mouse click → send to PTY (xterm mouse protocol SGR 1006)
2. Mouse scroll → scrollback navigation (up) or send to PTY (down in alternate screen)
3. Click+drag → text selection (integrate with alacritty_terminal's selection API)
4. Right-click → future context menu hook point

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs (add WindowEvent::MouseInput, CursorMoved, MouseWheel handlers)
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Mouse events are completely unhandled. TUI apps don't respond to clicks, scroll doesn't navigate scrollback, and text can't be selected. This is the biggest usability gap after the font/display fixes.

## Task
Add mouse input handling to the winit event loop.

### 1. Track cursor position
Add `cursor_position: Option<(f64, f64)>` to `App` struct. Update on `WindowEvent::CursorMoved`. Convert pixel coords to terminal grid coords using `cell_width` / `cell_height` from the grid renderer.

### 2. Mouse scroll → scrollback
Handle `WindowEvent::MouseWheel`. Scroll up → increase `display_offset` in alacritty_terminal (scrollback). Scroll down → decrease. Call `term.scroll_display(scroll)`.

### 3. Mouse clicks → PTY (SGR 1006)
Handle `WindowEvent::MouseInput`. When the terminal is in mouse reporting mode (check `term.mode().contains(TermMode::MOUSE_REPORT_CLICK)`), encode clicks as SGR mouse events and write to PTY:
```
\x1b[<button;col;row;M  (press)
\x1b[<button;col;row;m  (release)
```
Button: 0=left, 1=middle, 2=right. Add 4 for shift, 8 for alt, 16 for ctrl.

### 4. Text selection (stretch)
Use alacritty_terminal's Selection API. Click starts selection, drag extends it, release copies to clipboard. This can be a follow-up if the PR gets too large.

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs (add CursorMoved, MouseWheel, MouseInput handlers)
- crates/therminal-terminal/src/input.rs (may need mouse encoding helpers)

## Reference
- alacritty_terminal TermMode flags: MOUSE_REPORT_CLICK, SGR_MOUSE, etc.
- thermal-desktop input_handlers.rs for SGR encoding pattern

## When Done
Close issue: bd close tn-6iq --reason "Mouse scroll, click reporting, cursor tracking"
close_reason: Mouse scroll, click reporting, cursor tracking

title: Prefer Vulkan backend on Linux to avoid WSLg EGL/GLES instability
description:
## What
window.rs:141 uses `wgpu::Backends::all()` which probes EGL/GLES (Mesa ZINK) on WSLg, producing initialization failures and instability. Vulkan is the stable path on WSLg.

## Why
EGL probing on WSLg contributes to crashes and log noise. Preferring Vulkan on Linux avoids the unstable GLES path while keeping cross-platform compatibility.

## How
Use `cfg!(target_os = "linux")` to prefer `Backends::VULKAN` on Linux, with fallback to `Backends::all()`. Also update therminal-core's wgpu_ctx.rs:15 which uses `Instance::default()` (same issue).
close_reason: Prefer Vulkan on Linux to avoid WSLg EGL/GLES instability

title: Fix alpha_mode: prefer Opaque to prevent transparent client area
description:
## What
The wgpu surface configuration in window.rs:185 blindly picks `surface_caps.alpha_modes[0]`. On WSLg/Wayland, the compositor may advertise PreMultiplied first, causing the terminal client area to be transparent while server-side decorations remain.

## Why
This is the primary cause of the "transparent client area" symptom in tn-cz9. Fixing alpha_mode selection to prefer Opaque is low-risk and directly addresses the visual bug.

## How
In `init_gpu()`, replace `surface_caps.alpha_modes[0]` with logic that prefers `AlphaMode::Opaque`, falling back to `AlphaMode::Auto`, then the first available mode.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
WSLg/Wayland advertises PreMultiplied as the first alpha mode, causing the terminal client area to appear transparent. This is the most impactful fix for the WSL2 display bug (tn-cz9).

## Task
Fix alpha_mode selection in `init_gpu()` to prefer `AlphaMode::Opaque`.

In `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs` line 185, replace:
```rust
alpha_mode: surface_caps.alpha_modes[0],
```

With logic that picks the best alpha mode:
```rust
alpha_mode: *surface_caps.alpha_modes
    .iter()
    .find(|m| **m == wgpu::AlphaMode::Opaque)
    .or_else(|| surface_caps.alpha_modes.iter().find(|m| **m == wgpu::AlphaMode::Auto))
    .unwrap_or(&surface_caps.alpha_modes[0]),
```

Log the selected alpha mode at info level (similar to the adapter info log on line 156-159).

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs (line 185 — alpha_mode selection in init_gpu)

## Verification
Run `./scripts/ci.sh` to confirm build + clippy + tests pass.

## When Done
Close issue: bd close tn-3qe --reason "Prefer AlphaMode::Opaque to fix transparent client area on WSLg"
close_reason: Prefer AlphaMode::Opaque to fix transparent client area on WSLg

title: WSL2 Wayland maximize crash and transparent client area
description:
Environment: WSL2 / WSLg Wayland.

Observed behavior:
- Initial launch can show text with incorrect spacing, then terminal client area becomes transparent while server-side decorations remain visible.
- Clicking maximize triggers Wayland event loop failure, broken pipe errors, and process crash with SIGSEGV / exit code 139.
- Runtime logs include EGL initialization failures from wgpu_hal::gles::egl and Mesa ZINK/DRI2 warnings.

Code context:
- crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs selects backends via wgpu::Backends::all() and uses surface_caps.alpha_modes[0] directly.
- Surface errors in render() reconfigure/return but do not trigger any fallback path.
- The codebase has an open WSL2 issue (tn-8vw) for PTY/path quirks, but not for graphics/window stability.

Suspected causes:
- WSLg/Wayland graphics backend instability during EGL/GLES probing or resize/maximize.
- Choosing the first advertised alpha_mode may produce unintended compositor transparency on Wayland/WSLg.
- Resize/maximize path may be exposing a winit/wgpu/WSLg edge case without defensive fallback.

Acceptance:
- App no longer shows transparent client area on WSL2/WSLg.
- Maximize/resize does not crash.
- Backend/alpha selection is explicit and stable on WSL2, or there is a documented workaround/fallback.
close_reason: All 3 child issues complete: alpha_mode fix (tn-3qe), Vulkan backend (tn-xw0), resize crash defense (tn-s43)

title: Decision: shell integration injection strategy (tn-1z2)
description:
Needs human decision before Phase 1 work can begin. Three options:

1. **Auto-inject** (WezTerm style) — prepend to shell init, seamless but invasive
2. **Manual source** (Alacritty style) — user adds `source` line, explicit but friction
3. **TERM_PROGRAM detection** (Ghostty style) — shell checks THERMINAL_RESOURCES_DIR, zero-config but requires shell plugin ecosystem adoption

Recommendation: Option 3 (Ghostty style) — set TERM_PROGRAM=therminal + THERMINAL_RESOURCES_DIR, ship shell scripts that shells can auto-source. Best UX with least invasion. Can fall back to option 1 if adoption is slow.
close_reason: Resolved via tn-1z2: Ghostty-style TERM_PROGRAM detection chosen
