title: Add `environment` field to OSC 1341 markers for cross-boundary agent identification
description:
Hook scripts should include an `environment` field in OSC 1341 markers so the daemon knows where a signal originates without probing across boundaries. This is the key insight from the tn-63y7 decision: instead of the daemon crossing WSL/Docker/SSH boundaries to discover context, the shell cooperates and self-identifies.

**Examples of `environment` values:**
- `wsl:Ubuntu-24.04` — WSL distro
- `docker:agent-worker-3` — container name/ID
- `ssh:devbox.internal` — SSH hostname
- `local` — native host (default)

**How it flows:**
1. Hook script detects its environment (`WSL_DISTRO_NAME`, `/.dockerenv`, `SSH_CONNECTION`, etc.)
2. Emits `environment=<value>` in OSC 1341 marker payload
3. Marker travels through PTY stream transparently (no boundary crossing)
4. Daemon receives it at the exact pane — can display in chrome, filter in agent registry, use for cwd path interpretation

**Source location:** The canonical hook script is `~/.claude/hooks/scripts/state-tracker.sh`. The reference in therminal's tree is a **symlink** so they update together — do not duplicate the file.

**Why this matters:** Makes Docker containers and SSH sessions work with zero therminal daemon changes. The daemon never needs to know about UNC paths, PID namespaces, or container runtimes — it just reads the marker. Extends the same pattern that made OSC 1341 markers superior to state-file polling for WSL.

**Depends on:** OSC 1341 marker grammar already supports key=value pairs. This is adding a new key to the existing vocabulary.
acceptance_criteria:
- `state-tracker.sh` detects WSL/Docker/SSH and emits `environment=<type>:<name>` in OSC 1341 markers
- Daemon parses `environment` field and stores in `PaneCapacityCache`
- `terminal.agents.list` / `tn agents list` surfaces the environment field
- Chrome badge or status bar can display environment origin per pane
- Symlink from therminal tree to `~/.claude/hooks/scripts/state-tracker.sh` preserved
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: Clean end-to-end flow. The existing MarkerPatch pattern made adding a new field straightforward -- add to parser, add to patch struct, add to capacity entry, wire through bridge and MCP. Tests at each layer caught the missing field in struct literals quickly.
- What was unclear: Nothing significant. The task was well-scoped with exact line references.
close_reason: Environment field added end-to-end: hook script detection (WSL/Docker/SSH/local) -> OSC 1341 marker emission -> parse_osc_1341 parser -> MarkerPatch -> PaneCapacityEntry -> ensure.rs bridge -> AgentSessionDetail MCP result. 8 new tests, docs updated in harness CLAUDE.md, daemon CLAUDE.md, and root CLAUDE.md.

title: Add OSC 9;9 support for WSL cwd tracking (shell-side path translation)
description:
Windows Terminal uses OSC 9;9 for cwd tracking in WSL panes — the shell emits `wslpath -w "$PWD"` (a Windows-native path), so the terminal never needs to translate Linux paths to UNC. Therminal currently uses OSC 7 with Linux paths and translates via `linux_to_unc()` in the daemon, which is fragile.

**What to do:**
- Add OSC 9;9 handler to `SequenceInterceptor` (parse Windows path from payload)
- Update shell integration scripts (`resources/shell-integration/bash.sh`, `zsh.sh`, `fish.fish`) to emit OSC 9;9 alongside OSC 7 when `TERM_PROGRAM=therminal` and running inside WSL (detect via `WSL_DISTRO_NAME`)
- OSC 9;9 payload: `printf "\e]9;9;%s\e\\" "$(wslpath -w "$PWD")"`
- When OSC 9;9 is received, use the Windows path directly — no `linux_to_unc()` needed
- Keep OSC 7 + `linux_to_unc()` as fallback for shells that don't source integration scripts

**Why:** This is the pattern Windows Terminal deliberately chose over solving UNC path translation in code. Punting translation to the shell via `wslpath` is more reliable and eliminates a class of path-mapping bugs. Keeps `linux_to_unc()` for state file paths where therminal doesn't control the emitter.

**Reference:** [OSC 9;9 WT issue (#8166)](https://github.com/microsoft/terminal/issues/8166), [OSC 9;9 PR (#8330)](https://github.com/microsoft/terminal/pull/8330), [OSC 7 discussion (#8214)](https://github.com/microsoft/terminal/issues/8214)
acceptance_criteria:
- Shell integration scripts emit OSC 9;9 with `wslpath -w` when inside WSL
- Daemon parses OSC 9;9 and uses Windows path directly for cwd
- Chrome/status bar shows correct cwd for WSL panes without `linux_to_unc()` in the hot path
- OSC 7 fallback still works when shell integration not sourced
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: Clean separation — OSC 9;9 subtype detection slots neatly into the existing handle_osc_9() by checking params.len() >= 3 && params[1] == b"9" before falling through to the notification path. The WslCwd event variant wires through all 4 cwd consumers (daemon headless, app spawn, app remote_spawn, region_index) with a single or-pattern on the match arm.
- What was unclear: Whether the daemon should prefer WslCwd over CurrentDirectory when both arrive (OSC 9;9 is emitted after OSC 7 in the shell scripts). Currently both update the same cwd slot, so the last one wins — which is WslCwd since it's emitted second. This is the desired behavior for Windows-native daemons (they get the Windows path), and harmless for Linux/macOS daemons (WSL_DISTRO_NAME won't be set, so OSC 9;9 is never emitted).
close_reason: Implemented OSC 9;9 WSL cwd tracking: extended handle_osc_9() to detect 9;9 subtype, added InterceptedEvent::WslCwd variant wired through all 4 cwd consumers (daemon headless, app spawn, app remote_spawn, region_index), updated bash/zsh/fish shell integration scripts to emit OSC 9;9 via wslpath -w when WSL_DISTRO_NAME is set, added 7 unit tests, updated CLAUDE.md WSL2 docs. OSC 7 + linux_to_unc() preserved as fallback.

title: WSL distro detection: switch to registry-primary (HKCU\Lxss)
description:
Windows Terminal reads WSL distros from `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Lxss` via registry API — instant, no process spawn, no UTF-16 BOM pain. Therminal currently shells out to `wsl.exe -l -q` which can block for seconds on WSL cold-start and requires BOM stripping.

**What to do:**
- Use `winreg` crate to enumerate `HKCU\...\Lxss` subkeys, read `DistributionName` from each
- Filter out Docker distros (`docker-desktop*`) like WT does
- Keep `wsl.exe -l -q` as fallback when registry read fails (edge case: stale registry)
- Replace `detect_default_distro()` in `therminal-runtime::wsl` with registry-primary path

**Why:** Eliminates cold-start latency, removes UTF-16 LE BOM stripping complexity (tn-9ixz), and matches Windows Terminal's proven architecture.

**Reference:** [WslDistroGenerator.cpp](https://github.com/microsoft/terminal/blob/479ef264b2d9e284db1fc8457f3897d1b19ac32e/src/cascadia/TerminalSettingsModel/WslDistroGenerator.cpp), [registry lookup commit](https://github.com/microsoft/terminal/commit/f9a844dbdacff8b7b6b0a1221de7634668fa8ca7)
acceptance_criteria:
- `detect_default_distro()` reads registry first, falls back to `wsl.exe`
- BOM stripping code only exercised in fallback path
- Docker distros filtered out
- Startup latency measurably improved when WSL service is cold
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: Clean separation into registry-primary (`detect_distro_from_registry`) and wsl.exe fallback (`detect_distro_from_wsl_exe`) functions. The existing winreg 0.10 from portable-pty was already in Cargo.lock, so reusing the same version avoided pulling a second copy. Docker filtering via `is_docker_distro()` is pure and fully testable cross-platform. The `parse_distro_from_raw` test helper was updated to mirror the new docker-filtering logic, giving 3 new docker-specific parse tests plus 4 `is_docker_distro` unit tests.
- What was unclear: The task said "latest stable" for winreg but 0.56 is a massive jump from the 0.10 already in the dep tree. Used 0.10 to match portable-pty and avoid two copies. API was sufficient for the registry operations needed.
close_reason: Registry-primary WSL distro detection implemented. detect_default_distro() now reads HKCU\\Lxss registry (instant) with wsl.exe -l -q as fallback. Docker-desktop distros filtered from both paths. winreg 0.10 added. 71 tests pass, clippy clean.

title: Pinned state not restored on GUI reattach from daemon
description: In remote_spawn.rs, build_remote_pane_state sets pinned: false unconditionally. When the GUI reattaches to a running daemon, pinned panes lose their pin state. Need to derive pinned from the daemon's pane tags (pinned=true tag) or from PaneSummary.pinned during the reattach flow.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: the fix was straightforward — `ListPanes` already returns `PaneSummary.pinned`, so adding one extra IPC call in both the reconcile path (`reconcile.rs`) and the initial attach path (`init.rs`) was sufficient.
- The `build_remote_pane_state` signature addition was clean: added `pinned: bool` as the final parameter with a regular comment (doc comments on function params cause a compiler error in Rust).
- New-pane paths (split ops) correctly pass `false` — pinning a freshly-created pane always starts unpinned.
- What was unclear: the `init.rs` closure captures `pinned_map` by reference but the closure is `FnMut`, which Rust allows as long as it's not `move`. This worked without issues.
- Both reattach paths now restore pinned state from the daemon, so `Ctrl+Alt+P` panes survive GUI restarts.
close_reason: Fixed build_remote_pane_state() to accept pinned: bool parameter; both reattach paths (init.rs and reconcile.rs) now call ListPanes to restore PaneSummary.pinned; new-split paths pass false.

title: Retro Terminal theme: ANSI black near-invisible, muted chrome below WCAG AA
description: ANSI black (#1a2a1a) has only 1.26:1 contrast against background (#0a120a) — invisible in reverse video. chrome_fg_muted (#2a8a2a) is 3.86:1 against chrome_header_bg (#0f200f), below WCAG AA 4.5:1. Needs design decision: darken ANSI black (e.g. #050d05) and brighten chrome_fg_muted (e.g. #44a844).
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: All three problematic colors were fixed with minimal changes. Brightening chrome_fg_muted from #2a8a2a to #44a844 gave 5.62:1 vs header_bg (well above 4.5:1 minimum). Moving foreground_muted to #2a902a (same hue, slightly brighter green) gave 4.64:1 vs background. ANSI black moved to #050d05 — truly darker than background so reverse-video mode renders black cells visibly distinct from the background.
- What was unclear: The ANSI black fix is directionally opposite to what you might expect — making it darker (lower luminance) rather than lighter creates better reverse-video semantics. The task description says "darken" and #050d05 achieves this correctly. The contrast ratio of ANSI black vs background will always be low on a dark theme (both are near-black); the meaningful test is that the phosphor foreground reads well on the ANSI black background in reverse video, which it does (14.53:1).
close_reason: Fixed all three contrast violations in Retro Terminal theme: ANSI black darkened to #050d05, chrome_fg_muted brightened to #44a844 (5.62:1 vs header_bg), foreground_muted brightened to #2a902a (4.64:1 vs background). Added 6 WCAG AA tests in palette.rs. All 285 therminal-core tests pass.

title: WebView pane backend: render HTML pages inline alongside terminal panes
description:
Activate the existing `PaneBackendKind::WebView` stub to render HTML content inline in the pane grid, composited alongside terminal panes with the same split/resize/pin/workspace treatment.

**Implementation**: Use wry (Tauri's webview library) to embed a platform-native webview as a pane backend. Each WebView pane gets the same pane header, context menu, keybindings, and layout participation as terminal panes.

**Immediate content sources (all exist today):**

From ~/projects/pocketforge (Axum backend, vanilla JS/HTML/CSS, no build step):
- **git.html** — Canvas commit graph with stage/commit/push UI
- **git-tree.html** — Full commit history tree visualization
- **diff.html** — Side-by-side diff viewer with syntax highlighting
- **beads-board.html** — Kanban issue board with drag-drop
- **logs.html** — Live WebSocket log viewer with level filtering
- **terminals.html** — Session dashboard with context meters and agent status
- **files.html** — File browser with tree view, create/rename/delete
- **search.html** — Full-text search with replace across project files
- **markdown.html** — Markdown renderer with syntax highlighting
- All pages are context-aware via `?path=` query param

From ~/projects/markdown-themes (React + Go, 30 themes):
- **30 standalone CSS theme files** — pure CSS, each 9-17KB, drop-in theming for any markdown renderer
- **Mermaid chart rendering** — dynamic color extraction from CSS vars, SVG override for diagram elements
- **KaTeX math rendering** — inline and display math in markdown
- Theme names: Dark Academia, Cyberpunk, Retro Terminal, Nordic, Art Deco, Swiss International, etc.

**Architecture options:**
1. **Sidecar mode**: WebView panes load URLs from running PocketForge/markdown-themes servers (quick win, zero porting)
2. **Embedded mode**: Bundle HTML/JS/CSS into therminal binary, serve from embedded Axum or wry's custom protocol handler
3. **Hybrid**: Ship static pages embedded, but also support arbitrary URLs for user-hosted dashboards

**Key files:**
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/backend.rs` — PaneBackendKind::WebView stub (already exists)
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/state.rs` — PaneState (WebView needs url + content fields)
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window/render.rs` — render_single_pane needs WebView branch
- `crates/therminal-core/src/config/mod.rs` — ProfileConfig could gain `url` field for WebView profiles

**Platform considerations:**
- Linux: WebKitGTK (wry default)
- Windows: WebView2 (Edge Chromium, ships with Win10+)
- macOS: WKWebView (system framework)
- Keyboard/mouse: need input routing between terminal panes and WebView panes

**Depends on:** Pinned panes (tn-n5jk) make this much more useful — pin a git graph or kanban board while switching workspaces.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: The existing PaneBackendKind::WebView stub and JsonlTail reference pattern made the integration surface clear. wry's `build_as_child` API maps cleanly onto winit's window handle. The two-phase approach (insert PaneState into layout, then create native webview after borrow release) solved the mutable borrow conflict.
- What was unclear: wry's `url()` returns `Result<String>` not `&str` — minor API surprise. The flaky `prepare_bash_rcfile_creates_wrapper` test is pre-existing and unrelated. WebView pane creation is GUI-only (no daemon PTY), so the CLI `--url` flag produces an informational error directing users to the GUI.
close_reason: Implemented WebView pane backend via wry. v1 sidecar mode: platform-native webviews (WebKitGTK/WebView2/WKWebView) embedded as child surfaces with full layout participation (split/resize/workspace/header chrome/context menu). WebViewManager on App owns wry instances; render driver syncs bounds/visibility per frame; PTY input bypassed for native routing.

title: Background opacity / transparency slider in settings
description:
Add a background opacity control so the terminal background can be semi-transparent, showing the desktop/wallpaper behind it. A signature feature of sleek GPU-accelerated terminals (Alacritty, Windows Terminal, Kitty all have it).

**Config:**
- `[colors] background_opacity = 1.0` (range 0.0–1.0, default fully opaque)

**Settings overlay control:**
- Stepped select or slider with presets: 100% / 95% / 90% / 85% / 75% / 50%
- Live preview as user changes value (hot-reload pipeline)

**Implementation layers:**
1. Add `background_opacity: f32` to `ColorsConfig` in `therminal-core/src/config/mod.rs`
2. Request transparent surface from winit at window creation (`Window::default_attributes().with_transparent(true)`)
3. Set wgpu clear color alpha to `background_opacity` value
4. Apply opacity to `ChromePalette` background-derived fields (header_bg, status_bar_bg, etc.) so chrome also respects transparency
5. Settings overlay control following the existing select/stepper pattern from tn-ya01

**Platform considerations:**
- **Linux/Wayland**: works natively with compositor
- **macOS**: works natively with WindowServer
- **Windows native**: needs DWM composition — `SetWindowCompositionAttribute` or `DwmSetWindowAttribute` for acrylic/mica blur behind
- **WSLg**: may not support transparency (software compositor) — degrade gracefully to opaque

**Key files:**
- `crates/therminal-core/src/config/mod.rs` — ColorsConfig + chrome_palette()
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window/mod.rs` — window creation + clear color
- `crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs` — background rendering
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window/settings_overlay/sections.rs` — new control
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: The existing settings overlay pattern (tn-ya01) made adding the new control straightforward — ControlBinding/SettingsCommand/sections/rebuild pattern is clean and mechanical. The ChromePalette opacity multiplication in chrome_palette() was the right place to wire it — all bg-derived fields update in one spot.
- What was unclear: Whether to use PreMultiplied or PostMultiplied alpha mode for wgpu. Went with PreMultiplied (pre-multiplying RGB channels by alpha in the clear color) as it's the standard for compositors. The fallback chain (PreMultiplied → PostMultiplied → Auto → Opaque) handles platforms that don't support it.
- Design decision: Used Option<f32> instead of f32 for background_opacity to avoid conflicts with #[derive(Default)] on ColorsConfig (f32::default() is 0.0, not 1.0). The accessor unwraps with 1.0 default and clamps to 0.0..=1.0.
close_reason: Added background opacity config + settings control + wgpu transparency

title: JsonlTail pane hotkeys broken: Enter causes lag, 'e' expand doesn't work
description:
When a subagent JSONL tailing pane is open (PaneBackend::JsonlTail), keyboard hotkeys don't work correctly:

1. **Enter causes lag** — pressing Enter in a JsonlTail pane makes the UI laggy. Likely the keystroke is being written to a non-existent PTY or triggering an expensive operation.
2. **'e' doesn't expand** — the expand hotkey doesn't seem to fire. Either the key event isn't reaching the JsonlTail input handler, or the handler isn't wired up for the 'e' key.

Likely root cause: JsonlTail panes use `PaneBackend::JsonlTail` which doesn't have a PTY, but keyboard input may still be routed through the PTY write path. The JsonlTail-specific key handler (scroll, expand/collapse) may not be intercepting events before they hit the default PTY input path.

Check `pane/jsonl_tail.rs` for the key handling logic and `window/event_handler/pty_input.rs` for where keys get routed.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: Tracing the full keystroke data flow from winit event through encode_key() to PaneBackendKind::JsonlTail write_input() to handle_input() revealed two clear issues. The byte encoding was correct (Enter -> b"\r", 'e' -> b"e") and the match arms in handle_input() were correct.
- What was unclear: The task description suggested the issue might be multi-byte escape sequences not matching, but that turned out not to be the case. The real root cause was missing request_redraw() — the event loop uses ControlFlow::Wait so nothing triggers a repaint after JsonlTail keyboard input. Terminal panes get implicit redraws from the PTY echo callback, masking the missing explicit redraw.

## Root Cause
Two issues found:
1. **Missing request_redraw()**: After handle_key_input() in the else branch of handle_keyboard_input_event(), no request_redraw() was called. Terminal panes get implicit redraws from PTY echo, but JsonlTail panes handle input in-place with no echo loop — so state changes from Enter/e/j/k/etc were applied but never rendered until an unrelated event triggered a redraw. This caused both the perceived "lag" on Enter and 'e' appearing broken.
2. **Unnecessary refresh_shadow_term()**: The JsonlTail branch of write_input() called refresh_shadow_term() unconditionally on every keystroke, even when handle_input() returned false (unrecognized key). This caused a full VTE grid rewrite on every unrecognized key.

## Fix
1. Added request_redraw() after handle_key_input() in the else branch of handle_keyboard_input_event(). For terminal panes this is harmless (winit coalesces redundant redraws).
2. Made refresh_shadow_term() conditional on handle_input() returning true (state actually changed).
close_reason: Fixed JsonlTail keyboard input routing — Enter and e hotkeys working. Root cause was missing request_redraw() after handle_key_input() for non-PTY backends.

title: Populate session_title in state-tracker.sh from UserPromptSubmit prompt
description:
The state-tracker.sh hook captures the user prompt as DETAILS.last_prompt but never writes session_title to the state JSON. The harness already reads session_title and ClaudeChromeMeta.header_title() already prioritizes it — the pane header would show it instead of just 'Claude'.

**Fix**: On the first UserPromptSubmit of a session, extract a session_title from the prompt and write it to the state file.

**Title extraction logic**:
1. Check for a beads issue ID (e.g. 'tn-xxxx') → use as title
2. Check for slash commands (/commit, /review-pr 123) → use as title  
3. Fallback: first ~50 chars of prompt, trimmed to word boundary

**Key files**:
- resources/hooks/state-tracker.sh — user-prompt handler (line 192), state JSON builder (line 361)
- crates/therminal-harness-claude/src/state.rs — ClaudeSessionState.session_title (already wired)
- crates/therminal-app/src/claude_cwd.rs — header_title() (already reads session_title)

**Implementation**:
1. Add SESSION_TITLE variable, default empty
2. In user-prompt handler: if SESSION_TITLE is empty (first prompt), extract from PROMPT
3. In state JSON builder: add session_title field
4. Also consider returning hookSpecificOutput.sessionTitle to Claude Code so it can display the title in its own UI
close_reason: session_title populated from first UserPromptSubmit prompt in state-tracker.sh — beads ID > slash command > first 60 chars

title: Fix + test tn CLI on Windows, formalize MCP vs CLI boundary
description:
Two parts:

**1. Fix tn on Windows**
The `tn` bash wrapper doesn't run on Windows native. Blocked by tn-08oc (tn.cmd + build script copy). Once that lands, test the full CLI surface from a Windows PowerShell/cmd pane: `tn pane list`, `tn pane peek`, `tn agents list`, `tn pane send`, etc.

**2. Formalize MCP vs CLI boundary**
The CLAUDE.md already has a CLI-vs-MCP policy section but it needs testing and possible refinement. The principle:

- **`tn` CLI (bash calls)**: polling (`pane list`, `pane peek`, `agents list`), writes (`pane send`, `pane tag/untag`), fire-and-forget ops. Terse output, one bash call, no cache fragmentation.
- **MCP tools**: structured data for downstream tool calls (`get_details`, `get_cadence`, `find_with_capacity`), subscriptions (`terminal://pane/{id}/output`), blocking waits (`wait_for_output`), conductor tick (`get_summary`), destructive/admin ops.

**Cache health rationale**: Each MCP tool call is a separate cache key segment. Five `get_content` calls = ~10KB/tick of uncacheable context. `tn pane peek` returns ~90-500 bytes in a single bash call that's part of a larger cacheable block.

Test the full matrix of operations from both surfaces and update `docs/cli.md` + the CLAUDE.md policy section with any corrections.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: Systematic comparison of CLI source code (clap definitions) vs docs/cli.md decision table revealed 15+ gaps. The tools.rs doc comment classification table was already well-structured but incomplete.
- What was unclear: The decision table originally said destroy ops are MCP-only (Admin tier), but the CLI actually implements `pane destroy` and `session destroy` without trust-tier gates. Clarified that MCP enforces trust tier while CLI skips it — both surfaces are valid depending on the caller's trust model.

## Changes made
1. Updated docs/cli.md decision table: added 12 missing CLI commands (focus, move, swap, resize, workspace create/rename, layout batch, agent-event push), 4 missing MCP tools (create_tail, get_session_detail, widgets.timeline.toggle, capture_result), fixed destroy ops to show CLI+MCP dual availability, organized table by domain with section headers
2. Added Windows support section to docs/cli.md with wrapper comparison table and Windows-specific IPC/path notes
3. Added agent-event subcommand reference to docs/cli.md
4. Updated workspace subcommand signatures in docs/cli.md to match actual clap definitions
5. Rewrote "When MCP is always the right choice" section, split out "Destructive operations" subsection
6. Updated scripts/tn.cmd with binary discovery (THERMINAL_BIN, PATH, Desktop fallback, error message)
7. Updated scripts/tn.ps1 with binary discovery (THERMINAL_BIN, PATH, Desktop fallback, error message)
8. Updated scripts/tn bash wrapper policy comment to list all CLI-available commands
9. Updated CLAUDE.md CLI-vs-MCP section: added layout/destroy/hook-push/batch commands to CLI list, clarified trust-tier distinction, noted all three wrapper scripts
10. Updated crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp/tools.rs classification table: fixed destroy ops, added 4 missing MCP-only tools
close_reason: Formalized CLI-vs-MCP boundary: updated decision table with all 32 MCP tools + all CLI commands, added binary discovery to Windows wrappers, documented Windows-specific IPC/path notes, fixed workspace command signatures, clarified trust-tier distinction for destroy ops.

title: Font selector: filter to Mono Nerd Font variants only
description:
The current `FONT_FAMILY_OPTIONS` list in `settings_overlay/sections.rs` mixes Nerd Font, plain, and non-mono variants. On WSL only ~2 fonts show up; on Windows many non-mono fonts don't render well in the terminal grid.

**Fix**: Replace the static list with Mono Nerd Font variants only:

```rust
pub(crate) const FONT_FAMILY_OPTIONS: &[&str] = &[
    "JetBrainsMono Nerd Font Mono",
    "FiraCode Nerd Font Mono",
    "CaskaydiaCove Nerd Font Mono",
    "Hack Nerd Font Mono",
    "Inconsolata Nerd Font Mono",
    "SourceCodePro Nerd Font Mono",
    "UbuntuMono Nerd Font Mono",
    "Iosevka Nerd Font Mono",
    "RobotoMono Nerd Font Mono",
    "MesloLGS Nerd Font Mono",
];
```

**Why Mono variants specifically**:
- Guaranteed monospace grid rendering
- Full icon support (CSD buttons, powerline, Nerd Font glyphs in launcher tiles)
- Cross-platform consistency — Nerd Fonts are explicitly installed by the user on both WSL and Windows
- The "Mono" suffix variant includes all glyphs but forces single-width for double-width icons, preventing grid misalignment

Keep the existing availability filter (only show installed fonts). Also update the default config text if it references the old list.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: Straightforward replacement of the font list with clear test updates. All 676 tests passed on the first try.
- What was unclear: Nothing significant — the indices in the tests were the main thing to reconcile after changing the list.
close_reason: Font selector filtered to Mono Nerd Font variants

title: Wire profile param into MCP panes.create and CLI
description:
Add `profile: Option<String>` to `SpawnPaneParam` in the MCP tool handler and `--profile <name>` to `tn pane create` CLI.

**MCP** (`crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp/tools.rs`):
- Add `profile` field to `SpawnPaneParam`
- When set, resolve profile → SpawnOptions using the profile resolver
- `profile` takes precedence over `shell`/`command` params; warn if both specified
- Daemon needs access to config profiles — either pass at MCP handler init or re-read from config

**CLI** (`crates/therminal-app/src/cli/pane.rs`):
- Add `--profile <name>` to `pane create` subcommand
- Maps to IPC request with profile name → daemon resolves

**Validation**: profile name must exist in config; return clear error if not found.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: The profile resolver (tn-yh6k) was clean and self-contained, making integration straightforward. Following the existing patterns for threading config through the server (like trust_config) kept the plumbing consistent.
- What was unclear: The batch_layout_ops path doesn't have access to profiles — decided to skip profile resolution there since batch ops are used by layout restore/scripts, not profile-based spawning. The MCP server's profile resolution uses params.cwd as inherit_cwd (empty string if not provided); a future refinement could resolve the source pane's cwd from split_from when available.
close_reason: Profile param wired into MCP panes.create and CLI

title: Launcher overlay rendering
description:
Implement `OverlayMode::Launcher` — the visual grid panel showing profile tiles.

**Architecture**:
- New module `crates/therminal-app/src/window/launcher_overlay/` (or single file `launcher_overlay.rs` if small enough)
- `OverlayMode::Launcher` variant in the existing enum
- Keybinding: `Ctrl+Shift+L` → `KeyAction::OpenLauncher`
- Reads `config.profiles` to build tile list; prepends a "Default Shell" tile that uses `general.shell`

**Rendering**:
- Centered panel with semi-transparent background scrim (Modal tier via OverlayLayer)
- Grid of tiles — compute rows/cols from profile count and panel width
- Each tile: rounded rect background with per-profile color, beveled edges (lighter top, darker bottom — 2 extra rect draws), large Nerd Font glyph centered, profile name below in chrome_fg
- Focused tile gets focus_border highlight
- Rasterize via the existing rect_pipeline + glyphon text for labels/icons

**Navigation**:
- Arrow keys move focus between tiles
- Enter spawns: build SpawnOptions from profile, split focused pane, close overlay
- Esc closes overlay
- Could also support typing to filter (stretch goal)

**cwd inheritance**: read focused pane's cwd (from OSC 7 / PaneStatus) and pass to the profile resolver.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: Following the help_overlay.rs pattern made the GPU rendering straightforward. The existing OverlayMode enum + event_handler overlay dispatch pattern made wiring clean. ProfileConfig already had `icon` and `color` fields ready to use.
- What was unclear: The task mentioned IpcRequest::SplitPane having a `profile: Option<String>` field "just added in previous commit" but it was already present. The daemon-side profile resolution was already wired, so the launcher just needed to pass the profile name through.
close_reason: Launcher overlay with profile tile grid

title: Profile → SpawnOptions resolver
description:
Add a function that looks up a profile name in `TherminalConfig.profiles` and produces a `therminal_terminal::pty::SpawnOptions`, merging profile fields with the caller's cwd.

**Location**: `therminal-core/src/config/mod.rs` or a new `therminal-core/src/config/profiles.rs` module.

**Behavior**:
- `resolve_profile(config: &TherminalConfig, profile_name: &str, inherit_cwd: &str) -> Result<SpawnOptions, ProfileError>`
- If profile has `command`: set `SpawnOptions.shell` to the command string, skip shell integration (how to signal? add a `skip_shell_integration` field to SpawnOptions, or resolve the command to shell+args here)
- If profile has `shell` + `shell_args`: populate SpawnOptions normally
- `working_directory` from profile overrides `inherit_cwd`; if neither set, inherit_cwd wins
- Merge profile `env` into SpawnOptions.env

This is the bridge between config-land profiles and the PTY spawn path. Used by the launcher overlay, MCP pane create, and CLI.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: Clean crate boundary design — ResolvedProfile in therminal-core avoids cross-crate dependency while mapping 1:1 to SpawnOptions. Adding skip_shell_integration to SpawnOptions was straightforward with Default derive handling all existing call sites.
- What was unclear: The task spec's return type `(SpawnOptions, bool)` conflicted with step 3 adding the bool as a field on SpawnOptions itself. Resolved by putting the field in SpawnOptions and having the resolver return ResolvedProfile (core-native) instead.
close_reason: Profile resolver wired to SpawnOptions with shell_integration skip

title: Extend ProfileConfig with shell_args, command, shell_integration, icon, color
description:
The existing `ProfileConfig` in `therminal-core/src/config/mod.rs` has `shell`, `working_directory`, `env`, `font_size`, `scrollback_lines` but is unwired. Extend it for the launcher:

**New fields**:
- `shell_args: Vec<String>` — extra arguments passed to the shell (needed for `wsl -d Ubuntu-24.04`, etc.)
- `command: Option<String>` — freeform command string as alternative to shell+shell_args. When set, shell/shell_args are ignored and shell integration is skipped by default. Good for docker exec, ssh, etc.
- `shell_integration: Option<bool>` — explicit override; `None` = auto-detect (true for shell, false for command)
- `icon: Option<String>` — Nerd Font glyph for the launcher tile (e.g. "󰨊")
- `color: Option<String>` — hex color for the tile background (e.g. "#2563eb")

**Example config**:
```toml
[profiles.powershell]
shell = "pwsh"
icon = "󰨊"
color = "#2563eb"

[profiles.docker-dev]
command = "docker exec -it dev-container bash"
icon = "󰡨"
color = "#0db7ed"
shell_integration = false

[profiles.ssh-prod]
command = "ssh user@prod-server"
icon = ""
color = "#e5a00d"
shell_integration = false

[profiles.wsl-ubuntu]
shell = "wsl"
shell_args = ["-d", "Ubuntu-24.04"]
icon = ""
```

**Validation**: exactly one of `shell` or `command` should be set. Warn if both are specified (command wins). Update existing profile round-trip tests.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: Clear task spec with exact line numbers made implementation fast. Existing `ColorsConfig::parse_hex` reused for color validation. Raw string delimiter `r##"..."##` needed for TOML strings containing `"#`.
- What was unclear: Nothing significant — the task was well-scoped.
close_reason: ProfileConfig extended with shell_args, command, shell_integration, icon, color

title: Shell profile launcher overlay
description:
A visual overlay panel (Ctrl+Shift+L or similar) that shows configured shell profiles as a grid of clickable tiles. Each tile has a large Nerd Font glyph icon, colored background, and profile name. Arrow key navigation, Enter to spawn, Esc to dismiss.

**Behavior**:
- New pane inherits the focused pane's cwd (same as current split behavior)
- Profile selection builds a `SpawnOptions` from the profile config and splits the focused pane
- A "Default" tile at the top spawns with the inherited shell (replaces the mental model of bare "split pane")
- Uses `OverlayMode::Launcher` following the same pattern as Help/Settings/TrustEscalation overlays

**Visual design**:
- Centered grid of tiles, 3D-ish look via beveled rects (brighter top edge, darker bottom edge, or gradient fill via tiny-skia)
- Large Nerd Font glyph per tile (e.g.  bash, 󰨊 powershell, 󰡨 docker,  ssh,  linux)
- Per-tile background color from profile config, falling back to chrome palette default
- Profile name rendered below the icon

**Subtasks**: extend ProfileConfig, profile→SpawnOptions resolver, launcher overlay rendering, MCP+CLI `profile` param.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: The feature was already fully implemented across all layers (ProfileConfig, launcher overlay, keybindings, daemon IPC, CLI, MCP). Only CLAUDE.md documentation updates were needed.
- What was unclear: The issue was marked "open" and "ready" but all code was already in place and passing CI. The missing piece was solely the docs:update requirement in the CLAUDE.md files.
close_reason: Shell profile launcher overlay with tile grid, Nerd Font icons, beveled tiles, arrow key nav, Ctrl+Shift+L keybinding, daemon SplitPane.profile parameter wired through MCP + CLI. All code was already implemented; this commit adds the required CLAUDE.md documentation across 4 crates.

title: Multi-daemon architecture: run native daemons per environment instead of cross-boundary hacks
description:
**Context**: The Windows→WSL2 single-daemon architecture requires extensive cross-boundary workarounds: UNC path translation, PollWatcher fallbacks for SMB metadata caching, WSL process detector probe mode, PID namespace mismatch handling, SESSION_MAX_AGE differences, JSONL tailer home directory resolution. Every new observability feature hits the same class of bugs (tn-zw2r, tn-hprs, tn-ag7d, tn-62lq, etc).

**Proposal**: Instead of one daemon bridging Windows↔WSL, run a native daemon per environment:
- `therminal-daemon` on Windows — PowerShell/cmd panes, native Windows observability
- `therminal-daemon` on WSL — bash panes, native Linux observability, Claude harness works perfectly with no UNC paths

**Two options (not mutually exclusive):**

1. **Two independent instances** — each daemon exposes its own MCP server. The orchestrating agent connects to both. Zero new architecture, just update MCP to handle multiple server connections gracefully. Two GUI windows (or one GUI connecting to both as pane sources).

2. **Federated daemon** — one GUI connects to multiple daemons (local + WSL + remote SSH). The daemon-client protocol already supports remote connections. GUI composites panes from all sources into one window.

**What this eliminates:**
- `therminal_runtime::wsl` module (detect_default_distro, detect_wsl_home, linux_to_unc)
- PollWatcher fallback for UNC paths
- WSL process detector probe mode (wsl.exe -e ps)
- State file PID mismatch workarounds
- SMB metadata caching bugs (entire class disappears)

**What this enables:**
- Same pattern extends to SSH remotes — a third daemon on a remote box, same MCP surface
- Each daemon is simple and native to its environment
- Cross-device orchestration for free

**Decision needed**: Is this worth pursuing now (to simplify the codebase), or later (when the single-daemon approach becomes untenable)?
close_reason: Won't do for WSL/Docker. WT research (tn-s7nl) shows marker-primary architecture eliminates the WSL boundary pain that motivated this. Docker follows the same pattern — OSC markers flow through PTY transparently. The clean path forward is: enrich hook scripts with an `environment` field (container name, SSH host, distro) so the daemon knows where signals originate without crossing boundaries. Multi-daemon only revisit when SSH remotes with full local MCP + file watching become a real requirement — and even then, the hook-script-with-environment approach may suffice.

title: Subagent panes not reclaimed on Windows→WSL2 (mtime staleness check fails on UNC paths)
description:
**Problem**: Subagent JsonlTail panes spawn correctly but never close when the subagent finishes. The swarm watcher's reclaim logic relies on `std::fs::metadata(path).modified()` to detect 30s of mtime staleness, but this likely returns cached/incorrect values on Windows UNC paths (`\\wsl.localhost\...`), so the staleness timeout never fires.

Additionally, if the pane was spawned via the hook-driven path (`SubagentStarted` daemon event) but the `SubagentStopped` event doesn't arrive or isn't wired to reclaim, the file scanner's staleness check is the only fallback — and it fails on UNC paths for the same reason.

Same class of cross-filesystem issue as tn-zw2r (notify watcher not delivering events on UNC paths).

**Files**: `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/swarm_watcher.rs` (staleness check at line ~454), `crates/therminal-app/src/window/pane_ops/workspace_ops/swarm.rs` (reclaim handler)
close_reason: Fixed: added file_mtime_via_handle() that opens the file to get fresh mtime, bypassing Windows SMB metadata caching on UNC paths. Applied to both the freshness gate (discovery) and the staleness check (reclaim) in swarm_watcher.rs. Commit e361efc.

title: Archive historical agent observability port doc and point to core-vs-harness ADR
description: Archive the old thermal-desktop observability port design note, add a clear historical note at the top, and point readers to the current architecture rule that keeps harness-specific observability out of therminal core.
acceptance_criteria: The historical design doc is moved or clearly archived under docs, includes a top-level note that it is historical, and points to the active ADR describing the core vs harness boundary. Changes are committed and pushed.
close_reason: Archived the historical agent observability port note under docs/archive, left a stub at the original path, and added ADR 0005 to capture the active core-vs-harness boundary.

title: Capacity cache eviction on Removed uses file_stem which may not match cached session_id
description:
**Found by**: Codex code review of tn-igiz

**Code path**: crates/therminal-daemon/src/ensure.rs:457-458

ClaudeStateUpdate::Removed { path } evicts by path.file_stem(). But the capacity cache stores session_id as parsed from the file content, which prefers claude_session_id over session_id (state.rs:146-154). The hook can name files from a workdir hash or tmux pane ID before reading the real Claude session UUID sidecar (state-tracker.sh:75, 331).

When the file stem differs from the claude_session_id stored in the cache, remove_by_session_id() is a no-op and the entry survives until the 60s TTL.

**Fix**: Carry the resolved session_id in ClaudeStateUpdate::Removed instead of just the path. The poller already has the parsed state before removal — capture the session_id at that point.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: the sessions HashMap in ClaudeStatePoller already holds parsed state before removal, so capturing session_id from the removed entry is straightforward and zero-cost
- What was unclear: tool_call_hotspots.rs comment said Removed "carries only the file path" — now that the field is present, the index can evict immediately without waiting for retain_live; updated accordingly
close_reason: Removed event carries session_id, cache eviction matches correctly

title: SwarmWatchScope::Current degrades to All during startup — cross-instance leakage window
description:
**Found by**: Codex code review of tn-igiz

**Code path**: crates/therminal-app/src/pane/swarm_watcher.rs:278-287

When owned (the set of known session IDs) is empty, the Current scope silently falls back to admitting all subagents. This creates a startup window where unrelated sessions from other therminal instances can spawn panes. The set is populated asynchronously from PaneStatus.claude_session_id (workspace.rs:61), so there is always a race window.

**Fix options**:
1. Add a bounded grace period (e.g. 10s after startup) where Current scope queues discovered subagents instead of admitting them, then admits only those whose parent_session_id matches once session IDs arrive
2. Suppress subagent admission entirely until at least one session_id is known, with a timeout fallback to All
3. Accept the window as a known limitation and document it (pragmatic — the impact is a briefly visible pane that gets reclaimed)
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: Extracting `filter_grace_queue` as a pure helper made the grace-period replay logic fully unit-testable without running the watcher thread. The queue-and-replay approach (option 1) was cleaner than the simpler suppress-all alternative because it avoids losing legitimate subagents that appear in the first 5s window.
- What was unclear: Whether to use `mem::take` or clone for the queue drain — `mem::take` was the right call since we consume the queue exactly once on resolution.
close_reason: bounded grace period prevents cross-instance leakage at startup

title: Claude tool-call hotspot resolver uses host path semantics for Linux absolute paths
description:
Broken path
- `crates/therminal-harness-claude/src/tool_call_hotspots.rs:203-257` turns `Read(...)` / `Edit(...)` / `Update(...)` lines into hotspots and stores a pre-resolved absolute path in `resolved_text`.
- `resolve_against()` at `263-283` decides whether the tool argument is already absolute via `Path::is_absolute()` and otherwise joins it against `agent_cwd`.
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window/mouse.rs:828-840` prefers `resolved_text` over the visible text on click.

Why it fails today
- The resolver is supposed to understand Claude paths printed from inside WSL (`/home/...`, `/tmp/...`), but it uses host `std::path` semantics.
- On Windows builds, Linux absolute paths are not guaranteed to be treated as absolute by `Path::is_absolute()`, so a printed WSL path can be re-joined against `agent_cwd` and turned into nonsense before the click handler ever sees it.
- The tests are Unix-shaped only (`crates/therminal-harness-claude/src/tool_call_hotspots.rs:521-526`); there is no Windows-target regression coverage for `/home/...` inputs.

Silent vs visible
- Visible: clicking a Claude tool-call hotspot opens the wrong file or fails with a file-not-found path.
- Silent: the bad path is baked into `resolved_text`, so later click handling cannot recover from the mistake.

Proposed fix
- Make the resolver platform-independent for agent paths: treat `starts_with('/')` as Linux-absolute and reuse the existing Windows-absolute guard for `C:\...` / UNC.
- Avoid `Path::is_absolute()` for this boundary-crossing case.
- Add Windows-target tests for `/home/...`, `/tmp/...`, and mixed Windows/WSL path inputs.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: Clean extraction of the platform check into `is_absolute_any_platform()` — minimal diff, no behavioral change on Linux/macOS, fixes the Windows cross-boundary case. The existing test structure made it easy to add targeted coverage.
- What was unclear: Nothing significant — the bug was well-scoped and the fix was straightforward. The `starts_with('/')` check is the correct portable approach since `Path::is_absolute()` on Windows only recognizes Windows-style absolute paths.
close_reason: platform-independent path check, added Windows-target tests

title: Windows+WSL Claude observability still hard-depends on UNC filesystem watching and scans
description:
Fragile path
- `crates/therminal-harness-claude/src/state.rs:30-52` rewrites `/tmp/{claude,codex,copilot}-state` to `\\wsl.localhost\<distro>\tmp\...` on Windows.
- `crates/therminal-harness-claude/src/state.rs:545-599` requires `notify::recommended_watcher` to watch those directories; if every watch fails, the whole harness pipeline is disabled.
- `crates/therminal-harness-claude/src/jsonl_tailer.rs:292-317`, `346-360`, and `600-630` discover session and subagent JSONLs by `exists/read_dir` walks under `\\wsl.localhost\<distro>\home\<user>\.claude\projects`.
- `crates/therminal-harness-claude/src/wsl_paths.rs:13-16` asserts that `notify` works reliably over WSL UNC paths, but there is no runtime validation/fallback beyond warnings and empty results.

Why it is fragile today
- The Windows-native daemon still depends on direct traversal of WSL UNC paths for the state poller and transcript discovery.
- When distro/home detection is wrong, UNC access is flaky, or `notify` cannot watch the UNC path, the pipeline quietly degrades to "no events" / `None` / empty scans.
- Hook-push is not complete enough to cover these losses today (see audit issue for partial hook-push coverage).

Silent vs visible
- Silent: `resolve_session_jsonl()` returns `None`, subagent discovery returns `[]`, the event pipeline starts but stays dark, or the harness is disabled except for logs.
- Visible: missing badges/titles/transcripts/events on Windows+WSL, often with only a generic toast like `no Claude session transcript found`.

Proposed fix
- Demote UNC polling/scanning from load-bearing to best-effort on Windows+WSL.
- Make the primary Windows+WSL path an in-band signal source: richer PTY OSC markers or complete hook-push.
- Keep UNC file access only for optional history/transcript recovery, not for live pane association or current-state correctness.
notes: ## Retro\n- What worked: The existing code already had partial graceful degradation (individual dir failures were non-fatal since tn-966s). Adding PollWatcher fallback was straightforward because notify 8's `Watcher` trait is object-safe and both `RecommendedWatcher` and `PollWatcher` implement `Send`.\n- What was unclear: Whether `PollWatcher` can actually watch UNC paths — it polls via `std::fs` metadata which may also fail on UNC. The fallback chain (recommended -> poll -> skip) covers this case by trying both and accepting that file polling may be fully unavailable on some Windows+WSL configurations, relying on OSC 1341 markers as primary.
close_reason: UNC demoted to best-effort, PollWatcher fallback added

title: Swarm watcher current-scope ownership filter is broken on Windows+WSL
description:
Broken path
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/swarm_watcher.rs:251-307` reads `/tmp/claude-code-state/*.json` and collects owned Claude sessions by comparing each state-file `pid` against `descendants_of(pane_pids)`.
- `descendants_of` is built from host-side `sysinfo` process IDs (`crates/therminal-app/src/pane/swarm_watcher.rs:213-248`).
- The state files still carry the WSL hook PID written by `resources/hooks/state-tracker.sh:297` (`PPID`/`$$` inside WSL), not a host Windows PID.
- `SwarmWatchScope::Current` then filters subagent JSONLs through `subagent_in_scope` (`crates/therminal-app/src/pane/swarm_watcher.rs:341-350`) inside the spawn loop at `414-423`.

Why it fails today
- On Windows-native therminal with WSL panes, host pane PIDs and WSL state-file PIDs are different namespaces.
- `collect_owned_sessions()` therefore returns an empty set for real in-scope sessions.
- With `swarm_watch_scope = "current"`, the filesystem fallback skips the subagent as "out of scope" even when it belongs to the current pane.

Silent vs visible
- Silent: the watcher just never emits `SpawnSubagent` for the current pane when scope is `Current`.
- Visible: hook/file fallback auto-tile appears randomly broken on Windows+WSL unless the user switches to scope `All`.

Proposed fix
- Remove PID-descendant ownership as the Windows+WSL ownership test.
- Prefer a session-based ownership source: either daemon hook events already resolved to a pane, or PTY/OSC markers that bind `session_id` to the pane directly.
- If a session-based source is unavailable, disable `Current` scope on Windows+WSL instead of pretending the PID comparison is meaningful.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: The existing `PaneStatus.claude_session_id` field (populated by the daemon forwarder from `DaemonEvent::AgentChanged`) already solved the cross-namespace resolution problem — the swarm watcher just needed to consume it instead of doing its own PID-based resolution. Clean replacement of ~100 lines of PID-descendant + state-file-parsing code with ~10 lines of session-ID set lookup.
- What was unclear: Whether removing `root_pid()` and `collect_all_root_pids()` was safe — confirmed they were only used by the PID-based ownership path. The fallback behavior when session IDs aren't yet available (empty set) required careful thought to avoid silently filtering everything vs admitting everything.
close_reason: session-based ownership replaces PID descendancy

title: WSL audit: cwd fallback has no path normalization — trailing slash mismatch is silent
description:
**Code path**: `crates/therminal-daemon/src/ensure.rs:347-348`

```rust
if cwd == wd {
    return Some(entry.pane_id);
}
```

**Impact**: The cwd fallback (tn-ixfy) is the primary resolution path on Windows+WSL since PID matching always fails. It compares `state.working_dir` (from Claude hook) against `mgr.pane_cwd()` (from OSC 7) using exact string equality. No normalization is done:
- `/home/user/project` vs `/home/user/project/` — silent miss
- Symlink-resolved vs unresolved paths — silent miss
- Case differences (unlikely on Linux but possible) — silent miss

**Fails**: Completely silently. The capacity entry is dropped, MCP consumers see stale/missing data. The only log is when the fallback *succeeds* (`debug!` at line 349-352), not when it fails.

**Proposed fix**: 
1. Trim trailing slashes from both paths before comparing
2. Log at INFO when cwd fallback fails (include both paths for debugging)
3. Consider canonicalizing or normalizing paths
notes:
## files_touched
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/ensure.rs:347-348 (cwd comparison)

## prepared.prompt

## Context
The cwd fallback in `ensure.rs` is the primary pane resolution path on Windows+WSL since PID matching always fails (tn-y2d8). It uses exact string equality — a trailing slash mismatch (`/home/user/project` vs `/home/user/project/`) silently drops the capacity entry. No log is emitted on failure.

## Task
1. **Normalize paths before comparison**: Trim trailing slashes (and on Windows, trailing backslashes) from both `state.working_dir` and `mgr.pane_cwd()` before the `==` check at line 347-348.

2. **Log on failure**: When the cwd fallback fails (no matching pane), log at INFO with both the state working_dir and all available pane CWDs. Currently only success is logged.

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/ensure.rs:347-348`

## When Done
Run `cargo test -p therminal-daemon`. Close: `bd close tn-019y --reason "normalized cwd comparison, added failure logging"`
close_reason: normalized cwd comparison, added failure logging

title: WSL audit: pid_is_alive() is a no-op on Windows — stale sessions linger 2 hours
description:
**Code path**: `crates/therminal-harness-claude/src/state.rs:459-464`

```rust
#[cfg(not(unix))]
fn pid_is_alive(_pid: i64) -> bool {
    true // always assumes alive on Windows
}
```

**Impact**: On Windows native, dead Claude sessions are never pruned by PID liveness. The only cleanup is timestamp-based: `RECENT_UPDATE_GRACE` (120s) then `SESSION_MAX_AGE` (2 hours). A crashed Claude session whose state file persists on disk will keep generating `Upserted` events for up to 2 hours.

**Why it matters**: Combined with the PID mismatch issue, stale sessions pollute the capacity cache. The cwd fallback may match the wrong pane if a new Claude session starts in the same directory.

**Fails**: Silently — stale entries just linger. The `session_is_dead` check at line 466-497 short-circuits at line 483 (`return false`) when `pid > 0` and `pid_is_alive` returns `true`.

**Proposed fix**: On Windows, use `OpenProcess(PROCESS_QUERY_LIMITED_INFORMATION, false, pid)` + `GetExitCodeProcess` to check liveness. But this only works for Windows-side PIDs, not WSL PIDs. For WSL PIDs, could shell out to `wsl.exe -d <distro> -e kill -0 <pid>` but that's expensive. Better fix: shorten `SESSION_MAX_AGE` on Windows to something more aggressive (e.g., 5 minutes) and rely on timestamp freshness.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: Clean separation of cfg(unix)/cfg(windows)/cfg(not(any(unix,windows))) guards. The OpenProcess + GetLastError approach gives genuine PID liveness for native Windows processes while the shortened SESSION_MAX_AGE (5 min) handles the WSL PID namespace gap. RECENT_UPDATE_GRACE (120s) protects live sessions whose state files are actively updated, so returning false for WSL PIDs that don't map to Win32 PIDs is safe.
- What was unclear: Whether to return true (conservative) or false (aggressive) when OpenProcess fails for a PID that might be a WSL PID. Resolved by recognizing that RECENT_UPDATE_GRACE already protects live sessions, so false is safe — dead sessions get pruned faster, live ones are protected by their active state file updates.
close_reason: shortened SESSION_MAX_AGE on Windows, implemented Windows-native pid check

title: Subagent auto-close relies on 30s staleness fallback — hook-push CLI missing
description:
The fast auto-close path for subagent JSONL tail panes is non-functional. The pipeline requires:

1. Claude Code `SubagentStart`/`SubagentStop` hooks → call `therminal agent-event push`
2. CLI sends `IpcRequest::PushAgentEvent` to daemon
3. Daemon emits `DaemonEvent::SubagentStarted`/`SubagentStopped`
4. GUI forwarder feeds `SwarmDebouncer` → pane closes in ~1.5s

**Step 2 is missing**: the `therminal agent-event push` CLI command documented in `therminal-harness-claude/CLAUDE.md` was never implemented. The `state-tracker.sh` hooks handle subagent events by updating a count file in `/tmp/claude-code-state/subagents/` but never notify the daemon.

Result: all auto-close relies on the file scanner's `STALENESS_TIMEOUT` (30s). Fast subagents (6-9s) leave panes open for ~30s after completion. With 3 concurrent subagents finishing within seconds of each other, users observe 2-of-3 closing and 1 lingering (timing edge).
design:
Implement `therminal agent-event push` CLI subcommand that constructs a `HookSignal` and sends it as `IpcRequest::PushAgentEvent` to the daemon. Then wire state-tracker.sh `subagent-start`/`subagent-stop` cases to also call `therminal agent-event push --event subagent_start/subagent_stop` with the relevant fields (`--agent-id`, `--parent-session-id`, `--jsonl-path`).

Alternative: have the daemon's `ClaudeStatePoller` detect `subagent_count` changes in the state files it already watches and emit events directly — avoids the CLI round-trip.
acceptance_criteria: Subagent JSONL tail panes auto-close within ~2s of the subagent finishing (hook-push path active). Daemon logs show `SubagentStarted`/`SubagentStopped` events. File scanner remains as fallback.
close_reason: Implemented `therminal agent-event push` CLI subcommand and wired state-tracker.sh SubagentStart/SubagentStop hooks to call it (fire-and-forget). Full pipeline: hook script → CLI → IpcRequest::PushAgentEvent → daemon → SubagentStarted/SubagentStopped → SwarmDebouncer → auto-close in ~1.5s.

title: Stale swarm_panes entry blocks re-spawn after manual close
description:
When a user manually closes a JSONL swarm pane (Ctrl+W, context menu, close button), `close_pane_by_id` removes it from the layout tree but never cleans up `swarm_panes`. The `reclaim_subagent_pane` path does remove from the map, but that's only called when the agent exits via the debouncer.

If the user closes the pane and the agent later restarts (or another spawn event fires), `swarm_panes.contains_key(&agent_id)` at swarm.rs:62 returns true and silently blocks the re-spawn.

**Fix**: Add `self.swarm_panes.retain(|_, pid| *pid != target_id);` in `close_pane_by_id` after the pane is removed from the layout tree (close_ops.rs, after `wm.remove_pane_any`).
acceptance_criteria:
- Manually closing a swarm JSONL pane removes its `swarm_panes` entry
- A subsequent spawn event for the same `agent_id` creates a new pane
- `reclaim_subagent_pane` still works as before (no double-remove panic)
close_reason: Fixed in b98a06f — swarm_panes cleanup on manual pane close.

title: GPU render path for JsonlTail panes (shadow Term or custom draw)
description: tn-bjvl landed the structured JSONL parser, keyboard nav, and expand/collapse — but JsonlTail panes return term() -> None so the GPU render path skips them. Nothing displays on screen. Fix: either (a) feed formatted_content() ANSI output into a local shadow Term (like RemotePty does), so the existing grid_renderer draws it; or (b) add a custom render path for non-Term panes. Option (a) is simpler and already proven by RemotePty. Reference: ~/projects/thermal-desktop/crates/thermal-conductor/src/viewer.rs and window/render.rs have a custom render approach worth comparing.
notes: ## Retro\n- What worked: The RemotePty shadow Term pattern was a perfect template — same Term creation, same `term()` return path, zero changes needed in grid_renderer.rs. The `formatted_content()` ANSI output fed directly into `Processor::advance()` with no format conversion.\n- What was unclear: Initially imported `alacritty_terminal::vte::ansi` which collided with the local `mod ansi` (ANSI color constants). Renamed the import to `vte_ansi` to resolve. Also needed `#[allow(clippy::type_complexity)]` on the updated `spawn_jsonl_watcher` return type.
close_reason: JsonlTail panes now render on screen via shadow Term fed by formatted_content(). Shadow term created with zero scrollback, refreshed on every state change (new JSONL lines, scroll, expand/collapse, resize). GPU renderer picks it up with no changes to grid_renderer.rs.

title: (BUG) Pane header shows 'pane 1' instead of Claude session title/status
description: When running a Claude Code session in daemon mode on Windows+WSL, the pane header stays as 'pane 1' instead of showing the session title and agent status badge. The claude_cwd module gets session metadata from ClaudeStatePoller but the data isn't reaching the pane header. May be a PID mapping issue — the daemon runs as Windows native but Claude runs inside WSL, so the state poller's PID-to-pane resolution may fail. Reference screenshot: /mnt/c/Users/marci/Downloads/screenshots/Screenshot 2026-04-14 133412.png
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: The session_id-based fallback is clean and minimal — no new function signatures needed on spawn_remote_pane, just piggybacking on PaneStatus which is already shared with the forwarder.
- What was unclear: Initially considered passing ClaudeCwdTracker to the forwarder thread, but realized PaneStatus already bridges the forwarder-to-render gap. Also considered adding session_id to AgentEntry but that would change too many call sites.

## Implementation
- Added `session_id: Option<String>` to `DaemonEvent::AgentChanged` (backward-compatible via `serde(default)`)
- Daemon's `process_detector_task` populates session_id from `PaneCapacityCache` when emitting AgentChanged
- Added `claude_session_id: Option<String>` to `PaneStatus` (set by forwarder on AgentChanged)
- Added `chrome_meta_for_session()` to `ClaudeCwdTracker` for direct session_id lookup
- Render path falls back to session-based lookup when PID-based lookup returns None (4 sites: render.rs, 3x render_driver.rs)
- 3 new tests covering session-based lookup and the PID-mismatch scenario
close_reason: Pane header shows Claude session title via hook-push metadata bypassing PID mismatch

title: Structured JSONL viewer with expandable tool uses (thermal-desktop port)
description: Port the structured JSONL viewer TUI from thermal-desktop that renders agent events as readable rows with expandable tool_use/tool_result sections. thermal-desktop had this working well — reference: ~/projects/thermal-desktop/crates/thermal-conductor/. The current JsonlTail backend (tn-14c0) has basic format-aware rendering but needs the richer interactive display: collapsible tool calls, syntax-highlighted content, timestamps, token counts. This is the next evolution of tn-14c0's structured rendering.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: The thermal-desktop viewer.rs and session_log.rs were excellent references. The CC nested JSONL format parser ported cleanly. The existing JsonlTailState architecture was well-suited for extension -- adding structured events, display rows, and keyboard handling fit naturally into the shared Mutex pattern.
- What was unclear: The JsonlTail backend currently returns None from backend.term(), so the main render path skips it entirely. The structured viewer enhances the data model and formatted_content() output, but actual on-screen rendering requires a separate follow-up to either feed ANSI into a local Term or add a custom GPU render path for non-Term panes.
close_reason: Structured JSONL viewer with expandable tool sections ported from thermal-desktop

title: MCP tools for agent observability: create_tail, timeline toggle
description:
Add remaining MCP tools for the observability surface so external agents can drive views without therminal shipping harness-specific code.

## Tools to add
- `terminal.panes.create_tail({ path, format })` — spawn a JSONL tail pane (depends on JSONL tail backend)
- `terminal.widgets.timeline.toggle({ visible })` — show/hide the agent timeline widget (depends on timeline widget)

## Already done
- `terminal.agents.get_status` / `get_details` / `get_cadence` / `get_session_detail` — agent state queries exist
- `therminal://claude/events` — live event stream exists
- `therminal://agents/events` — agent lifecycle events exist

## Trust tiers
- Observation tools (create_tail, get_state): Sandboxed
- Widget toggles (timeline.toggle): Supervised

## Integration docs
- Update `docs/integrations/` with setup snippets for each harness showing how to install hooks and configure state file paths
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: The create_tail stub was already mostly complete from tn-14c0; just needed path validation, shell-quoting, and doc cleanup. The timeline.toggle tool followed the existing TrustEscalation broadcast pattern cleanly.
- What was unclear: Nothing major. The DaemonEvent exhaustive match in control.rs needed updating but was easy to find via grep for SubagentStopped.
close_reason: Completed create_tail (path validation, shell-quoting) and added terminal.widgets.timeline.toggle MCP tool (DaemonEvent broadcast, Supervised tier). Tool count bumped 31->32, all CI passes.

title: JSONL tail pane backend for structured agent state viewing
description:
New PaneBackendKind::JsonlTail variant that watches a file and renders structured rows in the existing terminal grid. This replaces the current approach of spawning a shell pane running `tail -F` with a native, format-aware renderer.

## Design
- New `PaneBackendKind::JsonlTail { path, parser, rows }` variant implementing `PaneBackend` trait
- File watcher (notify crate) for live updates
- Format-aware JSONL parser: known schemas (claude-state, codex-state) get column layouts and color schemes; unknown files render as raw colored JSON
- Format negotiation: if `format` is omitted, sniff the first line; if it matches a known schema, use it

## Files to touch
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/backend.rs` — add JsonlTail variant + PaneBackend impl
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/jsonl_tail.rs` (new) — file watcher, parser, row layout
- MCP tool `terminal.panes.create_tail({ path, format })` in daemon

## Benefits
- No shell process overhead per subagent pane
- Structured rendering with color-coded fields instead of raw JSON text
- Native scroll/search integration
- Works on Windows without needing WSL `tail` command
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Swarm panes currently spawn `tail -F` in a shell process to view subagent JSONL files. This is fragile on Windows (no `tail` without WSL), wastes a shell process per pane, and renders raw JSON without structure. A native JSONL tail backend would render structured, color-coded rows directly in the terminal grid.

## Task
Add a `PaneBackendKind::JsonlTail` variant that watches a file and renders structured rows in the grid.

### Step 1: Add backend variant
In `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/backend.rs` (lines 92-138), add `JsonlTail { path: PathBuf, watcher: ..., rows: Vec<...> }` to `PaneBackendKind`. Implement `PaneBackend` trait methods:
- `write_input()` — no-op (read-only pane)
- `resize()` — update column width for row layout
- `get_content()` — return formatted row text
- `backend_type()` — return `"jsonl_tail"`

### Step 2: Create file watcher and parser
New file `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/jsonl_tail.rs`:
- Use `notify` crate file watcher (already a dependency) for live updates
- Read file from offset, parse each line as JSON
- Format-aware rendering: detect Claude agent event schema (`type`, `message`, `toolUseResult` fields) and render with color-coded columns. Unknown formats render as pretty-printed colored JSON.
- Maintain a ring buffer of parsed rows (configurable max, default 1000)

### Step 3: Wire into pane creation
The swarm watcher's `spawn_subagent_pane` in `workspace_ops/swarm.rs` currently spawns a shell pane and writes `tail -F`. Update to create a `JsonlTail` backend instead when the target is a `.jsonl` file.

### Step 4: Add MCP tool stub
Add `terminal.panes.create_tail({ path, format })` tool definition in `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp/tools.rs`. Can be a stub that creates a pane with a startup command initially; full native backend integration comes when the daemon supports the new backend kind via IPC.

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/backend.rs` (lines 43-273 — PaneBackend trait, PaneBackendKind)
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/jsonl_tail.rs` (new — file watcher, parser, formatter)
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window/pane_ops/workspace_ops/swarm.rs` (lines 59-195 — spawn_subagent_pane)
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/spawn.rs` (line 319 — Terminal backend creation pattern)
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp/tools.rs` (tool definitions)

## When Done
Close issue: bd close tn-14c0 --reason "JSONL tail pane backend renders structured agent events"
## Retro
- What worked: Clear separation between native JsonlTail backend (local mode) and tail -F stub (daemon mode). Re-using existing PtyOutput UserEvent for wake signal was simpler than adding a new variant.
- What was unclear: SpawnPaneResult was initially not Deserialize-derived, needed to add Deserialize to parse it from the stub handler's re-wrapped response.
close_reason: JSONL tail pane backend renders structured agent events with color-coded type badges, timestamps, tool names. Local-mode uses native JsonlTail backend with notify file watcher; daemon-mode retains tail -F fallback. MCP tool stub terminal.panes.create_tail added.

title: WSL probe: scope agent detection to per-pane process subtree
description:
The WSL process detector probe (`scan_wsl`) runs `wsl.exe -d <distro> -e ps -eo pid=,ppid=,comm=,args=` which returns ALL processes in the distro. Unlike the sysinfo path (which BFS-walks from the pane's `shell_pid`), the WSL probe has no per-pane scoping — every WSL pane sees the same global process list.

Current mitigation (tn-alpb): scan coalescing (one `wsl.exe` call per distro per tick) + PID dedup in `apply_scan_results` (first pane to claim an agent PID wins). This prevents the "every pane gets a claude badge" blowup but assigns the agent to an arbitrary pane, not necessarily the one actually running it.

**Root cause**: The Windows-side `shell_pid` (the PID of `wsl.exe`) cannot be used to walk the WSL-side process tree because there is no exposed Windows→WSL PID mapping.

**Proposed fix**: Capture the WSL-side shell PID at pane startup via shell integration, then use it for tree-scoped scanning.

1. Add an OSC sequence to the therminal WSL bash rcfile (`wsl_bash_rcfile.bash`) that emits the shell PID: `printf '\033]7337;%s\007' "$$"`
2. Register OSC 7337 in the daemon's `TherminalInterceptor` to capture the WSL-side shell PID into the pane's metadata (new field on `Pane` or a tag)
3. Expose the WSL-side PID via `pane_detector_specs()` so the process detector can use it
4. Change `parse_wsl_ps` to accept a root PID and do BFS tree-walking using the ppid column (which it already parses but currently discards)
5. `scan_wsl` uses the WSL-side shell PID as the tree root instead of scanning globally

**Alternative approaches considered**:
- Env var (`THERMINAL_PANE_ID`) propagated through WSL + `/proc/*/environ` matching — works but requires per-pane env probing on each tick, slower than tree-walk
- PID namespace mapping via `/proc/*/status` NSpid — WSL2 PID namespace doesn't expose Windows PIDs
- Process group / session ID matching — no reliable way to map Windows-side groups to WSL-side groups

The scan coalescing + PID dedup from tn-alpb is the interim fix; this issue is the proper per-pane scoping.
design:
## Implementation Plan

### Step 1: Shell integration (WSL rcfile)
File: `resources/shell-integration/wsl_bash_rcfile.bash` (or wherever the WSL-specific rcfile lives)

Add near the top, after the guard:
```bash
# Emit WSL-side shell PID so the daemon can scope process detection
# to this pane's subtree.
printf '\033]7337;%s\007' "$$"
```

### Step 2: OSC handler registration
File: `crates/therminal-terminal/src/interceptor.rs`

Register OSC 7337 in the handler registry. When received, store the PID value on the pane's metadata. The daemon's `Pane` struct needs a new `wsl_shell_pid: Option<u32>` field.

### Step 3: Surface WSL shell PID in detector specs
File: `crates/therminal-daemon/src/session/manager.rs`

Change `pane_detector_specs()` to return a 4-tuple: `(pane_id, shell_pid, shell_command, wsl_shell_pid)`. The process detector task uses `wsl_shell_pid` when available.

### Step 4: Tree-walking in parse_wsl_ps
File: `crates/therminal-terminal/src/process_detector.rs`

Change `parse_wsl_ps(stdout) -> Vec<DetectedAgent>` to `parse_wsl_ps_tree(stdout, root_pid: u32) -> Vec<DetectedAgent>`:
1. Parse ALL rows into a `HashMap<u32, (u32, String, String)>` (pid -> ppid, comm, args)
2. Build a children map: `HashMap<u32, Vec<u32>>`
3. BFS from `root_pid` down through children
4. Classify each visited process

Keep the existing `parse_wsl_ps` (global scan) as fallback for panes that haven't received the OSC yet.

### Step 5: Wire it together in scan_wsl
If `self.wsl_shell_pid` is set, call `parse_wsl_ps_tree(stdout, wsl_shell_pid)`.
Otherwise fall back to the global `parse_wsl_ps` + dedup path.

### Step 6: Remove scan coalescing (optional)
Once tree-walking is in place, each pane's scan is correctly scoped, so the coalescing cache in `tick_once` becomes unnecessary (though keeping it as a perf optimization — one `wsl.exe` call shared across panes — is still valuable).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] WSL pane bash rcfile emits OSC 7337 with `$$` at startup
- [ ] Daemon captures WSL-side shell PID from OSC 7337 into pane metadata
- [ ] `pane_detector_specs()` surfaces the WSL shell PID
- [ ] `parse_wsl_ps_tree` does BFS from root PID using ppid column
- [ ] Agent detection registers only on the pane whose subtree contains the agent
- [ ] Multiple Claude sessions in different WSL panes each get their own badge on the correct pane
- [ ] Panes without OSC 7337 (non-therminal shells, ssh, etc.) fall back to global scan + dedup
- [ ] Existing tests pass; new table-driven tests for `parse_wsl_ps_tree` cover subtree isolation
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: The cwd `Arc<Mutex<...>>` pattern for sharing state between the PTY reader thread and the Pane struct was a clean model to follow for wsl_shell_pid. The WSL stdout caching optimization (fetch once per distro, parse per-pane) avoids redundant subprocess calls while still giving per-pane subtree results.
- What was unclear: The working tree had pre-existing staged changes from other issues (tn-5dpv scrollback keybindings, osc_7337 scaffolding in app InterceptorConfig initializers). These were committed by a prior agent session alongside the tn-ttie work in commit 60110c6. The commit message attribution is slightly misleading (says "tn-5dpv" in title but includes all tn-ttie implementation).
close_reason: Implemented per-pane WSL process tree scoping via OSC 7337 shell PID + BFS tree-walking in parse_wsl_ps_tree

title: PgDown does not scroll to bottom of terminal after output flood
description: After events that produce rapid terminal output (e.g. Claude Code redrawing on window resize, or any fast-scrolling output), PgDown in the terminal does not reach the actual bottom of the scrollback buffer. The user has to close and restart the pane to see the current prompt. The scroll position or scrollback extent may not be updating correctly when large amounts of output arrive quickly. Shift+End (scroll to bottom) behavior should also be verified.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: The root cause hypothesis was correct -- PgDown/Shift+End were being forwarded to the PTY as escape sequences instead of being intercepted as GUI scroll commands. The fix was straightforward: add ScrollPageUp/ScrollPageDown/ScrollTop/ScrollBottom KeyAction variants with Shift+PageUp/PageDown/Home/End default bindings, and auto-snap to bottom on any PTY-bound keystroke while scrolled back (matching Alacritty/Kitty behavior).
- What was unclear: The tree had pre-existing compile errors from in-progress tn-ttie work (WslShellPid variant, osc_7337 field) that needed fixing before the workspace would build. These were not related to the scroll issue but blocked clippy/test verification.
close_reason: Added ScrollPageUp/ScrollPageDown/ScrollTop/ScrollBottom KeyAction variants with Shift+PageUp/PageDown/Home/End default keybindings. Added scroll_focused_pane() and focused_pane_is_scrolled_back() helpers in event_handler.rs. Any PTY-bound keystroke while scrolled back now auto-snaps the viewport to the live bottom (display_offset=0), matching Alacritty/Kitty behavior. Also fixed pre-existing compile errors from tn-ttie work (WslShellPid match arm, osc_7337 field, ref binding fix).

title: Settings overlay: unified font family selector (grid + UI)
description: Add a font family control to the settings overlay that changes both the terminal grid font (font.family) and the chrome UI font (font.ui_font_family) in unison. One setting, one change, everything re-skins. The grid font change requires rebuilding the glyph atlas and cell metrics (heavier than ui_font_family alone). CSD button icons should remain on the grid/monospace font regardless (already fixed). Consider a dropdown or text input with common font suggestions (JetBrains Mono, Fira Code, Cascadia Code, Iosevka, etc.) or free-text entry.
notes:
## Follow-up fixes (post-close)

Three issues found during manual testing and fixed in `d65aa97`:

1. **Font selector showed uninstalled fonts** — the static `FONT_FAMILY_OPTIONS` list included fonts the user didn't have. Uninstalled fonts fell back to a proportional system default, causing wide character spacing. Fix: filter the list at runtime by probing `cosmic_text::FontSystem::db().faces()` via `GridRenderer::is_font_available()`. The selector now shows only installed fonts.

2. **CSD button icons broke on some fonts** — close/maximize/minimize icons (≡ ─ □ ✕) followed `font.family`, so switching to a font lacking those glyphs broke the buttons. Fix: CSD icons now use `Family::Monospace` (generic) instead of following the user's grid font choice.

3. **SetFontFamily carried an index into the static array** — with the filtered list, indices no longer mapped to `FONT_FAMILY_OPTIONS`. Fix: changed `SetFontFamily(usize)` to `SetFontFamily(String)` so the command carries the actual font name.

Also added FiraCode Nerd Font and CaskaydiaCove Nerd Font to the options list (matching fonts from TabzChrome/fonts).
close_reason: Added unified font family selector to settings overlay. New Font section (first section in panel) with a Select control listing 10 common monospace fonts. Selecting a font sets both font.family and font.ui_font_family, triggering the full hot-reload path (glyph atlas rebuild + chrome text re-render). Added SetFontFamily command variant, FontFamily control binding, FONT_FAMILY_OPTIONS constant, font_family_index helper, rebuild_font_section method, and 7 new tests. All 42 settings overlay tests pass with updated section indices.

Related to tn-966s WSL pane observability — the process_detector_task should be activating WSL probe mode for WSL panes, but it may not be triggering for MCP-created panes (vs GUI-created splits).

If tn-s8w3 lands, this becomes unnecessary for Claude. Can be revisited for non-hook agents (Codex, Aider) if needed.
close_reason: Superseded by hook-driven auto-tile (tn-s8w3). Hooks provide accurate instant detection without WSL process tree probing.
