title: Right-click context menu dismissed by mouse motion/release when TUI has mouse capture
description:
## Repro
1. Open any mouse-capture TUI in a therminal pane (tmux, htop, btop, lazygit, nvim with `mouse=a`, fzf `--mouse`, ranger)
2. Right-click anywhere in the pane to open the context menu
3. Move the mouse, or release the button

Observed: context menu vanishes immediately on motion/release.
Expected: menu stays open until a click outside the menu rect, Escape, or a menu item is selected.

## Root cause hypothesis
Menu dismissal fires on any mouse event while the menu is open. When a mouse-capture TUI is underneath, every motion/release is a mouse event, so the menu flickers away the instant the user tries to use it.

Two behaviors need splitting:
1. Menu dismissal should only trigger on click-outside-menu-rect, Escape, or item selection — not on motion/release/scroll.
2. While the menu is open, mouse events should not be forwarded to the PTY at all — the menu owns the mouse until it closes.

## Scope
Affects every TUI that enables SGR mouse reporting (tmux, htop, btop, lazygit, nvim `mouse=a`, fzf `--mouse`, ranger, etc.). Not tmux-specific.
close_reason: Closed

title: Settings gear: create therminal.toml if missing before opening editor
description:
CSD settings button (event_handler.rs:609) calls open_in_editor(config_path()), which stats the file and fails with "file not found" toast when therminal.toml doesn't exist. Config loader never writes defaults to disk — on a fresh install (especially Windows where AppData\Roaming\therminal is empty) the file legitimately doesn't exist.

Fix: before open_in_editor, if !config_file.exists(), create parent dir via ensure_config_dir() and write default_config_text() to disk, then open. Side benefit: new users get a v2-templated file immediately.
notes:
Initial fix (create-if-missing in event_handler.rs) was necessary but not sufficient. Follow-up work in commit 3f8717f and f623a78 uncovered two additional issues with the same button:

1. Path mangling (3f8717f): the old handler called self.open_in_editor(&config_file.to_string_lossy()), which runs every path through resolve_relative_to_cwd. On Windows with a WSL focused pane, the Linux-style cwd (/home/marci/...) joined with the Windows config path (C:\Users\marci\AppData\Roaming\therminal\therminal.toml) produced /home/marci/.../C:\Users\marci\...\therminal.toml — garbage that Explorer fell back to Documents on. Fixed by: (a) adding a new `open_absolute_in_editor` helper in pane_ops.rs that skips cwd resolution entirely for known-absolute paths, and (b) tn-jci5: teaching resolve_relative_to_cwd to recognize Windows-absolute paths (drive-letter + UNC) as the root-cause fix. Settings gear now calls open_absolute_in_editor with the PathBuf directly.

2. Glyph rendering (f623a78): ⚙ (U+2699) rendered as tofu because it's a symbol codepoint not shipped with most mono fonts and glyphon's fallback didn't pick up Segoe UI Symbol. Switched to ≡ (U+2261, identical-to) which ships with every serious mono font's Mathematical Operators block and visually reads as menu/settings.
close_reason: Fixed: settings gear now creates therminal.toml with default_config_text() via save_default_to() when missing, then opens the editor. event_handler.rs:609.

title: Fix regressions found in Wave 3B plugin/OSC review
description:
Code review of recent Wave 3B commits surfaced three concrete issues in the new plugin/pattern/OSC work.

1. OSC 1341 markers are consumed by the interceptor but dropped in production because daemon pane construction installs the shared OscHandlerRegistry without installing a harness-event sink. Tests wire a sink, but Pane::spawn / Pane::from_raw_fd do not, so Claude marker events never reach a daemon-side consumer.

2. Pattern-pack hot reload does not work for first-time plugin installation. The daemon only starts the notify watcher when the resolved patterns directory already exists at startup. If a user creates the directory later and adds their first pack while the daemon is running, no watcher exists and reload never fires.

3. Fallback pack names derived from filenames bypass the loader's own naming contract. pack_name_from_path does not lowercase the stem despite the comment claiming it does, and load_pack_from_str does not validate fallback names when pack_name is omitted. Mixed-case filenames can therefore load invalid pack ids and break collision/shadow behavior against shipped packs.

This issue tracks fixing those regressions and adding targeted tests so the Wave 3B surfaces behave correctly in daemon-backed production paths.
design:
Likely fixes:
- Thread a harness-event channel/sink through daemon pane construction, parallel to the existing interceptor event channel, and route TaggedHarnessEvent into the appropriate daemon event/log/broadcast surface.
- Adjust pattern watcher startup so missing directories do not disable future watching; watch the parent config dir or proactively create/watch the patterns dir.
- Normalize filename-derived pack names to lowercase and validate them through the same rules as explicit pack_name.

Review references:
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs
- crates/therminal-terminal/src/interceptor.rs
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/ensure.rs
- crates/therminal-terminal/src/semantic_patterns/loader.rs
acceptance_criteria:
1. A daemon-level production path test proves an OSC 1341 marker emitted through a real pane/interceptor path reaches a daemon-side consumer instead of being silently dropped.
2. The daemon watches the user patterns directory in a way that supports first-time creation/install while the daemon is already running, and a test covers that behavior or the nearest isolated watcher helper.
3. Fallback pack names derived from filenames are normalized and validated consistently with explicit pack_name values.
4. Tests cover mixed-case filename fallback naming / collision behavior so shipped-vs-user shadowing stays deterministic.
close_reason: Fixed all three regressions. #1: SessionManager::set_harness_event_sink threads a std::sync::mpsc sender into every pane's TherminalInterceptor at construction (Pane::spawn + Pane::from_raw_fd), and ensure.rs drains it on a dedicated OS thread. Production-path integration test (harness_osc_event_reaches_session_manager_sink in tests/session_pty.rs) spawns a real PTY, echoes OSC 1341 via printf, and asserts the TaggedHarnessEvent reaches the receiver — test passes. #2: ensure.rs now create_dir_all's the pattern watch dir before starting the notify watcher so first-time installs work. #3: pack_name_from_path lowercases + sanitizes filename stems (collapse dashes, fall back to "unknown"), and load_pack_from_str re-validates fallback names through is_valid_name. 7 new loader tests + 1 production-path test, all green.

title: Wire PatternEngine dispatch into PTY line-finalization and OSC 133 C hooks
description:
## Context

tn-yrjd shipped the pattern engine module with a working API, all three scopes (finalized_line / prompt_boundary / region), all three actions (hotspot / widget / emit_event), config + hot-reload + MCP terminal.patterns.stats, and 3 working example packs. However, the caller side — the actual dispatch of grid line commits and OSC 133 C command-finished events into PatternEngine::dispatch_* — was deferred to keep the engine change isolated.

Today, example packs will load cleanly and terminal.patterns.stats will report them, but no patterns will actually match against live terminal output because nothing is calling engine.dispatch_finalized_line() or engine.dispatch_prompt_boundary() from the PTY reader side.

## Task

1. Finalized line hook in crates/therminal-terminal/src/ — find the grid's line-commit path. After finalizing a line, call engine.dispatch_finalized_line(pane_id, line_text) and feed the returned matches into the hotspot registry + event bus + widget manager.

2. Prompt boundary hook. Wherever OSC 133 C is handled (grep 133 in therminal-terminal), after the shell integration marks a command as complete, collect the command's output transcript and call engine.dispatch_prompt_boundary(pane_id, transcript).

3. Action plumbing.
   - Hotspot matches: feed through hotspots_from_pattern_matches (from tn-yrjd) and merge into the per-pane hotspot set.
   - Widget matches: forward to the widget manager.
   - Emit_event matches: publish on the event bus.

4. PatternEngine handle plumbing. The engine is currently built in crates/therminal-daemon/src/ensure.rs and threaded into the MCP server. The PTY reader task needs access to the same Arc<PatternEngine>.

5. Integration tests. The therminal-integration-tests crate from tn-1kzt has a real PTY harness — add a scenario that drops a test pattern pack, runs a matching command, and asserts the match was dispatched.

## Acceptance
- Dropping an example pack into ~/.config/therminal/patterns/ and running a matching command produces real hotspots / events / widget dispatches
- Integration test scenario passes
- ./scripts/ci.sh clean

## Related
- tn-yrjd (closed) — the engine itself
- tn-hkpz — OSC handler registry; layer on top if it lands first
- tn-xula — unified event bus; use it for emit_event if it lands first
close_reason: PatternEngine dispatch wired via new daemon pattern_dispatch module; integration test pattern_engine_dispatches_finalized_line_match passes; ./scripts/ci.sh green

title: Research: Claude Code release notes for new hooks since ~4.37 that state-tracker.sh doesn't use
description:
## Context

The user spotted a recent Claude Code hook addition: `hookSpecificOutput.sessionTitle` on `UserPromptSubmit` hooks, which lets a hook script set a user-authored session title. That's inference-unrecoverable signal (no way to derive from the PTY stream) and unlocks pane-header center-title display, workspace tab labels, and session-switcher UX.

But: "there might be other new hooks I haven't seen." Need a focused research pass that:
1. Pulls the Claude Code release notes from the last ~4 weeks (roughly 2026-03-11 through 2026-04-08)
2. Extracts every hook-related addition (new hook names, new hook input/output fields, new events)
3. Cross-references against what `~/projects/conductor-mcp/plugins/conductor/hooks/scripts/state-tracker.sh` currently uses
4. Identifies unused capabilities that would add high-signal data to therminal

## Where to look

- **Claude Code release notes**: official changelog, wherever it lives. Anthropic publishes release notes on their docs site (`docs.anthropic.com/en/release-notes/claude-code` or similar). May also be in `npm view @anthropic-ai/claude-code` output or in `~/.claude/` if there's a version changelog stored locally.
- **Claude Code docs for hooks**: the canonical docs page describing all hook events, their input/output schemas, and the `hookSpecificOutput` contract. URL shape like `docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/hooks` or similar.
- **`~/projects/conductor-mcp/plugins/conductor/hooks/scripts/state-tracker.sh`**: the script currently in use. Inventory which hook events it handles (`SessionStart`, `PreToolUse`, `PostToolUse`, `UserPromptSubmit`, `Stop`, `SubagentStart`, `SubagentStop`, `Notification`).
- **`~/.claude/settings.json`**: the user's current hook wiring to confirm what's installed.

## What to deliver

A comment on this issue (or a short doc at `docs/integrations/claude-code-hooks-audit.md`) with:

### 1. Inventory of currently-used hooks + fields

Table of every hook event state-tracker.sh currently handles, with the input fields it reads from stdin and any `hookSpecificOutput` fields it writes. This is the baseline.

### 2. Inventory of hooks / fields added in recent releases

Table of every hook-related change in Claude Code release notes from ~4.37 onward. For each:
- Release version + date
- What changed (new hook event / new input field / new `hookSpecificOutput` field)
- What information it exposes
- Whether state-tracker.sh currently uses it (yes/no/partial)

### 3. Recommendations

Sorted by impact, a list of "therminal should read X" recommendations:
- What new field/hook to read
- What therminal feature it unblocks
- Whether it's inference-unrecoverable (high value) or redundant with existing inference (low value)
- What the ported first-party hook script needs to add (for tn-XXXX first-party hooks epic)

## Known starting points

The user explicitly named these:
- `hookSpecificOutput.sessionTitle` on `UserPromptSubmit` — already known. tn-5wrx wants to use this for pane-header center title.

Things the user thinks might exist but hasn't seen:
- "Maybe others for setting other pane-header metadata"
- "Probably some token-accounting additions"
- "Possibly subagent lifecycle improvements"

These are hunches, not confirmed. The research is to nail them down.

## What this feeds into

- **tn-ifee** (enrich MCP session surface) — should add any new fields discovered here to the `ClaudeSessionState` / `SessionInfo` struct before implementation starts
- **tn-97w8** (first-party hooks epic, Child A — schema lockdown) — the schema doc must include every known hook field from the research
- **tn-r2a3** (schema alias fix) — if the research reveals a NEWER schema convention than what conductor-mcp uses, the deserializer should handle that too
- **tn-5wrx** (pane header overhaul) — may reveal additional header-worthy signals beyond session title

## Constraints

- **This is a research task, not implementation.** Deliverable is a markdown doc + recommendations, not code.
- **Under 500 lines of writing.** Focused, table-heavy, link-heavy. Don't paraphrase release notes — quote them with links.
- **Confirm version numbers.** Don't guess; if the research can't find an exact "added in X.Y.Z" attribution for a field, say so explicitly.
- **Don't touch therminal source.** This issue is docs-only for this round.

## When Done

`bd close <id> --reason "Research done: N new hook fields identified, M recommendations filed. Fed into tn-ifee + tn-97w8."`
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context

These are inference-unrecoverable signals that would unlock high-value therminal features.

## Task

Use WebFetch on `docs.anthropic.com/en/release-notes/claude-code` and `docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/hooks`. Also inventory `~/.claude/settings.json` for the user's installed hooks.

## Deliverable

title: EPIC: First-party Claude Code hooks shipped in therminal-harness-claude (state-tracker + statusline + installer)
description:
## Context

Therminal's harness crate reads state files from `/tmp/claude-code-state/` but doesn't ship the scripts that write them. Today, users get the "AI-native terminal" experience only if they also have **conductor-mcp's hooks** installed at the user level — a sibling project the user built on tmux before therminal. That's fine while the user is dogfooding both, but it's an invisible prerequisite for anyone else picking up therminal, and the project shouldn't have an implicit dependency on a sister project's hook scripts.

The answer is for `therminal-harness-claude` to ship its own first-party hook scripts, installed via a dedicated `therminal harness install claude` command, writing a schema that's compatible with conductor-mcp (so the two can coexist on one machine) but owned by therminal.

### Architecture position — confirmed with user

The user has already committed to **first-party integration crates for the four target harnesses** (Claude Code, Codex, Copilot CLI, OpenCode). This epic is the Claude Code concretization of that commitment. Subsequent harnesses follow the same pattern under their respective crates: `therminal-harness-codex/hooks/`, `therminal-harness-copilot/hooks/`, `therminal-harness-opencode/hooks/`.

### Tier-1 / Tier-2 / Tier-3 boundary

This epic is the "Tier 1 vanilla Claude Code experience" — the things every user who installs therminal and runs `claude` should get by default, with a first-run prompt to install. Tier 2 (cache health widget, audio cues, statusline integrations) and Tier 3 (pattern packs, BeadsHive integration, opinionated slash commands) remain outside this epic. See conversation 2026-04-08 for the full tiering discussion.

**Explicitly in scope** (Tier 1):
- Session state tracking (what conductor-mcp's `state-tracker.sh` does)
- Context window tracking (what conductor-mcp's `statusline-script.sh` does)
- Subagent count tracking (conductor-mcp's atomic counter file)
- Session title capture (Claude Code 4.37+ `hookSpecificOutput.sessionTitle`)
- MCP server registration (`.mcp.json` or `~/.claude.json` entry for therminal)
- CLI wrapper slash commands for the daemon CLI (cache-friendly alternative to MCP)

**Explicitly out of scope** (Tier 2+):
- Cache health widget UI (separate issue; this epic exposes the data but doesn't render it)
- Audio announcements (opt-in, not Tier 1)
- Permission mode / rate-limit / auto-approve settings (opinionated; tier 2 or "not therminal's business")
- Custom slash commands beyond CLI wrappers

## Guiding principle (lock this in)

**Therminal never changes how Claude Code *runs*. Only how it's *observed*.**

This is the tiebreaker for future "should this be in the first-party hooks" questions. Anything that modifies `defaultMode`, `CLAUDE_RATE`, auto-approves tools, or changes which MCP servers load by default is out of scope. Anything that reads hook stdin and writes state files is in scope. This lets therminal be the AI-native terminal **without** being opinionated about *how* the user uses Claude Code.

## Children (rough decomposition — refine during /gg-plan)

### Child A: schema lockdown

Write `docs/integrations/claude-code-state-schema.md` formalizing the state file schema. Based on conductor-mcp's current schema, cover:
- Main state file: `/tmp/claude-code-state/{session_id}.json`
- Context window file: `/tmp/claude-code-state/{claude_session_id}-context.json`
- Subagent counter: `/tmp/claude-code-state/subagents/{session_id}.count`
- Every field: name, type, optionality, source (which hook writes it)
- Schema version tag: start at v1, increment on breaking changes, therminal's reader validates
- Coexistence with conductor-mcp: both projects write the same schema; when fields diverge, document which field is therminal-only vs conductor-only vs shared
- Escape hatch: the schema is OPEN — new fields can be added as long as they're optional and don't break v1 readers

Deliverable: the doc + a schema version constant in `therminal-harness-claude` + a reader-side version check.

### Child B: hook scripts (Linux/macOS + Windows)

Port conductor-mcp's hook scripts into `crates/therminal-harness-claude/resources/hooks/claude-code/`:
- `therminal-claude-state.sh` (Linux/macOS) — ported from `state-tracker.sh`, writes the schema defined in Child A
- `therminal-claude-statusline.sh` — ported from `statusline-script.sh`, writes the context-window companion file
- `therminal-claude-state.ps1` (Windows native) — PowerShell port, same behavior
- `therminal-claude-statusline.ps1` — same
- Tested against the user's current conductor-mcp setup: installing both should not conflict (they write different file names or use a namespace prefix)

Important: the first-party scripts should **not** overwrite or break conductor-mcp's files if both are installed. Options:
- Use a `/tmp/therminal-claude-state/` directory instead of sharing `/tmp/claude-code-state/` — cleanest but loses the "both can cohabit" win
- Use a filename prefix like `therminal-{session_id}.json` in the same directory — coexistence at the cost of a slightly ugly layout
- Write to a therminal-specific dir, symlink from `/tmp/claude-code-state/` if conductor-mcp isn't present

Pick one during Child B implementation. The user has strong opinions here — ask.

### Child C: installer subcommand

New `therminal` CLI subcommand:
```
therminal harness install claude [--hooks] [--mcp] [--slash-commands] [--all]
therminal harness uninstall claude
therminal harness status claude
```

- Detects whether Claude Code is installed (`~/.claude/settings.json` or the Windows equivalent exists)
- Detects whether therminal's hooks are already installed (idempotent)
- Writes or updates `~/.claude/settings.json` to wire up the hook scripts. CRITICAL: must not overwrite user-authored hooks — merge, don't replace. Back up the file before writing (with atime stamp suffix).
- Writes or updates the MCP server entry (`.mcp.json` or `~/.claude.json`) pointing at `therminal mcp` — same non-destructive merge.
- Writes slash command files to `~/.claude/commands/` (see Child E).
- Installs the hook scripts by copying from `$THERMINAL_RESOURCES_DIR/hooks/claude-code/` to `~/.claude/hooks/scripts/therminal/` (subdirectory so they don't mix with conductor-mcp or user scripts).

The CLI also handles uninstall: removes only the entries therminal added (keyed by a comment marker or a dedicated `[therminal]` section in the JSON), leaves everything else alone.

### Child D: first-run UX

When the GUI launches for the first time (tracked via a sentinel file at `$XDG_STATE_HOME/therminal/first_run_complete`), show a dialog:

> therminal detected Claude Code on this machine. For the full AI-native terminal experience, therminal can install its Claude Code integration:
> - [x] Session state tracking (shows what your agents are doing in pane headers)
> - [x] Context window tracking (shows how much context each session has used)
> - [x] Session title capture (shows the real task name in pane headers)
> - [x] therminal MCP server (let Claude Code drive your terminal from inside a session)
> - [x] Slash command wrappers (cache-friendly shortcuts for common therminal operations)
>
> [Install all] [Custom] [Skip for now]

"Install all" runs `therminal harness install claude --all`. "Custom" shows individual toggles. "Skip for now" sets the sentinel, doesn't nag again, but a status bar hint appears: "Claude Code detected — run `therminal harness install claude` for full integration."

If the user already has conductor-mcp's hooks installed, detect that and show an additional option: "You already have conductor-mcp's hooks installed. therminal can coexist — install its hooks alongside." (Defaults to yes, since conductor-mcp is the user's own project.)

### Child E: slash command wrappers

Ship a `~/.claude/commands/therminal/` directory with slash commands that wrap the daemon CLI for cache-friendly use from inside a Claude Code session:

- `/pane-peek <id>` → `therminal pane peek <id> --last 10`
- `/pane-write <id> <keys>` → `therminal pane write <id> <keys>`
- `/pane-list` → `therminal pane list`
- `/sessions-detail <pane>` → `therminal agents get-session-detail <pane>` (post tn-XXXX enrich)
- `/events-tail <filter>` → `therminal events --follow <filter>`
- `/workspace-split [h|v]` → `therminal pane create --split <dir>`

Keep the set small and focused. Each command is a single-file `.md` under `~/.claude/commands/therminal/` that just shells out.

### Child F: upgrade handling

When the schema version (Child A) bumps between releases:
- therminal's reader rejects the old schema with a clear error: "state file schema version X is older than this therminal build expects (Y). Re-run `therminal harness install claude` to update the hooks."
- The installer's upgrade path detects the old scripts by checksum, replaces them, updates `~/.claude/settings.json` if the hook paths changed.
- Hooks for other harnesses (codex, copilot, opencode) can be upgraded in the same command: `therminal harness upgrade all`.

### Child G: sibling harnesses (deferred to separate epics)

Not done in this epic, but the infrastructure from Children C+D+F is shared:
- `therminal-harness-codex` — same pattern, Codex's hook format
- `therminal-harness-copilot` — same pattern, GitHub Copilot CLI's hook format (does it even have hooks? research)
- `therminal-harness-opencode` — same pattern, OpenCode's hook format

Each gets its own epic once this one's shape is proven.

## Dependencies

- **tn-r2a3** — schema alias fix must land first so the reader actually works with the files conductor-mcp is already writing. Child A's schema lockdown should reference conductor-mcp's current schema as its starting point.
- **tn-XXXX enrich agent info MCP surface** — not a hard dep, but Children A + B are more valuable once that issue lands so the new fields have somewhere to go.
- **Claude Code 4.37+ hook research** (tn-XXXX) — should feed into Child A's schema (captures new hook fields we don't know about yet).

## Hard constraints

- **No behavior change to Claude Code**. Read-only observation only. The guiding principle above is inviolable.
- **No silent writes to user config files**. Every edit to `settings.json` / `.mcp.json` shows a dialog first with a diff preview.
- **Coexistence with conductor-mcp is mandatory**. The user is using both; therminal's hooks must not break conductor's.
- **Uninstall must be clean**. No orphaned files, no stale entries in `settings.json`, no leftover `/tmp/` garbage.
- **Backup before writing**. Always.

## When Done

Epic closes when Children A + B + C + D ship and a clean-install user can launch therminal, click "install all" in the first-run dialog, open a Claude Code session in a pane, and see the session title in the pane header. Children E + F + G are welcome but not blocking.

## Discovered
2026-04-08 — during conversation about "vanilla vs opinionated" line-drawing for harness integrations. User clarified that state-tracker.sh is from their own project lineage (tmuxplexer → terminal-tabs → conductor-mcp), not community code, and that conductor-mcp is the current canonical writer. Shifted the conversation from "defensive schema compatibility" to "adopt the authoritative schema."
notes:
## Framing correction, 2026-04-08

The original description included a "Guiding principle" section claiming **"therminal never changes how Claude Code runs. Only how it's observed."** After discussion with user, that principle is wrong:

- Hooks ARE Claude Code's first-class extensibility surface, not an observation-only mechanism
- Saying "we install hooks but don't change behavior" is a polite fiction — the line isn't real
- Claude Code users typically arrive with existing hook setups (user has ~8 hooks wired via conductor-mcp); therminal lands into a personalized environment, not a blank slate
- Other harnesses (Codex, Copilot CLI, OpenCode) are open source, so harness crates for them can be more ambitious than the Claude Code crate can be. The harness crates are NOT symmetric products.

### Replacement posture: additive + augment-existing

Therminal's installer is **detection-first, augmentation-first**:

1. **Detect existing hooks.** On first-run or `therminal harness install claude`, scan `~/.claude/settings.json` and `~/.claude/hooks/scripts/` for known patterns (conductor-mcp, community scripts, user-authored). Report what's found.
2. **Only install what's missing.** If conductor-mcp's state-tracker.sh is already writing `/tmp/claude-code-state/`, therminal skips its own state-tracker install and just reads the existing files. If the MCP server entry isn't registered, install it. If slash command wrappers aren't there, install them.
3. **Never replace user hooks silently.** Every write to `settings.json` shows a diff preview. Backups before writes. Uninstall removes only therminal-added entries (keyed by marker or dedicated section).
4. **Ship fallback hooks for users who have nothing.** The first-party scripts still live at `crates/therminal-harness-claude/resources/hooks/claude-code/` but they're the fallback path, not the primary one. For new users with no existing hooks, they get the full stack. For the user himself (with conductor-mcp installed), they're skipped.

### Scope shrinkage

The original epic had 7 children (schema lockdown, hook scripts Linux+Windows, installer, first-run UX, slash commands, upgrade handling, sibling harnesses). With the reframing, the load-bearing ones are:

**Load-bearing (blocks epic close):**
- Child A: schema adoption (read what conductor-mcp writes, extend reader to cover it — partial overlap with tn-r2a3)
- Child C: installer subcommand (`therminal harness install claude --mcp`), minimum is MCP server registration
- Child D: first-run UX (detect existing setup, offer augmentation)

**Nice-to-have (doesn't block close):**
- Child B: fallback hook scripts (for users with nothing)
- Child E: slash command wrappers (user can just `! therminal pane peek`)
- Child F: upgrade handling (deal with it when a second version exists)

**Deferred to sibling epics:**
- Child G: sibling harnesses (Codex, Copilot, OpenCode). Each gets its own epic. Filed as separate research tasks for the audit phase first.

### Rationale for the shrinkage

The conductor-mcp cautionary tale: ~40 MCP tools was too many. therminal should default to **consolidate, not add.** If a Child doesn't have a concrete reason to ship now, it waits. The reader-side schema adoption + MCP registration is the minimum that makes "therminal reads what's already there and exposes it via MCP" actually work end-to-end for the user's current setup. Everything else is additive surface area that has to be maintained forever.

### Thesis alignment

This epic is **Pillar 4 (AI visibility)** work per the project_thesis_four_pillars memory. The installer UX is also partial Pillar 3 (GPU-only appeal) since first-run dialogs are the kind of thing terminal-phobic users notice. Not Pillar 1 or 2 territory.

### Dependencies

- **tn-r2a3** — schema alias fix is a prerequisite for Child A (reader can actually link state files to panes)
- **tn-dboq** — Claude Code hook release notes research feeds Child A's field list
- **tn-ifee** — enriches the MCP surface with the fields conductor-mcp's hooks already write; this epic is what makes the data available in the first place

### Schema home

**Conductor-mcp is the canonical schema writer.** When therminal needs a new field:

1. PR upstream to conductor-mcp adding the field to state-tracker.sh / statusline-script.sh
2. Update therminal's reader to consume the field once the PR merges
3. Document in `crates/therminal-harness-claude/CLAUDE.md`

This treats conductor-mcp as the sibling project it is. therminal does not fork the schema. It extends it collaboratively. The "first-party fallback hooks" this epic ships should write **the exact same schema** conductor-mcp writes, so any user can run either script and therminal's reader sees the same thing.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: EPIC: /gg-delegate — spawn sibling Claude sessions into dedicated panes for capability-distributed work
description:
## Context

There are two fundamentally different shapes of multi-agent work, and therminal's current tooling only supports one of them well.

**Shape A — parallel in-process subagents** (what `/gg-execute` does today):
- Orchestrator spawns multiple `Agent()` / `Task` subagents that run inside the same Claude Code process
- **Shared prompt cache** (huge savings on shared context)
- Same MCP tool set, same permissions, same working directory, same system prompt as the parent
- Good for: batched independent beads issues where context uniformity is a feature, not a bug
- Bad for: anything where the subagent needs *different* tools, a different working dir, or a different persona

**Shape B — sibling Claude sessions with distinct capabilities** (what this epic is about):
- Orchestrator spawns a **new `claude` subprocess** into a new therminal pane
- **Separate prompt cache** (cost) — but with:
  - Different `--effort` level (high-effort planner while low-effort executor runs)
  - Different `--system-prompt` / persona (adversarial reviewer, domain expert, documentation writer)
  - Different MCP server set (browser control, filesystem isolated to a scratch dir, sandboxed experimentation)
  - Different working directory (worktree / sibling repo / throwaway scratch)
  - Different permission mode (sandboxed experimental runs)
- Good for: capability distribution, not parallelism
- Bad for: batched shared-context work (parent-side `/gg-execute` wins there)

User put it concretely: "spawn claude --effort high '/gg-plan' and have the backlog refined and planned out for issues not ready yet, as you are conducting /gg-execute" — or "spawn a claude in a separate folder who has browser control mcp tools while I don't have all those tools in your context."

## The pattern this epic enables

```
therminal pane create --spawn "claude --effort high -p '/gg-plan'"
                ├─ creates new pane
                ├─ spawns `claude` in that pane's PTY
                ├─ new Claude session JSONL appears at ~/.claude/projects/{hash}/{uuid}.jsonl
                ├─ therminal-harness-claude tailer picks it up (assumes tn-ix8c + tn-r2a3 fixed)
                └─ events stream on therminal://claude/events with source_id=claude, session={uuid}

orchestrator subscribes → sees sibling transition idle→thinking→streaming→done
                      → reads sibling's final result from its pane's command transcript
                      → summarizes back to the human
```

**Tiling thesis payoff**: every sibling Claude is visible as its own pane. The human can intervene, read, inspect, kill, or re-prompt any sibling at any time. `/gg-execute`'s in-process model has no such affordance — the user can't see a Task subagent, let alone talk to it.

## Scope — an epic, not one issue

This needs decomposition during `/gg-plan` before any implementation. Rough child issue shapes below; final breakdown happens at planning time.

### Child A (docs + design): sibling profile schema

A TOML file or config section defining reusable sibling "profiles":

```toml
[delegate.profiles.planner]
description = "Backlog refinement and issue planning"
command = "claude --effort high -p '/gg-plan'"
working_dir = "same"  # or "worktree" or "scratch/{random}"
mcp_enabled = ["beads"]
permission_mode = "default"

[delegate.profiles.browser-research]
description = "Web research with browser control"
command = "claude --effort medium"
working_dir = "scratch/{random}"
mcp_enabled = ["tabz-chrome", "web-search"]

[delegate.profiles.adversarial-review]
description = "Second-opinion code review with a skeptical persona"
command = "claude --effort high --system-prompt-file ~/.claude/personas/adversarial-reviewer.md"
working_dir = "worktree"
```

Defines the full set of knobs a profile can tune and locks the schema before impl.

### Child B (feature): `therminal pane create --spawn <cmd>`

Extend the existing `terminal.panes.create` MCP tool and CLI subcommand to accept a startup command that gets written to the new pane's PTY as the first input. The daemon already has `IpcRequest::SplitPane` and `CreatePane` — adding a `startup_command: Option<String>` field to those requests is a small change. The tricky part is timing: need to wait for the PTY's shell prompt before writing the command, otherwise the bytes get eaten by shell startup. Use OSC 133 A (prompt-start) as the signal, or a short delay for shells without integration.

### Child C (skill): `/gg-delegate`

Claude Code slash command that:
- Takes a task description + a profile name (or inline profile overrides)
- Resolves the profile → concrete `claude` invocation
- Calls `terminal.panes.create --spawn <invocation>` to create the sibling pane
- Subscribes to `therminal://claude/events?source_id=claude&kinds=agent_state` filtered to the new session's UUID
- Waits for the sibling to transition to `idle`/`done`
- Reads the sibling's final output from the pane's command transcript via `terminal.semantic.query_commands`
- Reports back to the parent orchestrator

### Child D (observability): unified orchestration view

When multiple siblings are active, the parent orchestrator needs to see their state at a glance. Leverage tn-5fgz (surface Claude events in GUI chrome) to show a status-bar summary like: "2 siblings: planner=streaming (87s), reviewer=waiting_for_input".

### Child E (ergonomics): sibling result capture

A sibling's output ends in its pane, but the parent orchestrator wants it as text it can summarize. Options:
- Use the OSC 633 command transcript indexer (already exists) — read the last command's full stdout
- Have the sibling write its result to a known file (`/tmp/delegate-result-{uuid}.md`) and parent reads it
- MCP subscription with a "final message" event type

Pick whichever is cleanest at impl time.

## Hard constraints

- **Must not cannibalize `/gg-execute`.** In-process parallelism stays the right answer for batched shared-context work. This is an additive pattern for capability distribution.
- **No "spawn 20 siblings" footguns.** Per-profile rate limits. Default to 1 active sibling per profile unless explicitly allowed.
- **Cost transparency.** Each sibling is a separate billable context — the skill must surface "this will spawn a fresh session, costing ~Nk input tokens" before the user approves the first sibling in a session. (Subsequent spawns under the same profile don't re-prompt.)
- **Sibling siblings are fine.** A delegated planner that itself needs a browser researcher should just call `/gg-delegate browser-research` in its own session. The topology doesn't need to be a strict tree.

## Dependencies

- **tn-r2a3** (state-tracker schema fix) — must land first, otherwise the sibling's JSONL won't be tailed on Linux/WSL
- **tn-ix8c** (Windows `$HOME`) — must land first, otherwise sibling JSONLs invisible on Windows
- **tn-bu1s** (event bus SPEC) — SHIPPED. The sibling's events flow through the unified event bus naturally.
- **tn-y92y** (harness crate extraction) — SHIPPED. The sibling events land on `therminal://claude/events` through the existing harness pipeline.

## Explicitly out of scope

- **Implementation** — this is the epic. Children get filed during `/gg-plan` decomposition after the SPEC in Child A lands.
- **Cross-session MCP state sharing** — siblings are independent. No shared state other than filesystem + the MCP server surface.
- **Cost accounting** — nice-to-have, not blocking.
- **Cancellation UX** — "kill the planner but keep the reviewer running" is a skill concern, not a therminal one.

## When Done

Epic closes when Children A+B+C land and a human has successfully used `/gg-delegate planner <task>` to spawn a sibling Claude, watched it complete in a pane, and seen the result reported back to the parent orchestrator.

## Discovered
2026-04-08, during conversation about in-process vs spawned subagent shapes. User surfaced the capability-distribution shape while discussing why the jsonl tailer looked silent (turned out to be tn-r2a3 schema bug, not a sibling-spawn issue — but the sibling-spawn pattern is the more interesting long-term question). Tiling thesis from the user's memory file drove the "make siblings into real panes" framing.
notes:
## Status: tn-ztv3.5 ready after Wave 0 refactors land (tn-l19x, tn-uww7)

## Current state (2026-04-10)
Epic is 50% complete (3/6 children done). Remaining: tn-ztv3.5 (result capture), tn-ztv3.3 (delegate skill, blocked by .5), tn-ztv3.4 (GUI chrome, blocked by .3). The delegate profile schema (tn-ztv3.1) and startup_command primitive (tn-ztv3.2) are shipped.

## Blocked on Wave 0 refactors
tn-ztv3.5 touches `mcp/mod.rs` (dispatch routing) and `session.rs` (`command_tracker_snapshot`). Both files are being split in parallel:
- **tn-l19x**: splits `mcp/mod.rs` (3,618 LOC) into `mcp/types.rs` + `mcp/helpers.rs` + trimmed `mod.rs`
- **tn-uww7**: splits `session.rs` (3,637 LOC) into `session/{snapshots,pane,window,base,layout,manager}.rs`
- **tn-ycld**: CI gate after all three Wave 0 refactors pass

tn-ztv3.5 (and by cascade tn-ztv3.3 and tn-ztv3.4) must not be executed until tn-ycld is closed. The prepared prompts for tn-ztv3.5 and tn-ztv3.3 have been updated with the post-refactor file paths.

## Execution sequence (after tn-ycld closes)
tn-ztv3.5 → tn-ztv3.3 → tn-ztv3.4

## When Done
Do NOT close until all children land and end-to-end delegate flow is verified.

title: Settings gear icon in window chrome CSD buttons — opens therminal.toml in $editor
description:
## Context

Therminal has no in-app way to edit its config today — users must know the path (`%APPDATA%\therminal\therminal.toml` on Windows, `~/.config/therminal/therminal.toml` on Linux) and reach it manually. The full GPU-rendered settings panel (tn-avjv) is in design and far from ready.

This issue adds a **minimal interim** affordance: a gear icon in the window chrome's CSD button row that, when clicked, opens the config file in the user's existing editor via the existing `open_in_editor` fallback chain. Matches the "surface over flags" philosophy: make the config's existence discoverable, build muscle memory for "click the gear" that will transfer 1:1 to the real panel later.

## Scope

Three files, ~150 LOC. Architecture recon already done — every prerequisite exists.

### 1. `crates/therminal-app/src/window/chrome/csd.rs` — add the button

- Add `CsdAction::Settings` variant to the existing enum
- Extend `csd_button_hit_test(px, bar_h, surface_width) -> Option<CsdAction>` to test four button slots instead of three. Insert the gear **left of minimize** so the layout becomes `[⚙ | ─ | □ | ✕]` (right-aligned, close stays rightmost). The formula pattern at lines 26-43 becomes:
  ```rust
  let close_x = surface_width - CSD_BTN_W;
  let max_x = close_x - CSD_BTN_W;
  let min_x = max_x - CSD_BTN_W;
  let settings_x = min_x - CSD_BTN_W;  // new
  ```
- Extend `draw_csd_buttons` to render the gear glyph `\u{2699}` (⚙) at `settings_x`. Use the existing glyphon text rendering path — the other buttons use Unicode glyphs (minimize `─`, maximize `□`, close `✕`) so the approach is consistent.
- Extend the hover-state match at lines 69-113 to include the gear slot. Gear hover tint should match the non-close buttons (white 10% opacity), not the close button's red.

### 2. `crates/therminal-app/src/window/event_handler.rs` — wire the click

At the existing `csd_button_hit_test` match arm (around lines 590-611), add:

```rust
chrome::CsdAction::Settings => {
    let config_file = therminal_core::config::config_path();
    self.open_in_editor(&config_file.to_string_lossy());
}
```

No new methods, no new imports beyond `therminal_core::config::config_path` (already public at `crates/therminal-core/src/config/mod.rs:32-35`).

### 3. `crates/therminal-app/src/window/chrome/mod.rs` — no changes expected

The re-export is already generic over the enum variants. Double-check after the edit.

## Behavior guarantees (provided by existing infrastructure)

- **`open_in_editor`** (`crates/therminal-app/src/window/pane_ops.rs:1346`) already handles `~`-expansion (post tn-3hqm), the `hotspots.editor_chain` fallback sequence, `open::that()` as last resort, and toasts on failure. A fresh user with empty `editor_chain` will still get *something* — the platform's native file association for `.toml`.
- **`config_path()`** resolves to the platform-correct path via `dirs::config_dir()`. No extra platform logic needed.
- **Hover affordance** is already parameterized by `hover_x` in `draw_csd_buttons`; the gear inherits hover visuals automatically once the match is extended.

## Non-goals

- **No settings panel v1.** Clicking the gear opens an external editor. That's the whole feature.
- **No tooltip system** — therminal's chrome has no tooltip infrastructure today. Leave as a todo.
- **No new keybinding.** Don't wire `Ctrl+,` — save that for tn-avjv when the real panel lands, so there's no awkward "keybinding opens panel, gear opens editor" divergence later.
- **No hot-reload coordination** — the existing `notify` watcher already picks up edits to `therminal.toml`, so the user saves, exits editor, and sees the change apply automatically.

## Tests

- Unit test `csd_button_hit_test` boundaries for 4 buttons at various surface widths (including very narrow surfaces where the button row might overflow the draggable area — verify the layout doesn't break)
- Unit test: clicking the Settings button at known coords returns `CsdAction::Settings`
- Manual: click gear, verify editor opens, verify toast-on-failure for bad editor_chain config

## Acceptance

- `./scripts/ci.sh` clean
- Gear button visible in the window titlebar on every platform (Linux CSD, Windows CSD, macOS if applicable)
- Clicking it opens `therminal.toml` in the configured editor
- Hover tint matches the other non-close buttons

## When Done

`bd close tn-5wrx --reason "Settings gear icon shipped; opens <path> via open_in_editor fallback chain"`

(Note the correct issue ID when closing — I think this becomes tn-XXXX on creation, replace accordingly.)
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
No in-app way to edit therminal.toml today — users must know the config path. The full settings panel (tn-avjv) is far out. This ships a minimal gear icon in the CSD button row that opens the config in $editor via the existing `open_in_editor` fallback chain. Matches the "surface over flags" philosophy. Every prerequisite already exists; ~150 LOC.

## Task
Add a gear glyph button left of minimize in the CSD button row `[⚙ │ ─ │ □ │ ✕]`. On click, open `therminal_core::config::config_path()` via `open_in_editor`.

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window/chrome/csd.rs` — add `CsdAction::Settings` variant; extend `csd_button_hit_test` for a fourth slot; render glyph `\u{2699}` in `draw_csd_buttons`; extend hover-state match (white 10% tint, not close-red)
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window/event_handler.rs` — add `CsdAction::Settings` arm at the existing hit-test match (around lines 590-611): call `self.open_in_editor(&config_path().to_string_lossy())`
- `crates/therminal-core/src/config/mod.rs:32-35` — `config_path()` already public, no changes

## Verification
- Click the gear icon → config file opens in $editor (or toast on failure)
- Hover affordance matches the other non-close buttons
- `./scripts/ci.sh` clean

## When Done
`bd close tn-2bfc --reason "settings gear icon shipped in CSD button row; opens therminal.toml via editor chain"`
## Retro
- What worked: the issue's architecture recon was spot-on — every prerequisite (open_in_editor with editor chain fallback, config_path() at the expected location, parameterized hover_x in draw_csd_buttons) was already in place; only ~70 LOC of additive edits needed. Tests were easy to extend (new settings_x() helper + 4 new tests alongside existing pattern).
- What was unclear: the pre-existing dirty tree had unrelated clippy errors in therminal-harness-claude/src/bin/claude-events.rs (unused imports on ReadHalf/WriteHalf/IpcClientStream/connect_client) that made ci.sh fail. Verified by stashing: those errors exist on the clean working branch too and are not from my changes. Running cargo clippy -p therminal-app --all-targets is clean, as are the 364 therminal-app tests (including 13 csd tests with 4 new ones for the settings button).
- No changes needed in chrome/mod.rs — CsdAction is re-exported as a type, so the new variant rides along automatically.
close_reason: settings gear icon shipped in CSD button row; opens therminal.toml via editor chain

title: jsonl_tailer home_dir() uses raw $HOME — subagent JSONL tailing silently broken on Windows native
description:
## Symptom
On Windows native builds of therminal, the Claude subagent JSONL tailer silently fails to find `~/.claude/projects/`. The tailer task runs (no config flag gates it — `ClaudeHarness::start()` is called unconditionally in `crates/therminal-daemon/src/ensure.rs:279-282`), but never emits any events because the path it watches doesn't exist.

The user reported "I haven't seen subagent JSONL tailing yet" on their Windows therminal build. No pixel-layer surfacing exists today, but even the underlying MCP resource (`therminal://claude/events`) and the `claude-events` dev CLI produce nothing on Windows because of this bug.

## Root cause
`crates/therminal-harness-claude/src/jsonl_tailer.rs:322-324` (after tn-y92y extraction — was `crates/therminal-daemon/src/claude_jsonl_tailer.rs` before):

```rust
fn home_dir() -> PathBuf {
    std::env::var("HOME").map(PathBuf::from).unwrap_or_else(|_| PathBuf::from("/home/builder"))
}
```

On Windows native, `$HOME` is typically unset — the convention is `%USERPROFILE%`. So the tailer falls back to `/home/builder` (a Docker-ism hardcoded at some point), reads nothing, watches nothing, emits nothing.

On WSL2 this works fine because WSL2 sets `$HOME=/home/<user>`. On Linux native it works. Only the Windows native target is broken.

## Fix
Swap `std::env::var("HOME")` for `dirs::home_dir()`. The `dirs` crate is already a transitive workspace dep (used by `therminal-runtime/src/paths.rs:31` which correctly resolves `%APPDATA%` on Windows via the same crate), so no new dependency is needed.

```rust
fn home_dir() -> PathBuf {
    dirs::home_dir().unwrap_or_else(|| PathBuf::from("/home/builder"))
}
```

Keep the `/home/builder` fallback or change it to something less Docker-specific — but secondary to the primary fix.

## Verification
- On Windows native: launch therminal, start a Claude Code session that spawns subagents, subscribe to `therminal://claude/events` via MCP, confirm events arrive.
- On Linux/WSL2: no regression — the test suite continues to pass.
- Add a unit test that stubs `dirs::home_dir()` and asserts the resolved path matches expected values for each platform convention.

## Related
- Companion to `claude-events` dev CLI Windows support (separate issue, bigger — Unix-sockets-only at `jsonl_tailer.rs:403`).
- Discovered while diagnosing the user's "I haven't seen subagent tailing yet" report, 2026-04-08.

## Key files
- `crates/therminal-harness-claude/src/jsonl_tailer.rs` (the `home_dir()` fn around line 322)
- Possibly other `HOME`-reading sites in the harness crate — `grep -n 'HOME' crates/therminal-harness-claude/src/` before landing
close_reason: Fixed in 966d9d2: swapped raw $HOME read for dirs::home_dir() in crates/therminal-harness-claude/src/jsonl_tailer.rs. dirs = "6" was already a workspace dep; added it to the harness crate's Cargo.toml. Verified cargo check/clippy/test/fmt clean on both the harness crate and the full workspace. WSL2 + Linux native unchanged (dirs::home_dir() falls through to $HOME there); Windows native will now resolve to %USERPROFILE%\.claude\projects\ on next rebuild.

title: Expand ~ in hotspot paths (folder open, editor open, directory promotion)
description:
Hotspot paths with `~/` prefixes were matched by the file-path regex only as `/...` (dropping the tilde), so clicking `~/projects/therminal` tried to `cd` into `/projects/therminal` (nonexistent). File-manager open and editor-open had the same class of bug — `~` is a literal character to `cd` inside single quotes, to Explorer/Finder, and to `stat(2)`.

## Fix
- `hotspot_detection.rs` FILE_PATH_RE now accepts leading `~/` and `~user/` in addition to `./`, `../`, `/`.
- New pure helper `expand_tilde(path, home_dir) -> Cow<str>` in `hotspot_detection.rs`:
  - `~/foo` with `HOME=/home/marci` → `/home/marci/foo`
  - `~` alone → `/home/marci`
  - `~alice/foo` → unchanged (no `getpwnam` call; click handler tries the literal and toasts on failure)
  - Non-tilde paths → no allocation
- Wire `expand_tilde` into four call sites:
  - `promote_directory_hotspots` (stat against the real path, not a literal `~/…`)
  - `open_folder_in_pane` (build a `cd` line that actually resolves)
  - `open_folder_in_file_manager` (file managers treat `~` as literal)
  - `open_in_editor` (pre-flight `stat` was failing the is-regular-file check)

## Tests
- Unit tests for each call site behavior (detect ~dir, ~file:line:col, ~user form, bare `~5` rejection)
- 6 unit tests on `expand_tilde` covering home/slash/suffix/no-home/non-tilde cases

## Files
- crates/therminal-terminal/src/hotspot_detection.rs
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/folder_open.rs
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/pane_ops.rs
close_reason: Implemented in prior session; committing the already-written patch.

title: Harness-resolved tool-call hotspots for Claude Code (Update/Read/Edit paths)
description:
## Context

Claude Code emits tool-call lines like `Update(crates/therminal-terminal/src/hotspot_detection.rs)`, `Read(foo.rs)`, `Edit(bar.toml)` directly into the terminal as part of its TUI rendering. These paths are *relative to the agent's cwd*, not the shell's cwd. Crucially, the agent's cwd can differ from the underlying shell's OSC 7 cwd in two ways that matter:

1. The agent may `cd` internally (worktree hops, subagent tasks) without any OSC 7 update to the parent shell.
2. Claude Code's own statusline updates to reflect the agent's current working tree, which is a different signal than OSC 7.

So the sibling issue for "resolve relative paths against `PaneState.cwd`" is the **wrong** answer for these specific lines — it would mis-resolve during worktree hops, which is exactly when the user wants the click to work.

## Why this belongs in the harness crate

Per the integration taxonomy in root `CLAUDE.md`:

> Does the source cooperate and emit structured data you control? → harness crate

Claude Code cooperates: the JSONL tailer already knows the agent's cwd per session, the state file in `/tmp/claude-code-state/` tracks session lineage, and subagent events carry parent pointers. All of that is being extracted into `crates/therminal-harness-claude/` in `tn-y92y`. Once that crate exists, it's the natural home for:

1. **A detector** that matches `Tool(path)` / `Update(path)` / `Read(path)` / `Edit(path)` in rows attributable to a Claude pane.
2. **A resolver** that takes the matched relative path and joins it against the *agent's* current cwd (from the harness's own session state, not OSC 7).
3. **A typed event** (`source_class=harness`, `source_id=claude`, `kind=tool_call`) on the event bus with the fully-resolved absolute path in the body.
4. **Renderer bridge**: the hotspot is drawn with display text = the original `crates/…/foo.rs` but click action = the resolved absolute path. Two options for the plumbing:
   - Extend `TextHotspot` with an optional `resolved_text` field, OR
   - Let the harness inject pre-resolved hotspots through a side channel that the renderer overlays on top of the core regex pass.

   The harness-crate `CLAUDE.md` should make the decision; the core `TextHotspot` change is cheaper and keeps the click path uniform.

## Scope

- New module in `therminal-harness-claude` for tool-call line detection.
- Regex or parser for the `Tool(…)` call shapes Claude Code actually prints (capture the full list — Update, Read, Edit, Write, Bash (no!), MultiEdit, Grep, Glob, WebFetch, Task, etc. — and decide which are file-path-bearing).
- Integration point with the existing JSONL tailer state so the resolver has the agent's cwd keyed by Claude session ID.
- Mapping from pane to Claude session (already exists via the harness's session-to-pane lookup).
- Tests: unit tests for each tool-call shape, a fixture-based test with a fake JSONL state, and a manual-verification checklist covering a worktree hop mid-session.

## Out of scope

- Codex / Copilot CLI / OpenCode equivalents — file separately per harness, they'll have different shapes.
- Generic "relative path against pane cwd" — that's the sibling issue for shell output.
- Extending the core regex — the harness crate should push its own hotspot entries, not broaden core detection.

## Blocked by

- `tn-y92y` — can't build this until `therminal-harness-claude` exists.

## Related

- `tn-fp42` — if Claude Code ever emits an OSC marker for tool-call boundaries, the detector becomes byte-accurate instead of regex-sniping the rendered text.
- `tn-2qzi` — the general "regex over-matches" cleanup; this issue adds a *narrower*, harness-scoped detector that should reduce load on the generic regex.
- Sibling: shell-cwd resolution for generic relative paths (core capability).
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Claude Code emits tool-call lines like `Update(crates/...)`, `Read(foo.rs)`, `Edit(bar.toml)` directly in the TUI. These paths are relative to the *agent's* cwd, which can diverge from the shell's OSC 7 cwd when the agent hops worktrees internally. The generic "relative paths against pane cwd" sibling issue (tn-vm2j) is the wrong fix here — it would mis-resolve exactly during worktree hops. This belongs in the harness crate because Claude cooperates: the jsonl tailer already knows agent cwd per session.

## Task
Build a harness-side detector + resolver + typed event for Claude tool-call lines. Per the taxonomy in root `CLAUDE.md`: `source_class=harness`, `source_id=claude`, `kind=tool_call`, body carries the resolved absolute path.

1. **Detector**: regex/parser module in `therminal-harness-claude` that matches `Tool(path)` / `Update(path)` / `Read(path)` / `Edit(path)` / `Write(path)` / `MultiEdit(path)` shapes. Decide which tool names are file-path-bearing (Bash is not). Only match in rows attributable to a Claude pane.
2. **Resolver**: joins the matched relative path against the agent's current cwd from harness session state (NOT OSC 7).
3. **Event**: emit on the event bus with `source_class=harness, source_id=claude, kind=tool_call` and the resolved absolute path in the body.
4. **Renderer bridge**: the hotspot displays original `crates/.../foo.rs` but clicks through the resolved absolute path. Decide between extending `TextHotspot` with `resolved_text: Option<String>` (cheaper, uniform click path — **preferred per root CLAUDE.md**) vs. harness-injected side-channel hotspots.
5. **Tests**: unit tests per tool-call shape, fixture-based test with fake JSONL state, manual-verification checklist for a worktree hop mid-session.

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-harness-claude/src/` — new module for tool-call detection
- `crates/therminal-harness-claude/src/jsonl_tailer.rs` — source of agent cwd per session
- `crates/therminal-harness-claude/src/pipeline.rs` — where events are emitted
- `crates/therminal-terminal/src/hotspot_detection.rs` — `TextHotspot` type if adding `resolved_text` field
- Pane → Claude session mapping (already exists via harness lookup)

## Out of scope
- Codex / Copilot CLI / OpenCode equivalents (separate per-harness issues)
- Extending core regex (harness pushes its own hotspots)
- Generic relative-path resolver for shell output (tn-vm2j handles that)

## Verification
- Click an `Update(path)` line in a Claude pane → correct file opens via editor chain
- Works after a worktree hop (agent cwd != shell cwd)
- `./scripts/ci.sh` clean

## When Done
`bd close tn-gidy --reason "Claude tool-call hotspots resolve via harness agent cwd; click routes to absolute path"`
## Retro
- What worked: pure-fn detector with explicit agent_cwd parameter kept tests trivial; TextHotspot.resolved_text field reuse meant click handler change was one line.
- What was unclear: task said 'use existing pane→claude-session mapping in the harness' but no such mapping exists — harness is pane-agnostic. Worked around by introducing ClaudeSessionCwdIndex (session→cwd) and deferring the pane→session lookup to a future renderer-bridge follow-up.
- Deferred: therminal-app does not depend on therminal-harness-claude today; calling detect_claude_tool_call_hotspots from render.rs requires adding that dep + plumbing an Arc<ClaudeSessionCwdIndex> + pid→session_id lookup via AgentRegistry. All scaffolding (field, event variant, detector, index, click handler preference) is in place so the follow-up is pure wiring.
- Concurrent merge: tn-ztv3.1 landed the TextHotspot.resolved_text field via a parallel commit while this task was in flight; no conflicts.
close_reason: Claude tool-call hotspots resolve via harness agent cwd; click routes to absolute path

title: Directory hotspot promotion fails on Windows build for WSL paths (is_dir stat cross-filesystem)
description:
## Symptom

On the Windows build of therminal running against a WSL-adjacent workflow (WSL2 dev machine, Windows GUI, shells inside panes are WSL bash), right-clicking a directory path like `/home/marci/projects/therminal` in the terminal grid shows only the **file** context menu ("Open in editor") — never the folder menu ("Open in new pane" / "Open in file manager" / "Copy path"). Clicking "Open in editor" fails because the path doesn't exist on Windows.

## Root cause

`window/render.rs::promote_directory_hotspots_from_fs` calls `std::fs::metadata(p).map(|m| m.is_dir())` with the raw hotspot text. On Windows, `std::fs::metadata("/home/marci/projects/therminal")` resolves against the current drive letter (`C:\home\marci\projects\therminal`), which doesn't exist, so the stat errors out. `promote_directory_hotspots` silently swallows IO errors (see test `promote_directory_hotspots_ignores_io_errors`) and leaves `is_dir = false`. The click handler then shows the file menu.

This is the Windows-GUI/WSL-content mismatch that also bit tn-5akk (paste CRLF) and tn-b77d (mode flags) — the GUI process and the pane content inhabit different filesystems.

## Evidence

From a live session on 2026-04-08:
- Windows build of therminal (`/mnt/c/Users/marci/Desktop/therminal.exe`)
- Panes run WSL bash (confirmed via `ps aux`)
- Directory paths in output (e.g. `/home/marci/projects/therminal`) render as hotspots
- Right-click → only "Open in editor" appears → clicking fails

## Fix options

### A. Detect the path looks like a POSIX absolute path on Windows and translate via `wsl.exe --exec stat` (or `\\wsl.localhost\<distro>\...`)
Correct but complicated — requires detecting the active WSL distro and probably caching it.

### B. Pass the stat probe through the same process that's running inside the focused pane
i.e. if the focused pane's PTY is running under WSL, ask that side to stat the path. Requires an IPC round-trip per hotspot promotion — too expensive for the render hot path.

### C. Pre-translate `/...` paths on Windows using `\\wsl.localhost\<distro>\...` or `\\wsl$\<distro>\...` UNC syntax
Cheapest. `\\wsl.localhost\Ubuntu\home\marci\projects\therminal` is a real UNC path Windows can stat. The distro name comes from the `WSL_DISTRO_NAME` env var that WSL sets inside the shell — but the GUI is on Windows, so it needs to discover the active distro a different way (config flag, or enumerate `wsl.exe -l -q`).

### D. Config escape hatch: `[hotspots] path_prefix_map`
`path_prefix_map = { "/home/marci" = "\\\\wsl.localhost\\Ubuntu\\home\\marci" }`. User-owned mapping; therminal applies it before `std::fs::metadata`, `open::that`, and the editor chain. Simple, deterministic, zero magic. Probably the right first step.

**Recommendation: D for v1, upgrade to A/C if multiple users hit this.**

## Scope

- Add `hotspots.path_prefix_map: HashMap<String, String>` to `HotspotsConfig`
- Apply the longest-match prefix substitution before `std::fs::metadata` in `promote_directory_hotspots_from_fs`
- Apply the same substitution before the editor chain (`window/hotspot_open.rs` / wherever files are opened) and before `folder_open.rs::open_folder_in_pane` / `open_folder_in_file_manager`
- Document in `docs/integrations/wsl.md` (if exists; otherwise folded into root CLAUDE.md WSL2 section)

## Also worth doing in the same pass

The user's config files (both WSL and Windows) predate the `[hotspots]` section and have NO custom `folder_pane_command` — they fall to the code default `["tfe", "{path}"]`. On Windows, `tfe` is not typically installed. The default platform detection in `HotspotsConfig::default()` should consider:
- Windows: leave `folder_pane_command` empty (fall straight through to the file-manager chain via Explorer) **or** use `["explorer", "{path}"]` as a reasonable default
- macOS: `["open", "{path}"]` for Finder-as-pane-equivalent

But changing defaults should be a separate decision — this issue is specifically the stat-fails-on-Windows part.

## Acceptance

- On Windows build with a configured `hotspots.path_prefix_map`, WSL-style paths in pane output get correctly classified as directories
- Right-click on a directory hotspot shows folder menu, not file menu
- Test coverage: unit test for `substitute_path_prefix()` + integration test that fakes the fs stat via the existing `is_dir_fn` callback

## Related

- tn-zqwg (original directory hotspot routing)
- tn-5akk (same Windows/WSL split, fixed on paste)
- tn-b77d (same split on mode flags)
notes:
## 2026-04-08 followup: tfe actually works in WSL panes

Re-examined the code path. The GUI's which_on_path("tfe") check is NOT the blocker — even when it returns false, the planner emits FallbackShell which still spawns the pane + writes 'cd' + shows a toast. User sees it visibly.

**The only thing preventing the folder menu from appearing is the std::fs::metadata call failing on Windows for /home/... paths.** Once is_dir is true, the folder menu appears, user clicks 'Open in new pane', which_on_path(tfe) returns false on Windows, fallback toast shows, pane splits with cd, user lands in the shell at the right directory. That's a degraded but usable outcome.

**Even better:** when the user runs in-pane via exec in the WSL bash shell, 'exec tfe /path' executes inside WSL where tfe IS installed. The which check only matters if you want to skip the exec entirely. Consider: should we skip the which_on_path pre-check on Windows (or cross-filesystem builds in general) and just always write the 'exec tfe' line into the pane? The worst case is a one-line error message inside the pane itself — the pane is still usable, cwd is still correct.

## Simpler v1 fix options (not requiring WSL path translation)

1. **Skip the is_dir stat on cross-filesystem paths.** On Windows, if the path starts with '/' (not 'C:\' etc.), treat it as 'probably a unix path, classify based on heuristics': trailing '/', no extension, known dir-name patterns (src, bin, etc.).
2. **Config-driven is_dir hint**: trust the detected path if it ends in '/' without stat'ing.
3. **Shift-click modifier**: let the user force the folder menu regardless of is_dir.
4. **Full fix (D from the original issue)**: hotspots.path_prefix_map config option.

Lean toward (1) + (2) as v1: cheap, no new config, works for 90% of cases. (4) is still the right long-term answer.
## Retro
- What worked: Lean Option B refactor — added pure heuristic + matched on Ok(true)/Ok(false)/Err in promote_directory_hotspots, no public API change. Pure function is trivially unit-testable.
- Heuristic ended up as 4 rules (rule 3 added during implementation): trailing slash, empty/root, bare-token-is-file, extensionless-last-component. The bare-token rule was needed to handle Makefile/README correctly per the manual sanity list — without it, rule 4 would misclassify them.
- What was unclear: The task spec listed 3 rules but the manual sanity required Makefile to return false. A bare-token guard before the extensionless rule resolved it cleanly.
- Test count: 36 hotspot_detection tests (was 24); added 8 new heuristic tests + 2 new promote_directory_hotspots tests; renamed the existing ignores_io_errors test to falls_through_to_heuristic_on_io_error and switched its input to /nope/missing.rs so the assertion still holds.
- Function signature: `pub fn classify_path_as_directory_heuristic(path: &str) -> bool`
- CI clean in worktree.
close_reason: Heuristic directory classification fallback lands in hotspot_detection.rs; unblocks directory right-click menu on the Windows build for WSL paths

title: Paste into focused pane inserts spurious line breaks (likely bracketed-paste regression)
description:
## Symptom

When pasting multi-line (or even single-line) text into a focused pane running an interactive app (observed: Claude Code TUI), the pasted content arrives with extra line breaks inserted, as if newlines in the clipboard buffer are being interpreted as Enter/submit rather than as literal characters inside a bracketed-paste envelope.

Reported by user mid-conversation while pasting prose into a Claude Code session running inside therminal.

## Where the code lives

\`crates/therminal-app/src/window/pane_ops.rs:1269\` — \`paste_clipboard()\`:

- Reads clipboard via \`crate::clipboard::paste_from_clipboard()\` (\`crates/therminal-app/src/clipboard.rs:129\`).
- Checks \`mode.contains(TermMode::BRACKETED_PASTE)\` from \`pane_term_mode()\`.
- If set, wraps the payload with \`\\x1b[200~\` ... \`\\x1b[201~\`.
- Writes \`text.as_bytes()\` **raw** with no newline normalization.

## Suspected causes (to investigate, in order)

1. **Bracketed-paste mode flag is not actually set on the daemon-managed pane.** Since tn-382v Phase B made the GUI a daemon client, \`pane_term_mode()\` reads from the locally synthesized \`Term\` populated by \`PaneStateSnapshot\` replay (tn-zamd). If \`TermMode::BRACKETED_PASTE\` isn't part of the snapshot's mode flags, every paste falls through the un-bracketed branch and the TUI sees raw \`\\n\` bytes — which it interprets as Enter. **This is the most likely cause given the timing** (paste worked in Phase A local-PTY mode).
2. **CRLF in clipboard.** On WSL2, clipboard text routed through \`pbpaste\`/\`win32yank\` may contain \`\\r\\n\`. We send those literally; some TUIs treat the \`\\r\` as a submit. Should normalize \`\\r\\n\` → \`\\n\` (and bare \`\\r\` → \`\\n\`) before writing.
3. **Trailing newline.** Many clipboard sources include a trailing \`\\n\`; inside a bracketed-paste envelope this is fine, outside it's a submit. Strip-trailing-newline is a common terminal behavior worth considering.

## How to verify cause #1

Add a \`tracing::debug!\` in \`paste_clipboard()\` logging \`bracketed\` and the full \`mode.bits()\`. Reproduce by running \`printf '\\e[?2004h'\` in the focused pane (enables bracketed paste mode) and pasting. If the debug line shows \`bracketed=false\` even after \`?2004h\`, the snapshot replay path is dropping the mode flag.

Cross-check: confirm \`PaneStateSnapshot\` in \`crates/therminal-protocol/src/daemon.rs\` actually serializes \`TermMode\` flags, and that the GUI reapplies them when reattaching.

## Acceptance

- Pasting multi-line text into Claude Code (or any \`?2004h\`-enabled TUI) inside a daemon-managed pane preserves newlines as literal characters inside the bracketed-paste envelope; no spurious submits.
- CRLF normalization applied before write.
- Regression test or manual repro steps documented.

## Out of scope

- Paste-from-selection (middle-click) — separate path if it exists.
- Paste into WebView pane backends — different path entirely.
notes:
## Shipped: workaround at the paste site

\`crates/therminal-app/src/window/pane_ops.rs\`:

1. **Always send the bracketed-paste envelope** (\`\\e[200~\` ... \`\\e[201~\`) regardless of the locally-tracked \`TermMode::BRACKETED_PASTE\` flag. The local flag is unreliable in daemon-client mode (tn-b77d).
2. **Normalize CRLF and bare CR to LF** via a new \`normalize_paste_text()\` helper. WSL2 clipboards routed through win32yank/WSLg often carry \`\\r\\n\`; without normalization the TUI sees the CR as a submit.
3. **Removed the now-dead \`pane_term_mode\` read** from \`paste_clipboard()\` and the unused \`TermMode\` import.

## Tests added

8 unit tests for \`normalize_paste_text\`: LF passthrough, CRLF collapse, bare CR collapse, mixed endings, trailing newline, empty input, no line endings, unicode. All pass.

## CI

\`./scripts/ci.sh\` green: fmt, clippy (\`-D warnings\`), build, full workspace test suite.

## What this does NOT fix

The underlying mode-flag drift between the daemon's authoritative \`TermMode\` and the GUI's local \`Term\` view — that's tn-b77d. \`mouse.rs\` still reads \`pane_term_mode\` at 4 sites and \`event_handler.rs\` at 1 site to make routing decisions, all of which can hit the same staleness bug for mouse-driven TUIs and arrow-key handling. tn-b77d is the architectural fix.

## Repro for verification

1. Open Claude Code (or any \`?2004h\`-enabled TUI) inside a daemon-managed pane.
2. Copy a multi-line text block.
3. Paste with the configured paste keybinding or right-click → Paste.

Before: each \`\\n\` in the paste was interpreted as Enter and submitted per line.
After: the entire paste arrives as one input with newlines preserved as literal characters inside the bracketed-paste envelope.
close_reason: Underlying mode-flag drift fixed in tn-b77d. The defensive always-wrap bracketed-paste envelope + CRLF normalization stays in place.

title: Package therminal-plugin AI skill in resources/skills/ for writing pattern packs
description:
## Context

Package the semantic pattern matching engine's authoring documentation as a distributable Claude Code skill. This is the piece that lets AI agents write pattern packs for therminal without ever loading therminal source into context — the key "clean context separation" goal from the brainstorm session.

Blocked on the pattern engine SPEC (for the authoring doc) and the implementation (to verify the skill's examples actually work).

## Scope

### 1. Skill directory

`resources/skills/therminal-plugin/` in the therminal repo (in-repo so it stays in sync with the shipped pattern engine version).

Structure:
```
resources/skills/therminal-plugin/
├── SKILL.md              # Top-level skill definition (Claude Code format)
├── schema.md             # The pattern pack schema reference
├── examples/             # Working example packs (same as plugins/examples/ but skill-scoped)
│   ├── cargo-errors.toml
│   ├── test-badges.toml
│   └── glossary.toml
└── cookbook.md           # "How do I do X?" recipes
```

### 2. SKILL.md

Claude Code skill format. Concise frontmatter + body describing:
- **Name:** therminal-plugin
- **Description:** "Use when the user wants to customize therminal terminal output — highlight patterns, add clickable hotspots, render widgets, or emit events for orchestrators. Writes pattern pack TOML files."
- **Trigger phrases:** "write a therminal pattern", "highlight X in my terminal", "make Y clickable in therminal", "add a therminal widget for Z"
- **Body:** quick-start + link to schema.md + cookbook.md

### 3. schema.md

The canonical schema reference, derived from `docs/pattern-packs-authoring.md` but trimmed for context efficiency. Target size: < 10KB. Covers:
- Pattern pack TOML structure
- All field names and types
- All action kinds (hotspot, widget, emit_event) with their sub-schemas
- All scope types with when to use each
- Scoping (global, harness, command)
- Regex flavor caveats (no lookaround, no backreferences — refer users to Rust `regex` docs)

### 4. cookbook.md

Task-oriented recipes. Each is ~15 lines: "the user wants X, here's the pack, here's why." Target 10-15 recipes covering the common cases:
- Highlight build errors as clickable hotspots
- Badge for test results
- Gauge for Claude context %
- Card popup for a glossary term
- Emit event when a long-running task completes
- Match across multiple lines within a command's output
- Only run a pattern when Claude is the active harness
- Only run a pattern inside cargo commands

### 5. Install script

Simple `resources/skills/install.sh` (or equivalent) that copies the skill into `~/.claude/skills/therminal-plugin/`. User-invoked. Not automatic.

### 6. CI check

Add a CI step that validates every example pack in `resources/skills/therminal-plugin/examples/` parses and loads against the real pattern engine. Prevents the skill from drifting out of sync with the implementation.

## Constraints

- **Total skill context footprint under 20KB.** Measure it. If it's over, trim examples or move edge cases to cookbook pages.
- **No therminal internals.** The skill should contain zero references to crate names, file paths inside `crates/`, Rust types, or wire formats. An AI using this skill should treat therminal as a black box that accepts pattern TOML files.
- **Must pass its own cookbook.** Every recipe in cookbook.md should produce a working pack that parses. Automated via the CI check.

## Acceptance

- Skill directory exists with the structure above
- Total uncompressed size < 20KB
- CI validates example packs against the real engine
- At least 10 cookbook recipes
- Can be copied into `~/.claude/skills/` and invoked

## When Done

`bd close` with the measured size + recipe count.
notes:

   ```
   resources/skills/therminal-plugin/
   ```
