close_reason: Dynamic tab names from cwd implemented

title: Smart context menu: pass through mouse to TUI apps, show menu otherwise
description:
Right-click context menu currently always shows on pane content, which conflicts with TUIs that have their own context menus (btop, lazygit, etc.).

Fix: check TermMode mouse flags (MOUSE_REPORT_CLICK, SGR_MOUSE, etc.) on the focused pane's terminal. If the app has mouse reporting enabled, pass the right-click through to the PTY. If not, show therminal's context menu.

Also add a tab bar right-click menu with tab-specific actions:
- New Tab
- Rename Tab
- Close Tab (close all panes in workspace)
- Move Pane to Tab N

This matches tmux behavior where right-click passes through to mouse-aware apps but shows tmux's menu for non-mouse-aware apps.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: Clean separation between mouse-mode detection and menu logic; existing pane_term_mode() and mouse encoding infrastructure made pass-through straightforward
- What was unclear: Nothing major; the flow was clear from reading the existing right-click handler
close_reason: Smart context menu with tab bar right-click implemented

title: Windows MCP server: daemon listen on named pipes
description:
The MCP stdio bridge (mcp_stdio.rs) already supports Windows named pipes on the client side, and socket_path() already returns `\\.\pipe\therminal-mcp` on Windows. But the daemon's MCP server (mcp.rs:start_mcp_server) only binds a Unix socket via tokio::net::UnixListener.

Need a Windows branch in start_mcp_server() using tokio::net::windows::named_pipe::ServerOptions to listen on the named pipe path. The accept loop should spawn the same TherminalMcpServer handler per connection.

Key files:
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp.rs (~line 1560) — add #[cfg(windows)] branch
- crates/therminal-runtime/src/paths.rs (~line 97) — path already correct
- crates/therminal-app/src/mcp_stdio.rs (~line 70) — client side already done
design: Add a #[cfg(windows)] block in start_mcp_server() that uses tokio::net::windows::named_pipe::ServerOptions to create and listen on the named pipe. Named pipes are single-instance, so after accepting a connection the server must create a new pipe instance for the next client (standard Windows named pipe server pattern). The rest of the handler (TherminalMcpServer, rmcp protocol) is platform-independent and shared.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
The MCP stdio bridge already handles Windows named pipes on the client side (mcp_stdio.rs:70-100). The socket path resolves to `\\.\pipe\therminal-mcp` on Windows. But the daemon's MCP server only listens on Unix sockets — so MCP doesn't work on Windows native builds.

## Task
Add a `#[cfg(windows)]` branch in `start_mcp_server()` that:
1. Uses `tokio::net::windows::named_pipe::ServerOptions` to create a named pipe
2. Accepts connections in a loop, spawning a new pipe instance after each accept (standard Windows named pipe server pattern)
3. Hands each connection to the same `TherminalMcpServer` handler via rmcp
4. Mirror the Unix path's permission setting, cleanup-on-shutdown, and stale-socket removal logic

Reference the existing Windows client code in mcp_stdio.rs:70-100 for the tokio named pipe API.

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp.rs (~line 1560, start_mcp_server)
- crates/therminal-app/src/mcp_stdio.rs (lines 70-100, client-side reference)
- crates/therminal-runtime/src/paths.rs (socket_path, line 97)

## When Done
Close issue: `bd close tn-68ma --reason "Daemon MCP server listens on Windows named pipes"`
close_reason: Daemon MCP server listens on Windows named pipes. Refactored start_mcp_server() with #[cfg(unix)] and #[cfg(windows)] branches. Unix path preserved as-is. Windows path uses tokio ServerOptions with first_pipe_instance + create-connect-handoff loop. Extracted generic spawn_mcp_connection() helper. All CI checks pass.

title: Upgrade sysinfo 0.33 → 0.38
description:
Low-effort upgrade isolated to therminal-terminal. Fixes Windows AV false positives, improves Linux process refresh perf. Key API change: `multithread` feature now opt-in, `kill_and_wait()` added, dead processes auto-removed.

Part of tn-x2zq (dependency upgrade epic).
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
sysinfo 0.33→0.38 is a low-effort upgrade isolated to therminal-terminal's process detector. Key changes: `multithread` feature now opt-in (v0.34), dead processes auto-removed on refresh, Windows drops `NtQueryInformationProcess` (fixes AV false positives), `kill_and_wait()` added (v0.35), MSRV 1.88 (v0.37).

## Task
Upgrade sysinfo from 0.33 to 0.38:

1. Update `sysinfo` version in workspace `Cargo.toml` from `"0.33"` to `"0.38"`
2. Check if `multithread` feature needs to be explicitly enabled (was default in 0.33, opt-in in 0.34+)
3. Fix any API changes in `crates/therminal-terminal/src/process_detector.rs` — this is the only file using sysinfo
4. The process tree walking API (`processes()`, `process()`, `parent()`) should be mostly stable — verify
5. Run `cargo build -p therminal-terminal && cargo test -p therminal-terminal`

## Key Files
- `Cargo.toml` — workspace dependency version
- `crates/therminal-terminal/Cargo.toml` — crate-level dep
- `crates/therminal-terminal/src/process_detector.rs` — all sysinfo usage

## When Done
Close issue: `bd close tn-d7hj --reason "sysinfo upgraded to 0.38"`
close_reason: sysinfo upgraded from 0.33 to 0.38 with multithread feature explicitly enabled. All 210 tests pass, no API changes needed in process_detector.rs.

title: Upgrade outdated dependencies (wgpu, cosmic-text, sysinfo + 6 others)
description:
9 workspace dependencies are behind latest. The three most outdated (wgpu, cosmic-text, sysinfo) have significant changes worth adopting. This epic tracks upgrading all of them.

## Dependency Overview

| Crate | Current | Target | Effort | Payoff |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **wgpu** | 23.0.1 | **28.x** | High | Compile time, perf, platform fixes |
| **cosmic-text** | 0.12.1 | **0.15.x** | Medium | ASCII fast-path (big terminal perf win) |
| **glyphon** | 0.7.0 | **0.10.0** | Medium | Coupled to wgpu+cosmic-text |
| **sysinfo** | 0.33.1 | 0.38.x | Low | Windows AV fix, Linux perf |
| **notify** | 7.0.0 | 8.x | Low | Config hot-reload crate |
| **dirs** | 5.0.1 | 6.x | Low | Path helpers |
| **portable-pty** | 0.8.1 | 0.9.x | Low | PTY lifecycle |
| **pollster** | 0.3.0 | 0.4.x | Low | Async block_on |
| **toml** | 0.8.23 | 1.x | Low | Config parsing |

**IMPORTANT**: glyphon 0.10 requires `wgpu ^28` and `cosmic-text ^0.15`. These three are version-locked and MUST be upgraded together. wgpu 29 is NOT compatible with glyphon 0.10 — target wgpu 28.

## wgpu 23 → 28

**v24**: All wgpu objects now `Clone` (internally refcounted) — drop `Arc` wrappers. `Instance::new()` takes `&InstanceDescriptor`.
**v25**: `device.poll()` API reworked (`Maintain` → `PollType`). Vulkan/GLES backends now behind feature flags.
**v26**: `as_hal` calls return guards instead of callbacks. DX12 no longer needs `dxil.dll`.
**v27**: `RenderPassDescriptor` gains `multiview_mask`. `enumerate_adapters` now async. `MipmapFilterMode` split from `FilterMode`.
**v28**: Mesh shaders. Deferred command encoding (perf change). Stricter validation.

## cosmic-text 0.12 → 0.15

**v0.13**: `ShapeBuffer` moved into `FontSystem`. Monospace fallback caching. Tab width support.
**v0.14**: Letter spacing, variable font features, configurable font fallback.
**v0.15**: **ASCII fast-path** skips complex shaping/bidi for ASCII runs — significant perf win for terminals. `rustybuzz` replaced by HarfRust (pure Rust). `set_text`/`set_rich_text` gain alignment param.

## sysinfo 0.33 → 0.38

**v0.34**: `multithread` feature now opt-in. Dead processes auto-removed on refresh. Windows: dropped `NtQueryInformationProcess` (fixes AV false positives).
**v0.35**: `kill_and_wait()` added. MSRV 1.75.
**v0.37**: MSRV 1.88 (Rust 2024 edition). Improved partial process retrieval perf on Linux.

## Execution order

1. **MSRV bump** (prerequisite for all)
2. **sysinfo** + **render triplet** + **low-effort 5** (parallel after MSRV)
3. **Gate: full check** after all complete
design:
Upgrade order matters because wgpu, cosmic-text, and glyphon are tightly coupled through the text rendering pipeline. cosmic-text feeds shaped glyphs to glyphon which renders via wgpu. Upgrading cosmic-text alone may work if glyphon 0.7 is compatible, but glyphon 0.10 likely requires wgpu 29. Check glyphon's wgpu version requirements before deciding whether to do cosmic-text alone or bundle all three.

MSRV will need to go to at least 1.88 (sysinfo 0.37 requirement). Verify CI toolchain version.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Client-side decorations: custom title bar with tabs and settings menu
description:
Replace OS-native window decorations with a custom GPU-rendered title bar, giving therminal a consistent cross-platform look and enabling tabs-in-titlebar UX similar to Windows Terminal.

## What
- Disable OS window decorations (`window.set_decorations(false)`) via winit
- Render a custom title bar with: window drag area, minimize/maximize/close buttons, workspace tabs (move from current tab bar), settings/hamburger menu button
- Implement window drag (on title bar click-drag), double-click to maximize/restore
- Platform-specific: respect macOS traffic light positioning (left side), Windows/Linux close button positioning (right side)
- Settings menu dropdown: font size, theme, config file open, about

## Why
- Consistent cross-platform appearance — same chrome on Windows, Linux, macOS
- Tabs-in-titlebar saves vertical space (merge current tab bar into title bar)
- Settings menu provides discoverability for config options without requiring manual TOML editing
- Professional terminal emulators (Windows Terminal, iTerm2, Warp) all use custom chrome

## Considerations
- Window dragging must feel native (no lag, correct hit-testing)
- macOS has strong conventions around traffic light position and full-screen behavior
- Wayland CSD is the norm; X11 needs careful handling of WM hints
- Config toggle: `general.use_custom_titlebar` (default true) with fallback to OS decorations
- Accessibility: title bar buttons need proper contrast and sizing
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: Clean separation of CSD logic from existing tab bar code. The `effective_tab_bar_height_csd` approach keeps backward compatibility while adding CSD awareness throughout the layout pipeline.
- What was unclear: Nothing major -- the codebase is well-structured with clear separation of chrome rendering, mouse handling, and geometry constants.
close_reason: CSD title bar implemented with window controls, drag-to-move, double-click maximize, and use_csd config toggle

title: Add tests for window controller layer (mod.rs, pane_ops.rs, mouse.rs)
description:
The window controller files (`window/mod.rs`, `window/pane_ops.rs`, `window/mouse.rs`) have zero test coverage, in contrast to state_inference.rs (500 lines of tests) and pane.rs (750 lines).

After the window facade is centralized, add focused tests covering:
- Relayout correctness after split/close/swap operations
- Workspace switching preserves layout state
- Separator drag updates split ratios correctly
- Focus navigation (next/prev pane) in various layout configurations
- Edge cases: close last pane, split when at minimum size, workspace with single pane

These tests should exercise the facade/helper layer, not require a full GPU context. May need to extract testable logic from the winit event loop into pure functions.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
The window controller layer (mod.rs, pane_ops.rs, mouse.rs) has zero test coverage. After the facade refactor, the helper methods provide clean testable seams.

## Task
Add focused unit tests for the window controller facade:

1. Test `relayout_and_redraw()` produces correct geometry after split/close/swap
2. Test workspace switching preserves and restores layout state
3. Test separator drag correctly updates split ratios
4. Test focus navigation (next/prev) in various layout configurations (2-pane, 3-pane, nested splits)
5. Test edge cases: close last pane behavior, split at minimum size, single-pane workspace

Tests should exercise pure logic, not require GPU context. May need to extract testable functions from event loop code or mock the renderer.

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window/pane_ops.rs` (facade methods to test)
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window/mouse.rs` (separator drag logic)
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane/layout.rs` (layout tree, already has tests for reference)

## Verification
Run `./scripts/ci.sh`. New tests must pass.

## When Done
Close issue: `bd close tn-1drw --reason "added N tests covering window controller facade"`
## Retro
- What worked: Testing pure data structures (WorkspaceManager, geometry helpers) without GPU context was straightforward. The existing test_leaf helper pattern in layout.rs was easy to reuse.
- What was unclear: Nothing major -- the task was well-scoped with clear focus areas.
close_reason: Added 42 unit tests: 14 for geometry helpers (content_area_rect, effective_*_height edge cases), 28 for WorkspaceManager (save/take/has_saved_layout roundtrip, switch_to, send_pane_to, workspace_ids consistency, remove_empty_active, focus transfer). All CI checks pass.

title: Centralize window controller facade for relayout/redraw/workspace access
description:
Create a small window controller helper layer to centralize the repeated patterns across `window/mod.rs`, `window/pane_ops.rs`, and `window/mouse.rs`.

Currently all three files repeat:
1. Workspace access: `self.workspaces.as_ref().map(|wm| wm.layout())` and mutable variants
2. Rect computation: `content_area_rect(gpu.config.width, gpu.config.height, show_status_bar, show_tab_bar)`
3. Relayout sequence: `layout.layout(rect)` → `layout.resize_all_panes(renderer)` → `w.request_redraw()`

The 4 macros in pane_ops.rs (`ws_layout_mut!`, `ws_layout!`, `ws_focused!`, `ws_set_focused!`) are a symptom of borrow-checker friction from this pattern.

Create helper methods (on App or a small WindowController struct) that:
- `get_layout()` / `get_layout_mut()` — replace macro + Option chain boilerplate
- `compute_layout_rect()` — bundle config + GPU dimensions into a single call
- `relayout_and_redraw()` — atomic relayout + resize_all_panes + request_redraw

Replace the 4 macros in pane_ops.rs with explicit helper method calls.

Depends on pane.rs split (tn-yu08) so layout/workspace are clean modules to build on.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: facade methods (focused_pane, set_focused_pane, compute_layout_rect, relayout_and_redraw, request_redraw) cleanly replaced macros and centralized repeated patterns. relayout_and_redraw eliminated ~10 inline relayout+resize+redraw sequences.
- What was unclear: get_layout/get_layout_mut borrow all of self, so they cannot be used when other fields (grid_renderer, config, modifiers) are simultaneously borrowed. Had to keep direct field access (self.workspaces.as_mut().map(|wm| wm.layout_mut())) in split/render/key-input methods. This is a fundamental Rust limitation -- the methods are useful for simple cases but not a universal replacement for the macros in borrow-heavy contexts.
close_reason: Removed all 4 workspace macros (ws_layout!, ws_layout_mut!, ws_focused!, ws_set_focused!) from pane_ops.rs. Added 7 facade methods to App: get_layout, get_layout_mut, focused_pane, set_focused_pane, compute_layout_rect, relayout_and_redraw, request_redraw. Replaced ~30 call sites across mod.rs, pane_ops.rs, and mouse.rs. relayout_and_redraw centralized the repeated layout+resize+redraw pattern that appeared ~10 times. Direct field access retained where borrow checker requires simultaneous access to multiple App fields.

title: Refactor maintainability hotspots in terminal runtime, window controller, and config modules
description:
Codebase health review identified several maintainability and architecture hotspots in first-party crates under `crates/`.

Primary findings:
- `crates/therminal-terminal/src/state_inference.rs` is a major hotspot (~2553 LOC) mixing pattern compilation, inference engine logic, cadence classification, command telemetry, state-file persistence, notifications, ANSI stripping helpers, and a large in-file test block.
- PTY/terminal runtime lifecycle code is duplicated between the GUI app and daemon. Both `crates/therminal-app/src/pane.rs` and `crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs` construct `Term`, spawn PTYs, clone readers/writers, and run ANSI processing loops.
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane.rs` mixes layout-tree logic, workspace management, pane runtime, PTY reader behavior, geometry constants, and tests in one module (~2066 LOC).
- The window layer has blurred ownership across `crates/therminal-app/src/window/mod.rs`, `crates/therminal-app/src/window/pane_ops.rs`, and `crates/therminal-app/src/window/mouse.rs`, with repeated relayout, redraw, workspace access, and pane spawn/config assembly logic.
- `crates/therminal-core/src/config.rs` (~2037 LOC) combines config schema/defaults, commented config generation, action metadata, default bindings, parser logic, and a large in-file test suite.
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window/pane_ops.rs` uses macros as a borrow-management layer for workspace access, which obscures control flow.
- The highest-risk UI controller files (`window/mod.rs`, `window/pane_ops.rs`, `window/mouse.rs`) appear to have little direct test coverage compared with the larger but better-tested core files.

Suggested refactor direction:
- Extract a shared terminal runtime / pane engine into `therminal-terminal` for PTY + `Term` lifecycle concerns used by both app and daemon.
- Split `state_inference.rs` by responsibility, likely into modules such as `patterns`, `engine`, `cadence`, `persistence`, and `test_support`.
- Split `pane.rs` into layout/workspace/runtime-focused modules.
- Introduce a small window-controller facade or helper layer to centralize relayout, redraw, and pane spawning behavior.
- Split `config.rs` into config schema, config docs generation, and keybinding parsing/metadata modules.
- Replace workspace access macros in `pane_ops.rs` with explicit helper methods where possible.
- Add focused tests around the refactored window/controller boundaries.
acceptance_criteria:
- A concrete refactor plan exists for the identified hotspots, with boundaries proposed for shared terminal runtime, state inference, pane/layout logic, window controller logic, and config/keybinding logic.
- Follow-up implementation issues are created or tracked for the major refactor areas.
- Any approved refactors reduce module responsibility mixing and duplicated PTY/terminal lifecycle code.
- New or reorganized tests cover the highest-risk controller/runtime seams introduced by the refactor.
close_reason: all 6 subtasks complete: 3 module splits, PTY runtime extraction, window facade, controller tests

title: Windows named pipe transport for MCP stdio bridge and daemon IPC
description:
The MCP stdio bridge (mcp_stdio.rs) and daemon IPC use Unix sockets, which don't exist on Windows. Need to add named pipe support so MCP works when therminal runs natively on Windows.

The `interprocess` crate (already a dependency in therminal-daemon) supports both Unix sockets and Windows named pipes. The change is:

1. mcp_stdio.rs: Use `tokio::net::windows::named_pipe` or interprocess's named pipe client instead of `UnixStream`
2. therminal-daemon server.rs/client.rs: Already uses interprocess which abstracts this — verify it works on Windows
3. MCP socket path config: On Windows, use a named pipe path (e.g. `\\.\pipe\therminal-mcp`) instead of a Unix socket path

The stdio bridge itself (stdin/stdout copying) is already cross-platform — only the internal daemon connection needs the transport swap.
close_reason: Cross-platform MCP transport: Unix sockets on Linux/macOS, named pipes on Windows. socket_path() returns platform-appropriate IPC paths.

title: Bootstrap MSVC build environment in Windows build helper
description: The Windows-native PowerShell build helper should automatically initialize the Visual Studio Build Tools environment when link.exe is not already on PATH. This avoids requiring the user to manually open a Developer PowerShell before running the helper.
acceptance_criteria:
- build-windows.ps1 detects missing link.exe.
- If Visual Studio Build Tools are installed, the script invokes cargo through VsDevCmd.bat or equivalent so MSVC linker tools are available.
- Missing build tools still produce a clear actionable error.
close_reason: Updated build-windows.ps1 to locate VsDevCmd.bat via vswhere and run cargo through the MSVC developer environment when link.exe is missing.

title: Make Windows build helper runnable from outside the repo
description: The new PowerShell build helper currently derives the repo root from its own path, which fails if the script is copied to Desktop or another folder. Update the script to accept an explicit repo root and provide a simple Windows-side launcher/wrapper for the current WSL-hosted repo path.
acceptance_criteria:
- build-windows.ps1 accepts an explicit repo root override.
- Running the helper from outside the repo no longer misdetects the repo root.
- A copied launcher/script on the Windows Desktop can invoke the repo-hosted build script against the current WSL repo path.
close_reason: Updated build-windows.ps1 to accept an explicit repo root and created a Desktop launcher targeting the current WSL repo path.

title: Add Windows-native PowerShell build/deploy helper for therminal-app
description: Add a PowerShell script intended to be run on Windows that builds therminal-app natively and optionally copies the resulting EXE to a Windows Desktop path. This is for easier native Windows testing from a repo shared with WSL2, and should be kept separate from the Linux CI script.
acceptance_criteria:
- Repo contains a PowerShell script for native Windows builds of therminal-app.
- Script builds therminal-app in release mode by default.
- Script can copy therminal-app.exe to a configurable Windows Desktop destination.
- README documents the recommended Windows-native workflow and script usage.
close_reason: Added a Windows-native PowerShell build/deploy helper and documented the recommended workflow in the README.

title: Cache overlay text buffers (status bar, tab bar, pane headers)
description:
Status bar, tab bar, and pane headers create new cosmic-text Buffer objects and call shape_until_scroll() every frame (chrome.rs:338-453). Text shaping is expensive and these strings rarely change.

Fix: cache shaped Buffers keyed by (rendered_string, width, focus_state). Only reshape when inputs change. For status bar: CWD, exit code, dimensions. For tab bar: active workspace, workspace count. For pane headers: pane index, focus state.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Status bar, tab bar, and pane headers create 10 new cosmic-text Buffer objects and call shape_until_scroll() every frame (chrome.rs). Text shaping is expensive and these strings change rarely — CWD changes on `cd`, exit code on command finish, workspace on switch.

## Task
Cache shaped Buffers for overlay text:

1. Add a cache struct in `chrome.rs` (or on GridRenderer): `OverlayCache` with fields for each overlay region. Each entry stores `(key: String, buffer: Buffer)` where key is the rendered text.

2. For each Buffer::new() call site in chrome.rs (10 total — lines 338, 368, 407, 423, 439, 667, 708, 729, 771, 1049):
   - Before creating a new Buffer, check if the cache entry's key matches the current text
   - If match: reuse the cached Buffer (just reference it for rendering)
   - If miss: create new Buffer, shape it, store in cache

3. The cache key for each section should be the concatenation of all inputs that affect the shaped output: text content + width + any style flags (focus state, active tab, etc.).

4. Static buttons ("X", "V", "H") should be shaped once and never reshaped since their text never changes.

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/chrome.rs (all 10 Buffer::new sites — draw_pane_header, draw_status_bar, draw_tab_bar)
- crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs (GridRenderer struct — could host the cache, or it can be standalone)

## When Done
Run `./scripts/ci.sh` to verify. Close issue: `bd close tn-149g --reason "summary"`
close_reason: Slot-based OverlayCache on GridRenderer caches shaped Buffers for status bar, tab bar, pane headers; only reshapes on text/width/style change

title: Replace per-cell String cloning for hotspot/hyperlink payloads with Arc<str>
description:
Hotspot and hyperlink text is cloned into every covered RenderCell (render.rs:210), then cloned again into row_cache (grid_renderer.rs:751-762), then cloned again into hotspot_map/hyperlink_map (grid_renderer.rs:961). A single match spanning 40 columns produces 40 String clones × 3 copies = 120 allocations.

Fix: store the matched text as Arc<str> so all cells sharing a match share one allocation. The maps and cache get cheap Arc clones (pointer bump) instead of String copies.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Hotspot and hyperlink text is cloned into every covered RenderCell, then cloned again into row_cache, then again into hotspot_map/hyperlink_map. A 40-column match = 120 String allocations. Arc<str> reduces this to pointer bumps.

## Task
Replace per-cell String cloning with Arc<str>:

1. In `hotspot_detection.rs`, change `Hotspot.text: String` to `Hotspot.text: Arc<str>`.
2. In `grid_renderer.rs`, change `RenderCell.hotspot: Option<(HotspotKind, String)>` to `Option<(HotspotKind, Arc<str>)>`.
3. In `grid_renderer.rs`, change `hotspot_map` value type from `(HotspotKind, String)` to `(HotspotKind, Arc<str>)`.
4. Do the same for `hyperlink_map` and `RenderCell.hyperlink` if they carry String payloads.
5. In `render.rs:204-213`, when annotating cells with hotspot data, use `Arc::clone()` instead of `String::clone()`.
6. In `url_detection.rs`, same treatment for URL text if it carries owned Strings.
7. Update `mouse.rs` click handlers to work with Arc<str> (deref to &str transparently).

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/hotspot_detection.rs (Hotspot struct)
- crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs (RenderCell, hotspot_map, hyperlink_map)
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/render.rs (cell annotation loop at :204-213)
- crates/therminal-app/src/url_detection.rs (URL text)
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/mouse.rs (click handlers)

## When Done
Run `./scripts/ci.sh` to verify. Close issue: `bd close tn-abw --reason "summary"`
close_reason: Arc<str> replaces String for hotspot/hyperlink payloads in RenderCell, detection, maps, and cache. ~120 String allocs per 40-col match -> 1 Arc + pointer bumps

title: Fix: hotspot_map only retains last-rendered pane in multi-pane mode
description:
In multi-pane mode, `reset_pane_caches()` clears `hotspot_map` before rendering each pane. After the render loop completes, only the last pane's hotspots survive in the map. Mouse hover/click queries (`hotspot_at()`) check this single map, so hotspot interaction only works on the last-rendered pane.

Same bug likely affects `hyperlink_map`.

Fix: either key the maps by pane ID, or store per-pane maps that don't get cleared between panes.
close_reason: Maps keyed by (PaneId, row, col), cleared once per frame — all panes' hotspots now survive

title: SCM_RIGHTS FD passing for true zero-downtime daemon handoff
description:
When the daemon does need to restart (protocol version bump), use SCM_RIGHTS to pass PTY master file descriptors from old daemon to new daemon over the Unix socket. This preserves live PTY sessions across daemon restarts.

Handoff flow:
1. New daemon connects to old daemon, sends GracefulShutdown with FD transfer request
2. Old daemon serializes session state (session IDs, names, workspace mapping, pane metadata)
3. Old daemon sends PTY master FDs via SCM_RIGHTS ancillary message
4. New daemon receives FDs, reconstructs SessionManager, reconnects reader threads
5. Old daemon exits, new daemon binds socket

Platform notes:
- Linux/macOS: SCM_RIGHTS is standard, well-supported
- Windows: named pipes don't support FD passing — would need a different strategy (or accept session loss on Windows daemon restarts)

This is how HAProxy/nginx/systemd handle zero-downtime restarts. Well-understood pattern.

Depends on PROTOCOL_VERSION landing first (tn-xxx) since that reduces how often this path is hit.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Current daemon handoff (handoff.rs) does a graceful shutdown + restart — the new daemon spawns fresh PTYs. With SCM_RIGHTS (Unix) or handle duplication (Windows), file descriptors for existing PTY masters can be passed to the new daemon process, achieving true zero-downtime: running commands don't lose their PTY.

## Task
Implement FD passing for daemon handoff:

1. On Unix: use `sendmsg`/`recvmsg` with SCM_RIGHTS ancillary data to pass PTY master FDs from old daemon to new daemon over the Unix socket
2. Define a handoff protocol: old daemon sends session metadata + FD array, new daemon reconstructs Session/Pane structs around received FDs
3. Modify `handoff.rs` `perform_handoff()` to use FD passing instead of clean restart
4. The new daemon needs to re-attach reader threads to the received FDs
5. On Windows: use `DuplicateHandle` with target process handle (different API, same concept)
6. Fallback: if FD passing fails, fall back to current graceful restart behavior
7. This is Linux/macOS only initially — gate behind `#[cfg(unix)]`

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/handoff.rs` — current handoff logic
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs` — Session/Pane reconstruction from FDs
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/framing.rs` — may need ancillary data support
- `crates/therminal-protocol/src/daemon.rs` — handoff message types

## When Done
Close issue: `bd close tn-jjl --reason "SCM_RIGHTS FD passing implemented"`
close_reason: SCM_RIGHTS FD passing implemented for Unix daemon handoff. Old daemon sends PTY master FDs via sendmsg/recvmsg ancillary data over a temporary handoff socket. New daemon reconstructs Session/Pane structs around received FDs with re-attached reader threads. Falls back to graceful restart if FD passing fails. Gated behind #[cfg(unix)].

title: Replace window title bar with workspace tab strip
description:
The window title bar currently shows a plain white "Therminal" title — wasted space. Replace it with a workspace tab strip showing numbered workspace tabs (1-9), with the active one highlighted.

This moves workspace indicators from the status bar (where they're small and easy to miss) into the title bar area where tabs traditionally live. Each tab could show the workspace number and optionally a name or the focused pane's CWD.

Design considerations:
- Could use a custom-drawn tab bar in the window content area (like how VS Code does it)
- Or use platform-native tab APIs if available
- The existing WorkspaceManager already has all the data — just needs a renderer
- Remove the workspace indicator from the status bar once tabs are in the title area
- Consider whether to keep the window title text somewhere (maybe in the active tab)
close_reason: Workspace tab strip replaces title bar — numbered tabs with click-to-switch. Added draw_tab_bar() in chrome.rs, tab click handling in mouse.rs, reserved 24px at top for tab bar, wired into render loop, added show_tab_bar config option with hot-reload support.

title: MCP tool: list_workspaces — expose tab/workspace state to agents
description:
Add a list_workspaces MCP tool that exposes the full workspace tab hierarchy. Currently WorkspaceManager in pane.rs manages named workspaces (Alt+1..9) but this state is invisible to MCP clients.

Should return: workspace ID/index, name, which workspace is active, and the pane IDs within each workspace. This gives agents the full picture of how the terminal is organized.

Part of making the complete workspace hierarchy queryable: workspaces → sessions → panes.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
With workspace state synced to the daemon (tn-9ub), agents can now query workspace/tab structure. This enables orchestration tools to understand the user's workspace layout.

## Task
Add a `terminal.workspaces.list` MCP tool:

1. Define `ListWorkspacesParam`: optional `session_id` filter
2. Result: vec of `{workspace_id, name, pane_count, is_active, pane_ids}`
3. Implement: read workspace data from daemon's Session model (added by tn-9ub)
4. Trust tier: Observer
5. Use naming convention from tn-8bu

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp.rs` — add tool
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs` — access workspace data from Session model

## When Done
Close issue: `bd close tn-g0b --reason "list_workspaces MCP tool implemented"`
close_reason: list_workspaces MCP tool implemented

title: Semantic scrollback navigation UI
description: Build a navigation UI for semantic scrollback — jump between typed regions (prompts, commands, outputs, errors, tool calls). Could be a sidebar, overlay, or keyboard-driven navigator.\n\nConsumes the region_index data. Consider whether this is better as an in-terminal overlay or a TUI connecting via MCP (per the gray zone in CLAUDE.md scope boundary).
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: RegionIndex already had clean event push API; adding region_before/region_after queries was straightforward. Wiring shared Arc<Mutex<RegionIndex>> through PaneState mirrored the existing PaneStatus pattern.
- What was unclear: ctrl+shift+up/down were already bound to FocusUp/Down, so used ctrl+alt+up/down for region nav and ctrl+alt+pageup/pagedown for error nav. Toast overlay was kept as a logged label only — full chrome rendering deferred.
close_reason: Semantic scrollback navigation implemented

title: Widget pre-rasterization via tiny-skia
description:
Implement widget rendering using tiny-skia to pre-rasterize overlay widgets into textures. Only re-rasterize when data changes (not every frame). Widgets include:
- Context gauge (token usage)
- Tool call cards
- Thinking indicator
- Agent status badges

Textures are composited in the overlay render pass.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Phase 6 widgets (context gauge, tool call cards, thinking indicator, agent badges) need to be rendered as overlay elements. Rather than re-rendering every frame, pre-rasterize them into textures using tiny-skia and only re-rasterize when data changes.

## Task
1. Add tiny-skia as a dependency
2. Create a `WidgetRasterizer` that renders widget shapes/text to a Pixmap
3. Upload rasterized Pixmaps as wgpu textures
4. Track data freshness — only re-rasterize when the widget's source data changes
5. Composite widget textures in the overlay render pass (from tn-9k2)
6. Implement at least one widget as proof of concept (e.g., agent status badge)

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs (texture management, render pipeline)
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/render.rs (render orchestration)

## When Done
Close issue: `bd close tn-npd --reason "Widget pre-rasterization via tiny-skia implemented"`
## Retro
- What worked: tiny-skia 0.11 was already transitive via winit/sctk-adwaita, so hoisting to [workspace.dependencies] was a one-line change with zero build-graph cost. The existing OverlayLayer's tier design made it natural to slot the widget pass in right after chrome_overlay.render() — no restructuring of render_driver needed. Freshness cache unit tests ride on a pure-CPU TestCache shim that mirrors the WidgetManager::upsert decision tree, so the rasterize-vs-noop contract is covered without needing a live wgpu device in tests.
- What was unclear: tiny-skia has no text stack, which the task brief glossed over. Decided to keep text rendering out of the pixmap entirely — the pill background is pre-rasterized but the 'claude · thinking' label is drawn on top via the existing glyphon overlay_text_renderer. Documented as a v1 scope boundary in crates/therminal-app/src/widgets/mod.rs; a follow-up can move text into the pixmap once a proper fontdue / cosmic-text path is chosen. Also: wgpu 28 split FilterMode into FilterMode + MipmapFilterMode — a small gotcha when writing SamplerDescriptor from scratch.
- Manual verification: documented in crates/therminal-app/src/widgets/gpu.rs (bottom) — run therminal with an agent (e.g. claude) in the focused pane, observe the top-right pill, set RUST_LOG=therminal_app::widgets=debug to watch rasterization events fire once per distinct agent state (not once per frame).
close_reason: tiny-skia rasterizer + freshness cache + agent status badge PoC composited in overlay pass

title: PaneBackend abstraction: Terminal | WebView
description:
Refactor the pane system to support a PaneBackend trait/enum that abstracts over Terminal and WebView pane types. Both should support:
- Tiling layout participation
- Geometry queries
- MCP tool access
- Event bus integration

This is the architectural foundation for WebView panes — split it out so the abstraction can land independently.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: systematic search-then-update approach; updating all consumers via grep before building ensured thorough coverage
- What was unclear: linter auto-modifications to spawn.rs/auto_tile.rs created merge noise; timing-dependent auto_tile test is flaky
close_reason: PaneBackend abstraction landed: PaneBackendKind enum (Terminal|WebView), PaneBackend trait (write_input/resize/get_content/backend_type), all consumers updated, CI passes

title: WM-style split targeting: split the largest pane, enforce minimums
description:
When auto-tiling spawns a new agent pane, it currently bisects the parent pane — which quickly produces tiny unusable panes with multiple agents. Instead, replicate WM-style (Hyprland) behavior:

1. Find the largest pane by area and split that one, not the parent
2. Enforce minimum viable pane size (e.g., 40 cols x 10 rows) — refuse to split below this
3. On agent exit, compact the layout: remove the pane and rebalance the tree to fill gaps

This is the equivalent of what thermal-desktop gets for free from Hyprland's tiling WM.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Auto-tiling (auto_tile.rs) splits the parent pane when an agent spawns. With multiple agents this creates tiny unusable panes via nested binary splits. Hyprland-style WMs solve this by always splitting the largest available space. therminal needs the same behavior, plus minimum size enforcement.

## Task
1. Add `find_largest_pane(&self) -> Option<PaneId>` to `LayoutNode` in layout.rs — traverse all leaves and return the one with the largest viewport area (width * height)
2. In `pane_ops.rs` `poll_auto_tile()` (~line 869), change the Split action to target the largest pane instead of the parent pane: call `find_largest_pane()` on the layout and split that pane
3. Add minimum pane size enforcement: before splitting, check if the resulting panes would be below `MIN_PANE_WIDTH` (already defined in geometry.rs) and `MIN_PANE_HEIGHT`. If so, refuse to split and log a warning
4. Add `compact_layout(&mut self)` to LayoutNode that removes Empty leaves and rebalances split ratios proportionally
5. Wire `compact_layout()` into the Reclaim action path in `poll_auto_tile()` after closing an auto-tiled pane
6. Add tests for `find_largest_pane()` and minimum size enforcement

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-app/src/pane/layout.rs (LayoutNode enum, split_pane, add find_largest_pane + compact_layout)
- crates/therminal-app/src/pane/auto_tile.rs (AutoTileDebouncer, AutoTileAction)
- crates/therminal-app/src/window/pane_ops.rs (poll_auto_tile ~line 869)
- crates/therminal-app/src/pane/geometry.rs (MIN_PANE_WIDTH, MIN_PANE_HEIGHT constants)

title: Hotspot rendering: underline on hover, action palette on click
description:
Render detected hotspots (URLs, file paths, errors, etc.) with visual affordances:

- Underline + cursor change on hover (dotted underline for detected patterns, solid for OSC 8)
- On click: open action palette showing contextual actions (open in editor, open in browser, copy, re-run in pane)

Depends on hotspot detection engine being complete. Should integrate with the existing mouse hit-testing in window/mouse.rs.

- What was unclear: Whether HotspotKind should carry the full matched text per-cell or just the kind. Solved by changing RenderCell.hotspot from Option<HotspotKind> to Option<(HotspotKind, String)> so each cell knows the full matched text for palette building.

title: Complete Layer 4 state machine: add Streaming and Thinking states
description:
The composite state machine in state_inference.rs has 4 of 6 planned states (Idle, Processing, ToolUse, AwaitingInput). Add the missing Streaming and Thinking states per PLAN.md spec:

- Streaming: sustained >500 chars/sec, monotonic, no backspaces (90% confidence)
- Thinking: spinner/indicator detected, extended delay, no output (75% confidence)

The cadence analysis infrastructure already exists — this is about adding distinct enum variants and transition logic.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: Clear task spec with exact line numbers made implementation straightforward. Existing cadence analysis infrastructure made Streaming detection clean to add.
- What was unclear: Nothing significant -- the task was well-scoped.
close_reason: Added Streaming and Thinking variants to InferredStatus enum. Spinner/thinking-dots patterns now return Thinking instead of Processing. Sustained high-throughput output (>500 chars/sec, no backspaces, >2s) returns Streaming via new is_streaming_cadence() method. Updated 3 existing tests and added 13 new tests. All 76 state_inference tests pass, clippy clean.

title: Cross-platform clipboard support (WSL2, Wayland, X11, macOS, Windows)
description:
arboard's Wayland backend fails on WSL2 because WSLg doesn't support the wlr-data-control protocol. Currently the error is non-fatal (warns and continues), but copy/paste is broken.

Need a reliable clipboard strategy per platform:
- **WSL2**: arboard tries Wayland → fails. Options: force X11 backend via arboard's `SetExtLinux` with X11 preference, or shell out to `clip.exe` (write) / `powershell.exe Get-Clipboard` (read). powershell read is slow (~200ms).
- **Native Wayland**: arboard with `wayland-data-control` works on wlroots compositors. Need to verify Gnome/KDE Wayland support.
- **X11**: arboard X11 backend works fine.
- **macOS / Windows**: arboard's default backends should just work.

Current code: `crates/therminal-app/src/clipboard.rs` — creates a new `arboard::Clipboard` on every call. Could also cache the instance.

The shell integration (`therminal.bash`) has no clipboard handling — user's `.bashrc` has `pbcopy`/`pbpaste` aliases to `powershell.exe` as a workaround for CLI use, but that doesn't help Therminal's Ctrl+C/V or OSC 52 paths which go through arboard.
design:
Detect platform at clipboard init and choose backend:
1. WSL2 (check `WSL_DISTRO_NAME` env var): prefer X11 via `SetExtLinux`, or fallback to `clip.exe`/`powershell.exe`
2. Native Linux: let arboard auto-detect (Wayland → X11 fallback)
3. macOS/Windows: arboard defaults

Consider caching the `Clipboard` instance instead of creating new on every call.
acceptance_criteria:
- Copy/paste works on WSL2 without errors
- Copy/paste works on native Wayland (wlroots, Gnome, KDE)
- Copy/paste works on X11
- OSC 52 clipboard sequences work across all platforms
- No user-facing errors or warnings in normal operation
close_reason: Cached clipboard instance, WSL2 forces X11 backend by temporarily unsetting WAYLAND_DISPLAY during init. Eliminates repeated Wayland fallback warnings.

title: Workspace tabs: Alt+N to switch, Alt+Shift+N to send pane
description: Add workspace/tab support inspired by Hyprland. Each workspace is an independent pane layout. Alt+1-9 switches between workspaces, Alt+Shift+1-9 sends the focused pane to that workspace. Tab bar or indicator shows which workspace is active and how many panes each has. Start with a single default workspace, create new ones on first switch.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Therminal currently has a single layout tree (one "workspace"). Hyprland has multiple workspaces you switch between with mod+N. This adds workspace/tab support so you can have independent pane layouts and switch between them.

## Task
1. **Workspace struct** in `crates/therminal-app/src/pane.rs` (or new `workspace.rs`):
   - `struct Workspace { id: usize, layout: LayoutNode, focused_pane: Option<PaneId> }`
   - `struct WorkspaceManager { workspaces: Vec<Workspace>, active: usize }`
   - Create workspace 1 by default with the existing layout
   - Support up to 9 workspaces (1-9)

2. **Switch workspace** — `switch_to(n)`:
   - Save current focused_pane to current workspace
   - Set active = n, create workspace n if it doesn't exist (with one default pane)
   - Restore focused_pane from target workspace
   - Trigger full redraw

3. **Send pane to workspace** — `send_pane_to(pane_id, workspace_n)`:
   - Remove pane from current workspace's layout tree
   - Add pane as a new leaf in target workspace's layout (auto-split)
   - If current workspace becomes empty, create a new default pane

4. **Keybindings** in `crates/therminal-core/src/config.rs`:
   - Add `SwitchWorkspace(u8)` and `SendToWorkspace(u8)` KeyAction variants
   - Default bindings: `alt+1` through `alt+9` → SwitchWorkspace, `alt+shift+1` through `alt+shift+9` → SendToWorkspace

5. **Status bar update** in `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs`:
   - Show workspace indicators in status bar: `[1] 2  3` where active is highlighted

6. **Wire into App** in `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs`:
   - Replace `layout: Option<LayoutNode>` and `focused_pane` with `WorkspaceManager`
   - Update all references to layout/focused_pane to go through the manager

- `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs` — App struct, keybinding dispatch, status bar, render loop

title: Snappy pane spawn/kill lifecycle for AI agent workflows
description:
When AI agents are working, terminals get created and destroyed rapidly. This needs to feel instant and natural, not ceremonial.

Key behaviors:
- **Instant spawn**: new pane appears immediately with shell ready, no visible loading state
- **Graceful kill**: Ctrl+Shift+X kills focused pane, space redistributes instantly (ties into smart sizing)
- **Batch operations**: kill all panes in a group, spawn N panes at once (for agent swarms)
- **Animation**: subtle fade-in on spawn, collapse animation on kill (optional, must be fast enough to not feel sluggish — think 100ms max)
- **No confirmation dialogs**: if the shell has exited, the pane just closes. Running process gets a single "process running, kill?" inline prompt, not a modal
- **Session memory**: daemon already tracks sessions — expose "restore last layout" for when you accidentally kill everything

This is the workflow enabler. If spawning a terminal has any friction, agents (and users) will avoid it.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
After tn-lpj (smart sizing) provides auto-rebalance and tn-tj1 (gate) verifies the build, this issue makes pane creation and destruction feel instant and natural for AI agent workflows where terminals are spawned and killed rapidly.

## Task
Optimize pane spawn/kill lifecycle:

1. **Instant spawn** in `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs`:
   - `split_focused_pane()` should be as fast as possible — profile and remove any blocking work
   - PTY spawn should happen off the main thread if it doesn't already
   - New pane should render on the very next frame (no intermediate blank state)

2. **Quick kill** in `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs`:
   - `close_focused_pane()` → immediate removal from layout tree, instant rebalance
   - If shell has already exited (PTY EOF), skip any confirmation
   - If process still running: inline warning in status bar "Kill running process? (y/n)" — NOT a modal dialog
   - On confirm or if shell exited: drop PTY, remove pane, rebalance, redraw

3. **Batch operations** — add new methods:
   - `close_all_panes()` — kill everything, single rebalance at end
   - `spawn_n_panes(n: usize)` — create N panes with auto-tiling layout (use smart split direction from tn-lpj)
   - Wire these to keybindings: Ctrl+Shift+Q (close all with confirmation), Ctrl+Shift+N (spawn dialog? or just spawn one)

4. **Session restore** via daemon:
   - On `close_all_panes()`, snapshot the layout tree (split directions, ratios) to daemon session state
   - Add "Restore Layout" keybinding that recreates panes from snapshot
   - This is a lightweight version — no scrollback restore, just pane geometry

5. **Animation** (optional, low priority):
   - If time permits, add a 50-100ms fade-in on new pane (animate alpha from 0→1)
   - Collapse animation on kill (shrink to zero width/height over 50ms)
   - Must be skippable (if next spawn happens during animation, cancel it)
   - If this adds complexity, skip it — snappiness > prettiness

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs` — split_focused_pane, close_focused_pane, new batch methods
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane.rs` — layout tree manipulation, rebalance
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs` — layout snapshot for restore

## When Done
Run `./scripts/ci.sh` to verify build + tests pass.
`bd close tn-h2y --reason "Snappy pane lifecycle: instant spawn/kill, batch ops, layout restore"`
close_reason: Snappy pane lifecycle: batch close/restore, layout snapshots, spawn_n_panes API

title: Brainstorm: Therminal UI design — chrome, themes, and interaction model
description:
Design the user-facing UI layer for therminal. The internals are solid (splits, mouse routing, config hot-reload, color overrides) but the terminal is visually bare — no chrome, no theme system, no discoverability.

Topics to explore:
- **Pane chrome**: tab bar vs header strip vs minimal separator lines. When to show/hide chrome (single pane = no chrome?)
- **Theme system**: preset themes (dark/light/solarized/etc), custom theme format, theme picker UI, live preview during selection
- **Command palette**: Ctrl+Shift+P style action palette for discoverability (split, close, theme, config)
- **Settings UI**: in-terminal settings panel vs edit-the-TOML-file approach vs both
- **Agent overlays**: how to render thinking indicators, tool call cards, status badges without cluttering the terminal
- **Hotspot rendering**: clickable file paths, URLs, errors — underline style, hover highlight, click behavior
- **Status bar**: bottom bar with session info, agent status, git branch, etc
- **Font/color picker**: visual font preview, color scheme editor
- **Competitive reference**: what do Warp, Ghostty, WezTerm, iTerm2 do well that we should learn from? What should we avoid?

This should feed into concrete issues for Phase 3+ implementation.
notes:
## Design philosophy — making terminals approachable

The biggest opportunity isn't features, it's perception. Terminals are the most powerful interface for AI coding but they look like 1985. People see a black rectangle and feel like they need to be an expert.

**Key principles:**
- **Warm, not cold** — the thermal palette already does this. A terminal that feels alive, not clinical
- **Progressive disclosure** — single pane = clean and simple. Complexity reveals itself as you use it. No chrome overload on first launch
- **Good defaults that delight** — the out-of-box theme should make people go "oh, this is nice." First impression matters more than configurability
- **Clickable everything** — file paths, URLs, errors should have hover states and be interactive. The terminal should feel like a modern app, not a text dump
- **Visual feedback for AI** — this is the differentiator nobody else has. When Claude is running, you should *see* it: thinking spinners, tool call cards, progress indicators, context usage. The terminal becomes a collaboration window, not a black box

**Competitive lessons:**
- Warp: proved approachable terminals get massive adoption, but went too far into "not a terminal" and alienated power users
- Ghostty: proved you can be fast AND pretty with GPU rendering
- iTerm2: feature-rich but overwhelming — settings have 100+ tabs
- WezTerm: great Lua config but zero discoverability for new users

**The AI-native angle:** When an agent is working and you can see tool calls as cards, thinking state as an indicator, context as a progress bar — that transforms "scary black box" into "I can see what's happening." That's what makes therminal different from "another terminal with AI bolted on."
## Design direction (2026-04-05)

**Core insight: therminal is an invisible multiplexer, not a dashboard.** AI data surfaces through MCP to TUIs — the terminal itself nails session management UX. Solve what makes tmux painful despite being powerful:

1. No prefix key tax — direct hotkeys like a native app
2. Right-click context menus for discoverability (learn hotkeys from the menu)
3. Select-to-copy like a browser, not prefix+[ vi-mode
4. Hyprland-style auto-rebalancing layouts, not rigid 50/50 splits
5. Zero-friction pane spawn/kill for AI agent workflows

Reference: tmux popup menu screenshot (grouped actions, right-aligned hotkey hints)
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Config hot-reload only applies title and font — padding, colors, shell, keybindings ignored
description:
## What
TherminalConfig advertises settings for padding, colors, keybindings, shell, env, profiles, and scrollback, but apply_config() only applies window title and font changes. Rendering hardcodes 4.0 padding. PTY spawning ignores configured shell and env. Color overrides are parsed but not applied to the renderer. Valid config changes are silent no-ops.

## Fix needed
Either:
1. Wire all config fields into the hot-reload path (padding, colors, keybindings at minimum)
2. Or explicitly mark unimplemented fields in config as `#[serde(skip)]` with TODO comments so users get parse errors instead of silent no-ops

Padding and color overrides are the highest priority since they're the most commonly customized settings.

## Found by
Codex audit 2026-04-05, finding #7
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
TherminalConfig advertises many settings but apply_config() only applies title and font. Padding is hardcoded to 4.0, color overrides are parsed but unused, shell/env settings are ignored by PTY spawn.

## Task
Wire remaining config fields into the hot-reload and startup paths:

1. **Padding**: Replace hardcoded `4.0` in window.rs/grid_renderer.rs with `config.general.padding`. Apply on hot-reload.
2. **Colors**: Wire `config.colors` overrides into the palette lookup. When background/foreground/cursor/selection are set, use them instead of palette defaults.
3. **Shell**: In spawn_shell(), check config.general.shell — if non-empty, use it instead of default shell.
4. **Env**: Merge config.general.env into the PTY spawn environment.
5. **Scrollback**: Pass config.general.scrollback_lines to Term creation.
6. For any settings that genuinely can't be hot-reloaded (shell, scrollback), apply at PTY spawn time only and log a note that restart is needed.

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-core/src/config.rs` — TherminalConfig
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs` — apply_config(), init_gpu(), hardcoded padding
- `crates/therminal-app/src/grid_renderer.rs` — padding usage
- `crates/therminal-terminal/src/pty.rs` — spawn_shell()

## When Done
`bd close tn-2xm --reason "Config fields wired into hot-reload and startup paths"`
close_reason: Config fields wired into hot-reload and startup paths

title: Session attach snapshots omit scrollback history
description:
## What
Module comment and API contract say attach/detach sends "grid + cursor + scrollback", but Pane::snapshot() only iterates 0..screen_lines() — visible screen only. Scrollback history is lost on reattach.

## Impact
Users reattaching to a daemon session lose all terminal history above the visible screen. This is a meaningful data loss for the core daemon use case (detach/reattach).

## Fix needed
Include scrollback rows in the snapshot. alacritty_terminal's Term provides grid.display_iter() and grid.total_lines() for accessing scrollback. Serialize scrollback rows in the snapshot, or at minimum include a scrollback_lines count so the client knows history is truncated.

## Found by
Codex audit 2026-04-05, finding #6
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Session attach API says it sends "grid + cursor + scrollback" but Pane::snapshot() only iterates visible screen rows. Reattaching clients lose all terminal history.

## Task
Include scrollback in session snapshots:

1. Read how alacritty_terminal's Term/Grid exposes scrollback (grid.total_lines(), grid.display_iter(), etc.)
2. Update Pane::snapshot() to include scrollback rows above the visible screen
3. Update PaneSnapshot struct to include a scrollback field
4. Consider a max scrollback limit in the snapshot to avoid huge payloads (e.g., last 10K lines)
5. Update Session::snapshot() aggregation

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/session.rs` — Pane::snapshot(), PaneSnapshot, Session::snapshot()
- `vendor/alacritty_terminal/src/grid/` — Grid API for scrollback

## When Done
`bd close tn-nm3 --reason "Session snapshots include scrollback history"`
close_reason: Session snapshots include scrollback history

title: Mouse-drag pane separator to resize splits
description:
Split ratio can only be adjusted via Ctrl+Shift++/- (0.05 increments). No mouse-based resize exists. Users expect to drag the separator between panes to resize.

Implement:
- Cursor change to resize cursor when hovering over a separator (2-4px hit zone)
- Click-and-drag to adjust the split ratio continuously
- Double-click separator to reset to 50/50

This is standard split-pane UX in every IDE and terminal multiplexer.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Split ratio can only be adjusted via Ctrl+Shift++/- (0.05 increments). No mouse-based resize exists. Users expect to drag the separator between panes.

## Task
Implement mouse-drag separator resize:

1. **Hit detection**: When mouse moves, check if cursor is within 4px of a pane separator. If so, change cursor to resize cursor (NS or EW depending on split direction).
2. **Drag tracking**: On mouse press within the separator hit zone, start tracking drag. On mouse move, update the split ratio proportionally.
3. **Ratio update**: Call `adjust_ratio` or set the ratio directly on the parent Split node based on mouse position relative to the split area.
4. **Double-click**: Double-click on separator resets to 50/50.
5. **Cursor restore**: When leaving the separator zone or releasing the mouse, restore normal cursor.

The separator positions can be computed from the layout tree — each Split node has two children with known viewport rects, and the gap between them is the separator.

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-app/src/window.rs` — mouse input handling (MouseInput, CursorMoved events)
- `crates/therminal-app/src/pane.rs` — layout tree, viewport rects, ratio adjustment

## When Done
`bd close tn-naz --reason "Mouse-drag separator resize implemented"`
close_reason: Mouse-drag separator resize implemented

- Deep nesting: split 5+ times, then close inner panes and verify tree rebalances correctly

- All-panes-closed: close every pane one by one, verify None returned on last

title: Add defensive handling for maximize/resize crash on WSLg
description:
## What
Clicking maximize on WSLg triggers Wayland event loop failure, broken pipe errors, and SIGSEGV (exit 139). The current render() handles SurfaceError but the crash occurs before wgpu can report it — likely a winit/wgpu/WSLg edge case during rapid resize.

## Why
This is the crash symptom in tn-cz9. While the root cause may be in winit/wgpu/WSLg, we can add defensive measures to prevent the SIGSEGV.

## How
1. Add a panic hook that logs crash context before SIGSEGV
2. Guard surface.configure() calls in resize() with error handling (catch_unwind or validate dimensions)
3. Consider rate-limiting rapid resize events to avoid overwhelming the WSLg compositor
4. Investigate if winit has known WSLg maximize issues and apply any workarounds
close_reason: Added resize debounce, catch_unwind guard, and panic hook for WSLg stability

title: Trust tier enforcement at MCP layer
description: Per-agent trust tiers (Observer/Reader/Writer/Admin) defined in therminal.toml. Filter available MCP tools by tier. Rate-limit destructive ops. Log all invocations.
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Trust tiers (Sandboxed/Supervised/Trusted) are defined in config.rs and parsed from therminal.toml, but nothing enforces them. The MCP server (tn-ar8) exposes session management tools. Trust enforcement should filter which MCP tools are available based on the connecting agent's tier.

## Task
1. **Add trust tier check** to MCP tool dispatch in `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp.rs`:
   - Map each MCP tool to a required tier level: read-only tools (list, capture) → Observer, write tools (send_keys, create) → Writer, destructive tools (destroy) → Admin
   - Before executing a tool, check the agent's tier against the required level
   - Return a permission error for unauthorized tools

2. **Agent identification**: Determine agent identity from the MCP connection. Options:
   - Client-provided agent name in MCP initialize
   - Process tree detection on the connecting process
   - Fallback to `trust.default_tier`

3. **Lookup tier from config**: Match agent name against `trust.agents` map, fall back to `trust.default_tier`

4. **Rate limiting**: Add a simple rate limiter for destructive operations (destroy_session, kill_pane) — max N per minute configurable

5. **Audit logging**: Log all MCP tool invocations with agent name, tool, and result (success/denied)

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/mcp.rs` — MCP server tool dispatch
- `crates/therminal-core/src/config.rs` — TrustConfig, TrustTier, AgentTrust
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/server.rs` — IPC server pattern for reference

## When Done
Run `./scripts/ci.sh` to verify build + tests pass.
`bd close tn-5g7 --reason "Trust tier enforcement at MCP layer with rate limiting and audit logging"`
close_reason: Trust tier enforcement at MCP layer with rate limiting and audit logging

title: Control mode (machine-readable protocol)
description: Text-based machine-readable protocol like tmux -CC. Structured output with begin/end blocks, async notifications. Critical for agent ecosystem (claude-squad, agent-deck depend on tmux scripting).
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
Control mode is a machine-readable protocol (like tmux's `-CC` mode) that lets external tools programmatically drive Therminal. This is critical for the agent ecosystem — tools like claude-squad and agent-deck currently depend on tmux scripting. Therminal's control mode gives them a first-class alternative.

## Task
Implement control mode in `therminal-daemon`:

1. **Protocol format**: Text-based structured output with begin/end blocks:
   ```
   %begin REQUEST_ID
   ... response data (JSON or structured text) ...
   %end REQUEST_ID
   ```
2. **Async notifications**: Unsolicited events prefixed with `%`:
   ```
   %session-changed SESSION_ID
   %pane-output PANE_ID
   %layout-changed
   ```
3. **Commands**: Map session manager operations to text commands:
   - `new-session [-n NAME]` — create session
   - `list-sessions` — list all sessions
   - `send-keys PANE_ID KEYS` — send input to pane
   - `split-pane [-h|-v]` — split focused pane
   - `select-pane PANE_ID` — change focus
   - `capture-pane PANE_ID [-p]` — get pane content
   - `kill-pane PANE_ID` — close pane
4. **Entry point**: `therminal --control-mode` flag on the client binary, or a dedicated `therminalc` control client
5. **Transport**: Uses the same IPC socket as regular clients, with a `mode: control` flag in the handshake

## Key Files
- `crates/therminal-daemon/src/main.rs` — add control mode handler
- `crates/therminal-protocol/src/message.rs` — add control mode message types
- `crates/therminal-app/src/main.rs` — add `--control-mode` CLI flag

## When Done
Close issue: `bd close tn-fvv --reason "Control mode protocol implemented"`
close_reason: Control mode protocol implemented: text-based machine-readable protocol (tmux -CC style) with %begin/%end response blocks, async %notifications, command parser for new-session/list-sessions/send-keys/split-pane/select-pane/capture-pane/kill-pane/list-panes/ping/exit, server-side auto-detection via 'mode: control' handshake, --control-mode CLI flag, and new IPC request/response types for pane operations (SendKeys, CapturePane, SplitPane, KillPane, SelectPane).

title: Output stream analysis (typing cadence, burst detection)
description: Implement Layer 3 detection: distinguish human typing (1-3 chars, 50-200ms, backspaces) from agent output (100-4000+ chars, <1ms delay, zero backspaces, >500 chars/sec). Spinner pattern detection (Braille, cursor control).
notes:
## prepared.prompt

## Context
State inference already has `feed_bytes()` (state_inference.rs:455) that processes PTY output. It strips ANSI codes and pushes lines into a ring buffer. But it has no timing analysis — it cannot distinguish human typing (slow, with backspaces) from agent output (fast bursts, no backspaces). This is Layer 3 detection.

## Task
Add output stream cadence analysis to the state inference engine.

### 1. Add timing collection in feed_bytes
In `crates/therminal-terminal/src/state_inference.rs`, modify `feed_bytes()` to track:
```rust
struct ByteChunkStats {
    timestamp: Instant,
    byte_count: usize,
    has_backspace: bool,  // 0x08 or 0x7F in the chunk
    has_cursor_control: bool,  // CSI sequences for cursor movement
}
```
Keep a small sliding window (last 10-20 chunks) in `AgentStateInference`.

### 2. Implement cadence classifier
Add a method `classify_output_cadence(&self) -> OutputCadence`:
```rust
pub enum OutputCadence {
    Human,      // 1-3 chars per chunk, 50-200ms intervals, backspaces present
    Agent,      // 100-4000+ chars per chunk, <10ms intervals, no backspaces
    Burst,      // Large dump (e.g., cat file, compiler output) — high volume but not agent
    Unknown,    // Not enough data
}
```

Classification logic:
- **Human**: avg chunk_size < 5, avg interval > 30ms, backspace ratio > 0.01
- **Agent**: avg chunk_size > 50, avg interval < 10ms, no backspaces, sustained > 2 seconds
- **Burst**: chunk_size > 1000 but only 1-2 chunks (not sustained)
- **Unknown**: fewer than 3 chunks in window

### 3. Spinner detection
The existing spinner pattern regex is at line 230 (`detect_spinner_processing`). Enhance it:
- Braille spinner: rapid single-char changes in same position (cursor back + new char)
- Progress bar: repeated `\r` with incrementing numbers
- Generic: any output that is cursor-control-heavy with low text content

Add `is_spinner_pattern(&self) -> bool` that checks recent chunks.

### 4. Integrate with state inference
When `classify_output_cadence()` returns `Agent`, the state inference should:
- Set `InferredStatus::Processing` if not already
- Boost confidence in agent detection (combine with Layer 1 OSC and Layer 2 process tree)

### 5. Emit state change notifications
Use the existing `StateChangeNotification` enum. Add a variant or use existing ones:
- When cadence switches from Human to Agent: emit notification
- When sustained agent output detected: emit ToolUse or Processing

### 6. Tests
- Test human typing pattern: feed small chunks with delays (use mock timestamps)
- Test agent burst pattern: feed large chunks rapidly
- Test spinner detection: feed cursor-control-heavy output
- Test transition: human → agent → human
- Test burst vs agent distinction

## Key Files
- crates/therminal-terminal/src/state_inference.rs (main implementation, modify feed_bytes + add classifier)
- crates/therminal-terminal/src/lib.rs (export if needed)

## When Done
Close issue: bd close tn-q37 --reason "Output cadence analysis: human vs agent vs burst classification"
close_reason: Output cadence analysis: human vs agent vs burst classification

title: Update tn-63y7 (multi-daemon) with WT findings — weaken case, strengthen OSC-primary
description:
Windows Terminal's architecture validates that most WSL boundary pain can be solved with "make the shell cooperate" (OSC 9;9, shell-emitted markers) rather than splitting into per-environment daemons.

**Key findings from WT comparison:**
- WT does zero PID namespace crossing — relies entirely on shell-emitted sequences for tab titles and cwd. No equivalent of therminal's `ProcessDetector::with_wsl_distro`.
- WT uses OSC 9;9 (shell does `wslpath -w`) instead of daemon-side path translation — eliminates the UNC mapping problem.
- WT reads distros from registry — no `wsl.exe` spawning overhead.
- WT never attempts cross-filesystem file watching — everything is sequence-driven.

**Impact on tn-63y7:** The remaining pain points that multi-daemon would solve are:
1. ~~UNC file watching~~ → mostly mitigated if OSC 1341 markers become primary (state-file polling becomes fallback-only)
2. ~~PID namespace~~ → already fallback behind OSC 1341 markers
3. ~~Path translation~~ → OSC 9;9 eliminates for cwd, `linux_to_unc()` remains only for harness state files

The multi-daemon approach still has merit for SSH remotes, but the WSL-specific motivation is weakening as marker-primary architecture matures.

**Recommendation:** Deprioritize tn-63y7 for WSL. Focus on OSC 1341 as primary signal + OSC 9;9 for cwd. Revisit multi-daemon only when SSH remote support becomes a priority.

**Reference:** WT does not cross the PID boundary at all ([#5694](https://github.com/microsoft/terminal/issues/5694)), validates therminal's marker-first direction.
close_reason: Decision captured and applied: tn-63y7 closed as won't-do. WT findings validated marker-primary + OSC 9;9 as the right approach over multi-daemon. Concrete work tracked in tn-2r9a (registry distro detection) and tn-kkr8 (OSC 9;9 cwd).

title: Port thermal-desktop TUI as a therminal-native Ratatui dashboard
description:
Port the Ratatui TUI from ~/projects/thermal-desktop (`thc tui`) to work with therminal's daemon/MCP backend instead of thermal-desktop's conductor-mcp.

**What thermal-desktop TUI has:**
- Sessions page with list/preview/chat locked side-panel layout
- Agent observability (status, tool calls, thinking indicators)
- Session management (create, switch, destroy)

**What changes for therminal:**
- Backend: connect to therminal-daemon via IPC (daemon-client crate) or MCP instead of conductor-mcp
- Data model: therminal sessions/panes/workspaces instead of thermal-desktop sessions
- Agent data: pull from `therminal://claude/events` and `terminal.agents.list` instead of conductor events
- Can run inside a therminal pane itself — gets hotspots (file paths, URLs, issue refs become clickable) and pinned pane support for free

**Architecture decision:**
- Could be a standalone binary (`therminal-tui`) that connects to the daemon
- Or a Ratatui app that runs inside a pane via `command = "therminal-tui"` in a profile
- Either way, it reads from the daemon-client or MCP — no special internal wiring needed

**Why this matters:**
With pinned panes + TUI + hotspots, users can compose their own IDE-like chrome: pin a session dashboard, pin an agent timeline, and switch workspaces freely for actual work. The control panels stay put. User-composable workspace UI without therminal having to bake any specific layout in.

**Reference:** ~/projects/thermal-desktop has the existing Ratatui TUI code (Sessions page, list/preview/chat layout). The user particularly likes the side-panel layout pattern.
notes:
## Retro
- What worked: Reading thermal-desktop TUI source gave a clear template for the tabbed page pattern, tab-bar click detection, and side-panel layout. The DaemonClient IPC API mapped cleanly to the TUI's needs — ListSessions/ListPanes/ListAgents/CapturePane covered all three pages.
- What was unclear: The thermal-desktop source heavily depends on ClaudeStatePoller and thermal-core types that don't exist in therminal. Had to design a fresh backend adapter that bridges sync crossterm event loop to async DaemonClient via a background tokio thread with mpsc channels. The Sessions page in thermal-desktop is very complex (~1600 lines across 8 submodules); ported the core layout pattern (side-panel list+detail) but simplified agent display to use daemon IPC wire types directly rather than porting the full ClaudeSessionState model.
close_reason: Implemented therminal-tui binary crate with 3 tabbed pages (Sessions/Panes/Agents), side-panel layout, sync-to-async IPC backend adapter, dark palette, 13 unit tests. Full CI passes (fmt, clippy, build, test).

Optionally also create `scripts/tn.ps1` (`& therminal @args`) for PowerShell-native users who source profiles.

CMD and PS1 wrappers provided as fallback for users who prefer scripts over exe copies.\n- What was unclear: Nothing significant — the task was well-scoped with clear deliverables.
